# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Data-Ink Ratio" as introduced by Edward Tufte. State its ideal value and write its formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Data-Ink Ratio (DIR)** is the proportion of a chart's total ink that is devoted to representing the actual data, as opposed to non-data decoration (gridlines, borders, 3D shadows, redundant labels). It was introduced by **Edward Tufte** in *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information* (1983).
>
> $$\text{DIR} = \frac{I_{\text{data}}}{I_{\text{total}}}$$
>
> The **ideal value is 1.0** (or 100%), meaning all ink on the chart represents data. Practically, a DIR ≥ 0.9 is considered excellent.
>
> *(Awarded: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark ideal value)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any THREE visual variables proposed by Jacques Bertin for graphical data encoding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Jacques Bertin (1967) proposed **seven** visual variables in his semiology of graphics. Any three of the following:
> 1. **Position** — placement on a 2D plane (most accurate).
> 2. **Size** — magnitude of a mark (e.g., bubble area).
> 3. **Shape** — geometric form (circle, square, triangle).
> 4. **Value (Lightness)** — gradient from black to white.
> 5. **Color (Hue)** — red, blue, green, etc.
> 6. **Orientation** — tilt angle of a mark.
> 7. **Texture** — pattern (dots, stripes, hatching).
>
> *(1 mark each, max 3)*

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **Cleveland-McGill perceptual accuracy hierarchy** of visual encodings. With a neat example, justify why a **bar chart** is preferred over a **pie chart** for comparing values across categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dataset of $n = 500$ student marks has $Q_1 = 40$ and $Q_3 = 80$. Using the **Freedman–Diaconis rule**, determine the **optimal bin width** and the **number of bins** for a histogram covering the range [0, 100]. Comment on whether Sturges' rule would have given a different result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q A(a)

**Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy (1984):**
The hierarchy ranks visual encodings by the accuracy with which the human eye can decode quantitative differences. The ranking, from **most to least accurate**, is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier 1 (Most Accurate):} & \quad \text{Position along a common scale} \\
\text{Tier 2:} & \quad \text{Position along identical, non-aligned scales} \\
\text{Tier 3:} & \quad \text{Length, Direction (Angle)} \\
\text{Tier 4:} & \quad \text{Area} \\
\text{Tier 5:} & \quad \text{Volume, Curvature} \\
\text{Tier 6 (Least Accurate):} & \quad \text{Shading, Color saturation}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Bar Chart > Pie Chart:**
- A **bar chart** uses **length along a common baseline** → Tier 3, highly accurate.
- A **pie chart** uses **angle and area** → Tier 4–5, moderately accurate.
- **Example:** Consider sales of Product A = 30, B = 35, C = 45.
  - In a bar chart, the eye can instantly rank B < A < C from the bar heights.
  - In a pie chart, the angular differences (108°, 126°, 162°) are **cognitively harder** to compare.
- Hence, **bar charts are preferred** for precise categorical comparison. Pie charts are acceptable only when showing simple part-to-whole with ≤ 5 slices.

*(Marks: 3 for hierarchy + 2 for example + 2 for comparison justification = 7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q A(b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute IQR:} \\
& \text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 80 - 40 = 40 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute } n^{1/3}: \\
& n^{1/3} = 500^{1/3} \approx 7.937 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Apply Freedman–Diaconis formula:} \\
& h = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{IQR}}{n^{1/3}} = 2 \cdot \frac{40}{7.937} = 2 \cdot 5.040 = 10.08 \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Round to a clean bin width: } h \approx 10. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Number of bins for range [0, 100]:} \\
& k = \frac{100 - 0}{10} = 10 \text{ bins} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Compare with Sturges' rule:} \\
& k_{\text{Sturges}} = 1 + 3.322 \cdot \log_{10}(500) = 1 + 3.322 \times 2.699 = 1 + 8.966 \approx 10 \\
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \text{Both rules coincidentally give } k = 10 \text{ bins in this case,} \\
& \text{but FD rule adapts better to skewed or multimodal data.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Marks: 5 for FD computation + 2 for Sturges comparison = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **5-step Production Pipeline** in Data Visualization. For each step, state one engineering application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup records the following monthly revenue (in ₹ Lakhs): 12, 15, 14, 18, 22, 20, 25, 28, 30, 27, 32, 35. Produce a visualization recommendation. Identify: (i) chart type, (ii) key visual channels, (iii) two chart-junk elements you would remove, and (iv) the trend classification (linear, exponential, etc.) based on a simple mean-based analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q B(a)

| Step | Name | Description | Engineering Application |
|------|------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Data Sanity Check** | Validate, clean, and preprocess raw data | Removing noise from sensor data in an IoT bridge-monitoring system |
| 2 | **Variable Type Classification** | Identify nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio | Classifying soil type (nominal) and moisture % (ratio) in a civil survey |
| 3 | **Visual Channel Mapping** | Assign data attributes to Bertin's variables | Mapping temperature (ratio) to color hue in a thermal map of a CPU |
| 4 | **Chart Geometry Selection** | Pick the appropriate chart type | Using a contour plot for stress distribution in a mechanical bracket |
| 5 | **Aesthetic Layering** | Add labels, colors, annotations | Adding tooltips and legends to a Power BI factory dashboard |

*(1.4 marks per step × 5 = 7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q B(b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Chart Type:} \quad & \textbf{Line chart (with markers)} \\
& \text{— Time series data (12 months) with a single continuous variable (revenue).} \\
\text{(ii) Visual Channels:} \quad & \text{Position (X = month, Y = revenue),} \\
& \text{Line slope (trend direction), Marker shape (data points).} \\
\text{(iii) Chart-Junk to Remove:} \quad & \text{(1) Heavy 3D shadows under the line, (2) Background gridlines in vivid colors,} \\
& \text{(2) redundant data labels on every point (use tooltips instead).} \\
\text{(iv) Trend Analysis:} \quad & \text{Compute mean of first 6 months: } \bar{x}_1 = \frac{12+15+14+18+22+20}{6} = 16.83 \\
& \text{Compute mean of last 6 months: } \bar{x}_2 = \frac{25+28+30+27+32+35}{6} = 29.5 \\
& \text{Growth: } \frac{29.5 - 16.83}{16.83} \times 100\% \approx 75.3\% \text{ in 6 months.} \\
& \text{Compound monthly growth: } r = \left(\frac{35}{12}\right)^{1/11} - 1 \approx 0.1016 = 10.16\% \\
& \text{→ Conclusion: Steady, approximately exponential growth.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Marks: 2 for (i)+(ii) + 1 for (iii) + 4 for (iv) = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing bin width with bin count** — Students often interchange $h$ and $k$ in histogram problems. **Remember**: $h$ is width, $k$ is number of bins.
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 in Sturges' formula** — A frequent 1-mark loss. The formula is $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$, not just $3.322 \log_{10}(n)$.
> 3. **Listing Bertin's variables without examples** — Always pair each variable with a concrete chart example (e.g., "Hue → color of bars in a categorical bar chart").
> 4. **Truncating the Y-axis in bar charts** — Tufte's Lie Factor becomes > 1, leading to graphical dishonesty. Always start bar charts at **zero**.
> 5. **Using pie charts for > 5 categories** — Examiner will deduct marks. Switch to a **stacked bar** instead.
> 6. **Not citing the source dataset range** in histogram problems — Range [0, 100] is required to compute $k$ from $h$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Production** = the act of converting cleaned data → visual artifacts.
- ⭐ **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio**: target = **1.0**; minimize chart-junk.
- ⭐ **Bertin's 7 Visual Variables**: Position, Size, Shape, Value, Color, Orientation, Texture.
- ⭐ **Cleveland-McGill hierarchy** ranks encodings by perceptual accuracy; **Position > Length > Area > Color**.
- ⭐ **Sturges' Rule**: $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$.
- ⭐ **Freedman–Diaconis Rule**: $h = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{IQR}}{n^{1/3}}$.
- ⭐ **Lie Factor** = 1.0 means honest visualization; > 1 = exaggeration, < 1 = understatement.
- ⭐ **WCAG Contrast Ratio** for accessibility ≥ **4.5:1** for text.
- ⭐ **5-step pipeline**: Clean → Type → Map → Geometry → Aesthetic.
- ⭐ **Bar chart > Pie chart** for categorical comparison (perceptual accuracy).
- ⭐ **Histogram** for distribution (1 var); **Scatter** for correlation (2 var); **Line** for time-trend.
- ⭐ **Choropleth maps** require **normalized** data, not raw counts.
- ⭐ **Always start bar charts at Y = 0** to avoid Lie Factor > 1.
- ⭐ **ColorBrewer** is the gold standard for perceptually uniform, colorblind-safe palettes.
- ⭐ **Banking to 45°** (Cleveland) — choose aspect ratio so line segments average ~45°.
- ⭐ **KTU-favorite exam question types**: Sturges rule, DIR definition, Bertin's variables, chart-type selection with justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Search" in Data Handling?

In the context of **Data Handling and Visualization**, **Search** refers to the systematic process of locating a specific data element, record, or pattern within a data collection (such as an array, list, tree, database table, or text corpus). It is one of the most fundamental operations in computer science and data engineering, forming the bedrock of virtually every data retrieval system ranging from simple in-memory lookups to complex search engines like Google.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *Search Algorithm* is a step-by-step procedure used to find a particular target value (called the **key**) within a *data structure*. The operation returns either the position (index) of the target element or a sentinel value indicating failure (e.g., `-1` or `NULL`).

The two classical categories of search are:
- **Unordered (Sequential) Search** – applicable when data has no inherent ordering.
- **Ordered (Binary) Search** – applicable only when data is pre-sorted in a known sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a huge library with **1 million unsorted books** piled in random order and you need to find a specific book titled *"Data Visualization Cookbook."*
>
> - **Linear Search** is like picking up each book one by one from the top of the pile until you find your target. It is *slow* (worst case: 1,000,000 checks) but requires no pre-work.
> - **Binary Search** is like going to a library where books are arranged **alphabetically by title on the shelves**. You walk straight to the middle shelf, see whether your target comes *before or after* it, ignore half the library, and repeat. You find the book in about **20 steps** ($\log_2 1{,}000{,}000 \approx 20$).
> - **Hash-Based Search** is like the library giving every book a unique locker number based on its title. You compute the locker number and walk *directly* to it in **O(1)** time.

This analogy maps directly to the time complexities:
- Linear Search $\Rightarrow$ **O(n)**
- Binary Search $\Rightarrow$ **O(log n)**
- Hash Search $\Rightarrow$ **O(1)** average case

## 1.3 Why Search Matters in Data Handling

In real-world data engineering pipelines:
1. **ETL Operations** (Extract, Transform, Load) require constant lookups to identify duplicates, missing values, and outliers.
2. **Database Management Systems (DBMS)** use indexed search to answer queries in milliseconds.
3. **Search Engines** use inverted indices — a search-specific data structure.
4. **Visualization Dashboards** rely on fast search to filter and slice large datasets dynamically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course treats Search as a *preparatory step* to data visualization. Before any chart, graph, or dashboard is created, the underlying data must be *retrieved efficiently*. Hence, understanding search complexity directly impacts the *performance* of every visualization tool (Tableau, Power BI, Python's Pandas, etc.).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Search Analysis

The following standard metrics are used throughout this module:
- **Time Complexity (T(n))** – Number of operations performed as a function of input size $n$.
- **Space Complexity (S(n))** – Auxiliary memory required by the algorithm.
- **Best Case ($\Omega$)**, **Average Case ($\Theta$)**, **Worst Case ($O$)** – Asymptotic bounds.
- **Comparisons** – The most expensive primitive operation in comparison-based search.
- **n** – The total number of elements in the search space.
- **k** – The position of the target element (1-indexed).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear (Sequential) Search — Operational Breakdown

**Linear Search** scans each element of the data structure one-by-one from the first index to the last until a match is found or the entire collection is exhausted.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Start at the **first index** $i = 0$ (or $i = 1$ in 1-indexed notation).
2. **Compare** the element at index $i$ with the target key.
3. If `data[i] == key`, **return** $i$ (success — element found).
4. Otherwise, **increment** $i \leftarrow i + 1$.
5. If $i \geq n$ without a match, **return** $-1$ (failure).

**Why does this work?** It is a brute-force, exhaustive strategy. It makes no assumptions about the order of data, which is both its strength (works on any dataset) and its weakness (slowest possible approach).

**Best Case:** The target is at the very first position — **1 comparison**.
**Worst Case:** The target is at the last position OR absent — **n comparisons**.
**Average Case:** On average, the target lies at the middle — **$\frac{n+1}{2}$ comparisons**.

## 2.2 Binary Search — Operational Breakdown

**Binary Search** is a *divide-and-conquer* algorithm. It requires the data to be **sorted in ascending or descending order** beforehand.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Let `low = 0`, `high = n - 1`.
2. Compute `mid = floor((low + high) / 2)`.
3. If `data[mid] == key`, return `mid` (success).
4. Else if `key < data[mid]`, set `high = mid - 1` (search left half).
5. Else, set `low = mid + 1` (search right half).
6. Repeat steps 2–5 until `low > high` (failure).

**Why does this work?** Each comparison eliminates **half** of the remaining search space. This is the mathematical heart of logarithmic complexity.

## 2.3 Hash-Based Search — Operational Breakdown

A **hash function** $h(k)$ maps a key $k$ to an index in a fixed-size array (called a *hash table*). Searching becomes a direct lookup: compute $h(k)$, jump to that slot, and read the value.

**Collisions** (two keys mapping to the same slot) are handled by:
- **Chaining:** Each slot holds a linked list of all colliding keys.
- **Open Addressing:** Probe linearly, quadratically, or via double hashing until an empty slot is found.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU Board Exams — Memorize These!**

| Algorithm | Pre-condition | Best Case $T(n)$ | Average Case $\Theta(n)$ | Worst Case $O(n)$ | Space $S(n)$ | Stable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | None (any data) | $O(1)$ | $\frac{n+1}{2}$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |
| Binary Search (Iterative) | Sorted data | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |
| Binary Search (Recursive) | Sorted data | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Yes |
| Jump Search | Sorted data, block size $m$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |
| Interpolation Search | Sorted, uniformly distributed | $O(1)$ | $O(\log \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |
| Hash Search | Hash function $h(k)$ defined | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Depends |
| Exponential Search | Sorted, unbounded list | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes |

**Key Derived Formulas (KTU Exam Favorites):**

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | Recurrence for Binary Search |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | Recurrence for Linear Search |
| $W(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ | Worst-case comparisons for Binary Search |
| $m = \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil$ | Optimal block size for Jump Search |
| $mid = low + \frac{(key - A[low]) \cdot (high - low)}{A[high] - A[low]}$ | Probe position for Interpolation Search |
| $\text{Load Factor } \alpha = \frac{n}{m}$ | Hash table performance metric |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Linear Search:** Embedded systems firmware lookup, small control arrays, sensor data filtering.
- **Binary Search:** Database B-tree indices, version control (git bisect), debugging sorted logs.
- **Hash Search:** DNS resolution, caching (Redis, Memcached), password verification, blockchain lookups.
- **Interpolation Search:** Phone directory lookups (uniform distribution), dictionary word search.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When designing a real-time dashboard that filters millions of records, choosing Binary Search over Linear Search can reduce query latency from seconds to milliseconds. This is exactly the kind of trade-off the KTU examiner loves to test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case Complexity of Binary Search

We solve the recurrence relation that mathematically describes Binary Search.

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1 \quad \text{...(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The "+1" represents the single comparison performed at each level of recursion. The "$T(n/2)$" represents the recursive call on half of the remaining array.

**Apply the Master Theorem** (case $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n^{\log_b a} &= n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^0)$, this falls under **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \Theta(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification by Iteration Method** (unrolling the recurrence):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1 \\
     &= \left[T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1\right] + 1 \\
     &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
     &= \dots \text{after } k \text{ unrollings} \\
     &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k
\end{aligned}
$$

The recursion terminates when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., $2^k = n$, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \log_2 n
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(1) + \log_2 n = 1 + \log_2 n \\
     &= O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Binary Search requires at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ comparisons in the worst case. For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ elements, that is just **20 comparisons**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Number of Comparisons in Linear Search

**Setup:** Assume the target is equally likely to be at any of the $n$ positions, and there is an $(n+1)$-th case where the element is **not present**. All $(n+1)$ cases are equiprobable with probability $\frac{1}{n+1}$.

**Number of comparisons for each case:**

| Case | Target Position | Comparisons Needed |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Element at index 1 | 1 |
| 2 | Element at index 2 | 2 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $n$ | Element at index $n$ | $n$ |
| $n+1$ | Element absent | $n$ |

**Expected (Average) Number of Comparisons $E[X]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} i \\
     &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} i + (n+1)\right] \\
     &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[\frac{n(n+1)}{2} + (n+1)\right] \\
     &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[\frac{n(n+1) + 2(n+1)}{2}\right] \\
     &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}\right] \\
     &= \frac{n+2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Simplifying for large $n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &\approx \frac{n}{2} = \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** On average, Linear Search examines **half the array** before finding (or failing to find) the target.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimal Block Size for Jump Search

In **Jump Search**, the array is divided into blocks of size $m$. We jump ahead by $m$ indices until we find a block where the target *could* lie, then perform a linear scan within that block.

**Total Comparisons:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jumps} + \text{Linear Scan} &= \frac{n}{m} + (m - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

We minimize $f(m) = \frac{n}{m} + (m - 1)$ by taking its derivative and setting it to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dm}\!\left[\frac{n}{m} + m - 1\right] &= -\frac{n}{m^2} + 1 = 0 \\
1 &= \frac{n}{m^2} \\
m^2 &= n \\
m &= \sqrt{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the optimal block size is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m = \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back gives the time complexity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \frac{n}{\sqrt{n}} + (\sqrt{n} - 1) \\
     &= 2\sqrt{n} - 1 = O(\sqrt{n})
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Interpolation Search Probe Position

Interpolation Search improves over Binary Search when data is **uniformly distributed** by predicting the position of the target based on its value.

**Derivation:** We model the sorted array as a linear function $f(i) = A[i]$. The probe position $mid$ is computed such that the ratio of distances equals the ratio of value differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{mid - low}{high - low} &= \frac{key - A[low]}{A[high] - A[low]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $mid$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
mid - low &= \frac{(key - A[low])(high - low)}{A[high] - A[low]} \\
mid &= low + \frac{(key - A[low])(high - low)}{A[high] - A[low]}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives an average case complexity of $O(\log \log n)$ for uniformly distributed data, which is **faster** than Binary Search's $O(\log n)$ for such datasets.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Given the sorted array $A = [3, 8, 14, 19, 25, 31, 40, 52, 67]$, find the target key = 31 using Binary Search. Tabulate each iteration.

**Solution Table:**

| Iteration | low | high | mid | A[mid] | Comparison | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 8 | 4 | 25 | 31 > 25 | low = mid + 1 = 5 |
| 2 | 5 | 8 | 6 | 40 | 31 < 40 | high = mid - 1 = 5 |
| 3 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 31 | 31 == 31 | **Return 5 (Success)** |

**Number of comparisons = 3**. Verification: $\lceil \log_2 9 \rceil = 4$, and we used 3 (within the theoretical bound of $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1 = 4$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Classification of Search Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SEARCH ALGORITHMS] --> B[Comparison-Based]
    A --> C[Hash-Based]
    A --> D[Tree-Based]
    B --> B1[Linear Search]
    B --> B2[Binary Search]
    B --> B3[Jump Search]
    B --> B4[Interpolation Search]
    B --> B5[Exponential Search]
    C --> C1[Direct Hashing]
    C --> C2[Chaining]
    C --> C3[Open Addressing]
    D --> D1[BST Search]
    D --> D2[AVL Search]
    D --> D3[B-Tree Search]
    D --> D4[Red-Black Tree Search]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The root `SEARCH ALGORITHMS` branches into three major families. Comparison-based algorithms work on any iterable data. Hash-based algorithms use a *key-to-index* mapping. Tree-based algorithms exploit hierarchical organization.

## 4.2 Binary Search Flow (Divide-and-Conquer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([START: low=0, high=n-1]) --> C{Is low ≤ high?}
    C -- No --> F([RETURN: -1 Not Found])
    C -- Yes --> M[Compute mid = floor low+high / 2]
    M --> Q{Is A mid equal to key?}
    Q -- Yes --> R([RETURN: mid Found])
    Q -- No --> L{Is key less than A mid?}
    L -- Yes --> H[Set high = mid - 1]
    L -- No --> K[Set low = mid + 1]
    H --> C
    K --> C
```

## 4.3 Linear Search State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Initialize i = 0] --> Q{i less than n?}
    Q -- Yes --> R{A i equals key?}
    R -- Yes --> S([RETURN i])
    R -- No --> T[i = i + 1]
    T --> Q
    Q -- No --> U([RETURN -1])
```

## 4.4 Hash Table Collision Resolution — Chaining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hash Function h key] --> I[Compute Index i]
    I --> J[Bucket i in Hash Table]
    J --> K[Linked List of Keys]
    K --> K1[Node key1 value1]
    K --> K2[Node key2 value2]
    K --> K3[Node key3 value3]
```

## 4.5 Time Complexity Comparison Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[n = 10] --- A2[Linear: 10]
    A1 --- A3[Binary: 4]
    A1 --- A4[Hash: 1]
    B1[n = 1000] --- B2[Linear: 1000]
    B1 --- B3[Binary: 10]
    B1 --- B4[Hash: 1]
    C1[n = 1000000] --- C2[Linear: 1M]
    C1 --- C3[Binary: 20]
    C1 --- C4[Hash: 1]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Insight:** As $n$ grows from $10$ to $1{,}000{,}000$, the number of comparisons in Linear Search scales linearly (10 $\to$ 1,000,000), Binary Search scales logarithmically (4 $\to$ 20), and Hash Search remains nearly constant ($\approx 1$). This is why Big-O notation is the *single most important concept* in search algorithm design.

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Search Engine (Data Visualization Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Query] --> C[Crawler and Indexer]
    C --> D[Document Repository]
    D --> P[Preprocessor]
    P --> T[Tokenizer]
    T --> I[Inverted Index Builder]
    I --> R[Ranker with TF-IDF or BM25]
    R --> S[Search Results Page]
    S --> V[Visualization Layer Charts and Graphs]
    V --> A[Analytics Dashboard]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Model]`
**Differentiate between Linear Search and Binary Search. Mention one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
| Data pre-condition | Works on unsorted data | Requires sorted data |
| Approach | Sequential scan | Divide and conquer |
| Time Complexity (Worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ iterative, $O(\log n)$ recursive |
| Preferred Scenario | Small datasets, unsorted data, single lookup | Large sorted datasets, repeated lookups |

**Preferred Scenario (1 Mark):** Linear Search is preferred for *small or unsorted* datasets (e.g., checking a sensor buffer of 10 values). Binary Search is preferred for *large sorted* datasets (e.g., dictionary word lookup in a million-entry index).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Model]`
**What is a hash collision? Name any two techniques used to resolve collisions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **hash collision** occurs when two distinct keys $k_1 \neq k_2$ are mapped by the hash function $h$ to the same slot index, i.e., $h(k_1) = h(k_2)$. *(2 marks for definition)*

**Two collision resolution techniques: (1 mark)**
1. **Chaining (Open Hashing):** Each slot of the hash table stores a linked list. Colliding keys are appended to the same list.
2. **Open Addressing (Closed Hashing):** On collision, probe the next available slot using a defined strategy — Linear Probing, Quadratic Probing, or Double Hashing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the Binary Search algorithm with a suitable pseudocode. Analyze its worst-case and best-case time complexity using recurrence relations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the sorted array $A = [2, 5, 8, 12, 16, 23, 38, 56, 72, 91]$, search for `key = 23` using Binary Search. Show every iteration in a tabular form. Also compute the total number of comparisons made. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Pseudocode for Iterative Binary Search: (3 Marks)**

```
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], key):
    low ← 0
    high ← n - 1
    WHILE low ≤ high DO
        mid ← floor((low + high) / 2)
        IF A[mid] = key THEN
            RETURN mid          // Success
        ELSE IF key < A[mid] THEN
            high ← mid - 1      // Search left half
        ELSE
            low ← mid + 1       // Search right half
        END IF
    END WHILE
    RETURN -1                  // Failure (not found)
END ALGORITHM
```

**Best-Case Complexity Analysis: (1 Mark)**
The best case occurs when `A[mid] == key` on the very first comparison (i.e., the target is the middle element). So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worst-Case Complexity Analysis: (3 Marks)**
The recurrence relation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the Master Theorem with $a = 1, b = 2, f(n) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n^{\log_2 1} &= n^0 = 1 = f(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is Case 2 of the Master Theorem. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \Theta(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

In the worst case, Binary Search requires $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ comparisons.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Iteration Table:** (5 Marks)

| Step | low | high | mid | A[mid] | Comparison | New low / high |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 9 | 4 | 16 | 23 > 16 | low = 5 |
| 2 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 56 | 23 < 56 | high = 6 |
| 3 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 23 | 23 == 23 | **FOUND at index 5** |

**Total Comparisons Made = 3** *(1 Mark)*
**Verification:** Theoretical worst case $= \lfloor \log_2 10 \rfloor + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$ comparisons. We used 3, which is within the bound. *(1 Mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the Linear Search algorithm with a flowchart and pseudocode. Derive its average-case time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Linear Search, Binary Search, and Hash-based Search in terms of time complexity, space complexity, and pre-conditions. Justify which algorithm you would choose for searching a million customer records in a banking database. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Pseudocode for Linear Search: (2 Marks)**

```
ALGORITHM LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], key):
    FOR i ← 0 TO n - 1 DO
        IF A[i] = key THEN
            RETURN i              // Success
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN -1                    // Failure
END ALGORITHM
```

**Flowchart:** (Already drawn in Section 4.3 — refer to it during the exam) *(2 Marks)*

**Derivation of Average-Case Complexity: (3 Marks)**

We consider $(n+1)$ equally likely cases — $n$ successful cases plus 1 failure case.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} i \\
     &= \frac{1}{n+1} \left[ \frac{n(n+1)}{2} + (n+1) \right] \\
     &= \frac{n+2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For large $n$, $E[X] \approx \frac{n}{2}$, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg}(n) &= \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table: (4 Marks)**

| Parameter | Linear Search | Binary Search | Hash-Based Search |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time (Best) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Time (Average) | $\frac{n+2}{2}$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Time (Worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Space | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ iter. / $O(\log n)$ rec. | $O(n)$ |
| Pre-condition | None | Sorted data | Hash function defined |
| Implementation | Trivial | Moderate | Complex |

**Justification for Banking Database (1 million records): (3 Marks)**

For a banking application searching **1 million customer records** by Account Number:

1. The data is *not naturally sorted* by account number across all DBMS systems, but most modern databases maintain sorted B-Tree indices on primary keys. So Binary Search becomes feasible.
2. Real-time response within **milliseconds** is mandatory.
3. **Recommendation: Hash-Based Search (with a B-Tree fallback).** Reason: Hash search offers $O(1)$ average-case lookup, ideal for transactional systems. Binary Search with $O(\log n) \approx 20$ comparisons is an excellent secondary option. Linear Search is *unacceptable* because $O(10^6)$ comparisons would take seconds, violating real-time SLAs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write "Binary Search is $O(\log_2 n)$" without stating the **pre-condition of sorted data**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In Master Theorem applications, students forget to identify $a, b, f(n)$ explicitly. Always write these down before invoking the theorem.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Students confuse the worst case ($O(n)$) and average case ($n/2$) of Linear Search. The average case is **$n/2$**, not $n$.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** When computing mid in Binary Search, students sometimes write `mid = (low + high) / 2` without the floor function — leading to wrong iterations. Always write `mid = floor((low + high) / 2)`.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In the iteration table for Binary Search, students forget to update `low` or `high` after each comparison, which breaks the recursion. Update **both** bounds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- **Linear Search** → No pre-condition → $T(n) = O(n)$ worst, $\frac{n+2}{2}$ average, $O(1)$ best.
- **Binary Search** → Requires **sorted** data → Recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ → $O(\log n)$ worst.
- **Master Theorem Case 2** applies to Binary Search because $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- **Hash Search** → $O(1)$ average but $O(n)$ worst due to collisions.
- **Jump Search** → Optimal block size $m = \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil$ → Complexity $O(\sqrt{n})$.
- **Interpolation Search** → Probe formula $mid = low + \frac{(key - A[low])(high - low)}{A[high] - A[low]}$ → $O(\log \log n)$ average for uniform data.
- **Exponential Search** → Used for unbounded/infinite sorted lists → $O(\log n)$.
- The phrase **"divide and conquer"** is the KTU buzzword for Binary Search.
- Always write **pre-conditions explicitly** in exam answers — examiners award marks for this.
- Time complexity bounds use **Big-O ($O$)** for worst case, **Big-$\Omega$ ($\Omega$)** for best case, and **Big-$\Theta$ ($\Theta$)** for tight (average) bounds.
- For a **million records**, Binary Search needs only $\approx 20$ comparisons while Linear Search needs up to 1,000,000 — a $50{,}000\times$ speedup.
- In **visualization pipelines**, faster search = faster filter/slice operations on dashboards = better user experience.
- Hash table performance depends on the **load factor** $\alpha = n/m$. When $\alpha \to 1$, collisions increase sharply.
- Always mention **stability** in comparison-based sort/search analysis: Linear and Binary Search are both stable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Query

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Query** in the context of Data Handling and Visualization is a formal, structured request for specific data retrieval, manipulation, or analysis from a dataset or database management system (DBMS). According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for PECMT695, a query is treated as the primary mechanism through which raw data is transformed into actionable, meaningful, and presentable information.

In formal terms, a query $Q$ can be defined as a function:

$$Q: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathcal{R}$$

where $\mathcal{D}$ represents the **source dataset** (structured, semi-structured, or unstructured) and $\mathcal{R}$ represents the **result set** that satisfies the logical, conditional, and projection constraints specified by the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A query is **NOT** merely a search string. It is a declarative statement expressed in a query language (such as SQL, XQuery, SPARQL, or a DataFrame operation in Python) that the underlying engine interprets, optimizes, and executes to return a refined subset or transformation of the source data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a vast **library** (your database/dataset) containing millions of books shelved across thousands of aisles. A **query** is the formal request slip you hand to the librarian. The slip doesn't just say *"find me something interesting"* — it precisely states:

- **WHAT** you want (e.g., "books published after 2020")
- **WHICH attributes** matter (e.g., "title, author, ISBN")
- **FROM WHERE** (e.g., "Science section, Rack 14")
- **IN WHAT ORDER** (e.g., "sorted alphabetically by author")
- **HOW MANY** (e.g., "top 10 results")

The librarian (the **query engine**) then walks through the aisles, applies the filters, and returns exactly what was asked — no more, no less. If the slip is poorly written, you get irrelevant books. If it's precise, you get exactly the right shelf.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Analogy:** In a SCADA system monitoring a power grid, a query like *"SELECT MAX(voltage), MIN(current) FROM sensor_readings WHERE timestamp > '2024-01-01' AND substation_id = 'KTM-07' GROUP BY hour"* is analogous to asking: *"What were the peak voltage dips and minimum current draws at the Kottayam-07 substation in 2024, bucketed by hour?"* This transforms thousands of raw telemetry points into a clean hourly summary.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Data Cardinality:** The number of rows $N$ in a relation/table.
- **Query Latency:** Time $T_q$ (in milliseconds) between query submission and result delivery.
- **Selectivity ($\sigma$):** The fraction of rows returned relative to total rows, formally:

$$\sigma = \frac{\vert R_{out} \vert}{\vert R_{in} \vert}$$

- **Throughput ($\lambda$):** Number of queries processed per second (QPS).

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** Questions on "query optimization" frequently expect you to compute or interpret selectivity, cost, and cardinality. Memorize the symbols above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Query — Structural Decomposition

Every well-formed query, regardless of the language (SQL, relational algebra, MongoDB, etc.), decomposes into **five logical clauses**:

1. **SELECT clause (Projection $\pi$):** Specifies *which columns/attributes* to retrieve.
2. **FROM clause (Cartesian Product $\times$):** Identifies the *source tables/datasets*.
3. **WHERE clause (Selection $\sigma$):** Applies *row-level filtering* using predicates.
4. **GROUP BY clause (Aggregation $\mathcal{G}$):** Partitions rows into buckets for summary functions.
5. **ORDER BY clause (Sorting $\tau$):** Arranges the result set in a specified sequence (ascending/descending).

### 2.2 Relational Algebra — The Mathematical Backbone of Queries

KTU 2024 places strong emphasis on **Relational Algebra (RA)** as the theoretical foundation of any query. Every SQL query can be translated into an equivalent RA expression.

| RA Operation | Symbol | KTU Function | Real-world Utility |
|--------------|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| Selection | $\sigma_{p}(R)$ | Filter rows by predicate $p$ | "Show only Kerala customers" |
| Projection | $\pi_{A,B}(R)$ | Pick specific columns | "Show name and phone only" |
| Cartesian Product | $R \times S$ | Combine all rows of $R$ with all rows of $S$ | Rarely used directly; basis for JOIN |
| Union | $R \cup S$ | Combine rows from two compatible relations | "Merge North and South branch data" |
| Set Difference | $R - S$ | Rows in $R$ but not in $S$ | "Find unsold inventory" |
| Rename | $\rho_{x}(R)$ | Rename relation or attributes | "Alias 'cust_id' as 'id'" |
| Natural Join | $R \bowtie S$ | Equi-join on common attributes | "Match orders with customers" |
| Theta Join | $R \bowtie_{\theta} S$ | Join with arbitrary condition $\theta$ | "Join where price > 100" |
| Division | $R \div S$ | Find rows in $R$ matching all of $S$ | "Students who took ALL required courses" |

### 2.3 Query Execution Lifecycle (The "How")

The DBMS/data engine processes a query through a deterministic pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Parsing:** The query text is tokenized and checked against the language grammar (syntax validation). A **parse tree** is constructed.
- **Step 2 — Translation:** The parse tree is converted into an intermediate **relational algebra expression** (the query plan's skeleton).
- **Step 3 — Optimization:** The query optimizer applies **equivalence rules** (e.g., pushing selections down, join reordering) to minimize the cost function $C(Q)$.
- **Step 4 — Execution Plan Generation:** An optimized tree of physical operators (index scans, hash joins, sort-merge joins) is produced.
- **Step 5 — Evaluation:** The plan is executed against the physical data, producing the result set $\mathcal{R}$.
- **Step 6 — Return:** The result is materialized, sorted if requested, and returned to the caller.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Description | KTU Weightage |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Selectivity | $\sigma = \frac{\vert R_{out} \vert}{\vert R_{in} \vert}$ | Fraction of rows surviving a filter | ★★★★ |
| Query Cost (simplified) | $C(Q) = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot I_i$ | Weighted sum of I/O, CPU, network | ★★★ |
| Cartesian Product Size | $\vert R \times S \vert = \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ | Multiplicative row explosion | ★★★★ |
| Natural Join Size (upper bound) | $\vert R \bowtie S \vert \leq \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$ | Worst case is the cartesian product | ★★★ |
| GROUP BY Cardinality | $\vert \mathcal{G}_{A}(R) \vert \leq \vert R \vert$ | Distinct group count $\leq$ total rows | ★★ |
| Result Size after $\pi$ | $\vert \pi_{A}(R) \vert \leq \vert R \vert$ | Projection never increases rows | ★★★ |
| Set Operation Result | $\vert R \cup S \vert = \vert R \vert + \vert S \vert - \vert R \cap S \vert$ | Inclusion-exclusion for unions | ★★ |
| Query Throughput | $\lambda = \frac{N_{queries}}{T_{total}}$ | Queries per second | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Projection ($\pi$)** with **Selection ($\sigma$)**. Remember: Projection works on **columns** (vertical), Selection works on **rows** (horizontal).

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Queries form the foundation of:
- **Business Intelligence (BI):** Generating dashboards, KPIs, executive summaries.
- **IoT & Sensor Networks:** Real-time telemetry aggregation (e.g., smart grid monitoring).
- **Healthcare Informatics:** Patient record retrieval with privacy filters.
- **Financial Systems:** Fraud detection queries over transaction logs.
- **Scientific Computing:** Filtering massive simulation outputs (CERN, climate models).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From SQL to Relational Algebra (with Full Justification)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Given two relations:
- $R$ = `Employee(emp_id, name, dept_id, salary)`
- $S$ = `Department(dept_id, dept_name, location)`

Translate the following SQL query into relational algebra:

```sql
SELECT E.name, D.dept_name
FROM Employee E, Department D
WHERE E.dept_id = D.dept_id
  AND D.location = 'Kochi'
  AND E.salary > 50000;
```

**Step 1 — Identify the FROM clause (sources).**

The FROM clause lists two relations, $E$ (alias for $R$) and $D$ (alias for $S$). In relational algebra, this corresponds to the **Cartesian Product** of the two:

$$T_1 = R \times S$$

**Step 2 — Apply the WHERE conditions (selection).**

The WHERE clause has three conjunctive predicates joined by AND. By the **selection decomposition rule**:

$$\sigma_{p_1 \land p_2 \land p_3}(T) \equiv \sigma_{p_1}(\sigma_{p_2}(\sigma_{p_3}(T)))$$

We cascade the selections:

$$T_2 = \sigma_{E.dept\_id = D.dept\_id}(T_1)$$

$$T_3 = \sigma_{D.location = 'Kochi'}(T_2)$$

$$T_4 = \sigma_{E.salary > 50000}(T_3)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SELECT clause (projection).**

The SELECT clause lists two attributes: $E.name$ and $D.dept\_name$. We project the result:

$$T_5 = \pi_{E.name,\ D.dept\_name}(T_4)$$

**Step 4 — Combine and simplify using equivalence rules.**

By the **join replacement rule**, the combination $\sigma_{E.dept\_id = D.dept\_id}(R \times S)$ is equivalent to a **natural join** $R \bowtie S$ on the common attribute `dept_id`. The final compact expression is:

$$\boxed{\pi_{name,\ dept\_name}\left(\sigma_{location = 'Kochi' \land salary > 50000}\left(Employee \bowtie_{dept\_id} Department\right)\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the result cardinality.**

If $\vert R \vert = 1000$ employees, $\vert S \vert = 20$ departments, and assuming:
- Selectivity of `location = 'Kochi'` is $\sigma_1 = 0.1$ (2 out of 20 departments)
- Selectivity of `salary > 50000` is $\sigma_2 = 0.3$

Then the expected result size is:

$$\vert T_5 \vert = \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert \cdot \sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2$$

$$\vert T_5 \vert = 1000 \times 20 \times 0.1 \times 0.3$$

$$\boxed{\vert T_5 \vert = 600 \text{ rows}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Query Type Classification (KTU-Exam-Ready)

| Query Type | SQL Construct | RA Symbol | Returns | Use Case Example |
|------------|--------------|-----------|---------|------------------|
| **Projection-only** | `SELECT col1, col2 FROM T` | $\pi_{col1,col2}(T)$ | Subset of columns | "Show all customer emails" |
| **Selection-only** | `SELECT * FROM T WHERE cond` | $\sigma_{cond}(T)$ | Subset of rows | "Find orders above ₹10,000" |
| **Join Query** | `SELECT * FROM T1 JOIN T2 ON ...` | $T_1 \bowtie T_2$ | Combined rows | "Orders with customer details" |
| **Aggregation** | `SELECT dept, AVG(sal) FROM T GROUP BY dept` | $\mathcal{G}_{AVG(sal)}(T)$ | One row per group | "Average salary per department" |
| **Nested Query** | `SELECT * FROM T WHERE id IN (SELECT ...)` | $\sigma$ with $\in$ | Conditional subset | "Students enrolled in B.Tech CS" |
| **Set Operation** | `SELECT ... UNION SELECT ...` | $R \cup S$ | Combined distinct rows | "Past + present employees" |
| **Division** | `SELECT s FROM R WHERE FORALL t IN S, (s,t) IN R` | $R \div S$ | "Universal match" rows | "Suppliers who supply ALL parts" |
| **Window Query** | `SELECT ..., RANK() OVER (...)` | Extended RA | Ranked rows | "Top 3 scorers per class" |

### 3.3 Optimization Derivation: Pushing Selections Down

**Theoretical Justification:** Given a query $\pi_L(\sigma_C(R \times S))$, the naive execution first computes the full cartesian product (expensive), then filters. The **equivalence rule** states:

$$\pi_L(\sigma_C(R \times S)) \equiv \pi_L(\sigma_{C_1}(R) \bowtie \sigma_{C_2}(S))$$

where $C = C_1 \land C_2$ and $C_1$ references only $R$, $C_2$ references only $S$.

**Cost Comparison (numerical example):**

Let $\vert R \vert = 10{,}000$, $\vert S \vert = 5{,}000$, and $\sigma_{C_1} = 0.1$, $\sigma_{C_2} = 0.1$.

- **Naive Plan Cost:** $\vert R \times S \vert = 10{,}000 \times 5{,}000 = 50{,}000{,}000$ intermediate rows.
- **Optimized Plan Cost:** $\vert \sigma_{C_1}(R) \bowtie \sigma_{C_2}(S) \vert = 1{,}000 \times 500 = 500{,}000$ intermediate rows.

**Speedup Factor:**

$$S = \frac{50{,}000{,}000}{500{,}000} = 100\times$$

This is why the KTU 2024 syllabus strongly emphasizes **query optimization heuristics** — pushing selections down is one of the most powerful transformations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Exams:** Whenever you see a query with joins and WHERE conditions, always **first filter, then join**. This is the single most common optimization question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 Query Optimization — Plan Tree Before vs. After

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE OPTIMIZATION"]
        B1[pi L] --> B2[sigma C]
        B2 --> B3[cross product R x S]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER OPTIMIZATION"]
        A1[pi L] --> A4[Natural Join bowtie]
        A2[sigma C1] --> A3[Relation R]
        A3 --> A4
        A5[sigma C2] --> A6[Relation S]
        A6 --> A4

```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The "BEFORE" tree processes the full cartesian product (red — expensive). The "AFTER" tree applies selections on individual relations first, then joins (green — efficient). This visual representation is a high-scoring answer for KTU 14-mark questions on query optimization.
