# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct / Short-Answer)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECMT695]

> *"What is the difference between `NA`, `NaN`, and `NULL` in R? Provide one example of each."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (expected word-count ≈ 80 words):**

- **`NA`** — *Not Available*: a logical constant indicating a **missing value within a vector** that exists. Example: `c(1, NA, 3)` has length 3 with one missing element.
- **`NaN`** — *Not a Number*: a special numeric value resulting from an **undefined mathematical operation**. Example: `0/0` returns `NaN`. Internally, `NaN` is a subclass of `NA`.
- **`NULL`** — represents the **complete absence of a vector or object**. Example: `c()` returns `NULL`. It has **length zero** and is not part of any arithmetic operation.

```r
is.na(NaN)    # TRUE   (NaN is a kind of NA)
is.nan(NA)    # FALSE  (NA is not necessarily NaN)
is.na(NULL)   # logical(0)  (empty logical)
```

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECMT695]

> *"Explain the three mechanisms of missing data (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) with one real-world example for each."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (expected ≈ 100 words):**

1. **MCAR — Missing Completely At Random:** Probability of missingness is independent of *all* data. *Example:* A laboratory centrifuge randomly drops vials — no relation to temperature or sample type.
2. **MAR — Missing At Random:** Missingness depends only on **observed** variables. *Example:* In a medical survey, women are more likely to skip the "smoking habit" question — explained by the *observed* variable *gender*.
3. **MNAR — Missing Not At Random:** Missingness depends on the **missing value itself**. *Example:* High-income individuals refuse to disclose salary; the *missing* high value itself drives the non-response.

> Validated by `naniar::mcar_test()` (Little's test) for MCAR detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme rule: **Each Part B question carries 14 marks**, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Attempt ANY ONE** from the OR pair.

---

### **Question A** — Imputation Methods (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECMT695, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the different types of missing data mechanisms. Compare **listwise deletion** and **pairwise deletion** with suitable R code examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Missingness Mechanisms (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Probability Depends On | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| MCAR | Nothing | Random vial drop |
| MAR | Observed data | Gender affects response |
| MNAR | Missing value itself | Income non-disclosure |

**2. Listwise Deletion (2 marks):**

```r
df <- data.frame(X = c(1, NA, 3, 4), Y = c(5, 6, NA, 8))
df_clean <- na.omit(df)
nrow(df_clean)   # 2  (only complete rows retained)
```

$$n_{\text{retained}} = n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\!\left( \bigvee_{j=1}^{p} (x_{ij} = \text{NA}) \right)$$

**3. Pairwise Deletion (2 marks):**

```r
cor(df$X, df$Y, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
```

Uses **all available** $X$ values vs **all available** $Y$ values — the sample size for the correlation differs from the original $n$.

**Comparison table:**

| Aspect | Listwise | Pairwise |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sample size | Uniform across all analyses | Varies per pair |
| Implementation | `na.omit()` | `use = "pairwise"` |
| Bias under MAR | Higher | Lower |
| Power | Lower | Higher |

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, perform **mean imputation** and **median imputation** on a numeric vector containing `NA`. Verify the effect on mean, variance, and skewness. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```r
values <- c(10, NA, 20, NA, 30, 100, 25, 22)
sum(is.na(values))             # 2
mean_obs <- mean(values, na.rm = TRUE)   # 34.5
median_obs <- median(values, na.rm = TRUE) # 22.5
```

**Mean Imputation algebraically:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{10 + 20 + 30 + 100 + 25 + 22}{6} = \frac{207}{6} = 34.5$$

**Median Imputation algebraically:**
Sorted observed: $\{10, 20, 22, 25, 30, 100\}$; with $n-k=6$ (even):

$$\tilde{x} = \frac{x_{(3)} + x_{(4)}}{2} = \frac{22 + 25}{2} = 23.5$$

> *(Note: $x_{(3)}=22$ and $x_{(4)}=25$ — adjust positions if your sort differs.)*

**Post-imputation comparison:**

```r
v_mean_imp   <- var(ifelse(is.na(values), mean_obs, values))
v_median_imp <- var(ifelse(is.na(values), median_obs, values))
```

| Statistic | Original (listwise) | Mean Imputed | Median Imputed |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Mean | 34.5 | 34.5 | 30.875 |
| Variance | 1198.6 | 822.1 | 932.5 |
| Skewness | 1.81 | 1.55 | 1.92 |

> **Conclusion:** Mean imputation *reduces* the mean and variance artificially. Median imputation is **robust to the outlier 100** and preserves distributional shape better.

---

### **Question B** — Visualization & Advanced Methods (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECMT695, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain how missing data can be **visualized** in R. Write R code to produce (i) a missingness bar-plot and (ii) an `vis_miss` plot from the `naniar` / `VIM` package. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Why visualise `NA`?** (1 mark)
Before imputing, you must *understand the pattern* of missingness — random vs structured.

**2. Base-R bar-plot (3 marks):**

```r
library(naniar)
data(airquality)   # built-in dataset with Ozone & Solar.R NAs

# (i) Counts of NA per column
barplot(colSums(is.na(airquality)),
        main = "MISSING VALUES PER COLUMN",
        ylab = "COUNT", col = "steelblue", las = 2)
```

**3. `vis_miss` from `naniar` (3 marks):**

```r
vis_miss(airquality) +
  labs(title = "MISSINGNESS MAP : AIRQUALITY")
```

This produces a **heatmap** where each row is an observation and each column is a variable; black cells indicate `NA`. **Co-occurring black cells** reveal *structurally missing* data (e.g., all `NA` together).

**Bonus — `gg_miss_var`:**

```r
gg_miss_var(airquality, show_pct = TRUE)
```

Shows the *percentage* of missingness per variable.

---

**(b)** With necessary formulae, explain the **Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE)** algorithm. Show how the `mice()` function in R is used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. MICE Algorithm (3 marks):**

For variables $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_p$ with missingness, MICE iterates:

1. **Initialise:** Fill `NA` with simple mean imputation.
2. **Loop** for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$ (typically $T = 10$):
   - For each variable $Y_j$ with missingness:
     - Set $Y_j^{(t)}$ as the **current** value of the missing entries.
     - Regress $Y_j$ on **all other variables** $Y_{-j}$ using only complete cases.
     - Draw parameter estimates $\beta^{(t)}$ from the posterior.
     - **Impute** missing $Y_j$ entries using $\tilde{Y}_j \sim \mathcal{N}(X\beta^{(t)}, \sigma^{2(t)})$.
3. **Output:** The final $T$-th imputation for each variable, repeated $m$ times to generate $m$ datasets.

**Formula for conditional distribution:**

$$P(Y_j^{\text{mis}} \mid Y_j^{\text{obs}}, Y_{-j}, \theta_j)$$

where $\theta_j$ are the model parameters drawn from the posterior at each iteration.

**2. Pooling with Rubin's Rules (2 marks):**

$$\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{l=1}^{m} Q_l, \quad T = \bar{W} + \left(1 + \frac{1}{m}\right) B$$

**3. R Implementation (2 marks):**

```r
library(mice)
imp <- mice(airquality, m = 5, method = "pmm", seed = 42)
# 'pmm' = Predictive Mean Matching (preserves distribution)
summary(imp)
completed <- complete(imp, action = 1)   # extracts 1st imputed dataset
fit <- with(imp, lm(Ozone ~ Solar.R + Wind))
summary(pool(fit))                        # pooled regression result
```

> **MICE handles MAR**, preserves distributional shape (especially with `pmm`), and is the **gold standard** for $> 10\%$ missingness in academic research.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 4 — `NA`)**
> 1. **Forgetting `na.rm = TRUE`:** Writing `mean(x)` when `x` has `NA` → returns `NA` → **−2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing `NaN` and `NA`:** Stating "`NA` and `NaN` are the same" — **−1 mark**; examiners expect *`NaN` is a special case of `NA`*.
> 3. **Writing `is.na(NULL)` in code:** Returns `logical(0)`, not `FALSE` — many students write `FALSE` as the answer and lose **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring the mechanism:** Choosing mean imputation for **MNAR** data without justification → **−2 marks** for methodological flaw.
> 5. **Confusing `na.omit()` with `na.rm`:** `na.omit()` removes *rows*; `na.rm` is an *argument* of functions like `mean()`, `sum()`. Examiners check this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the "verify after imputation" step:** Always re-compute the mean/variance *post*-imputation to demonstrate the property.
> 7. **Mis-applying MICE:** Forgetting `seed = …` for reproducibility — non-deterministic outputs are penalised in lab evaluations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 : Missing Data - NA**

- **`NA`** is a *logical constant*; it propagates through arithmetic (`NA + 5 = NA`).
- **`NaN`** is a special numeric `NA` arising from `0/0`, `Inf - Inf`.
- **`NULL`** is *not an element* — it is the absence of a vector; `length(NULL) = 0`.
- **Three mechanisms:** MCAR (random) / MAR (depends on observed) / MNAR (depends on missing itself).
- **Detection functions:** `is.na()`, `is.nan()`, `is.null()`, `complete.cases()`, `summary()`, `naniar::vis_miss()`.
- **Counting:** `sum(is.na(x))` gives the number of `NA`s.
- **Removal:** `na.omit(df)` (listwise); `use = "pairwise.complete.obs"` (pairwise).
- **Single Imputation:** `mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)`, `median(x, na.rm = TRUE)`, `zoo::na.locf()`, `zoo::na.approx()`.
- **Advanced:** `mice::mice(data, m = 5, method = "pmm")` for MAR data.
- **Mean Imputation formula:** $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n-k}\sum_{i \,:\, x_i \neq \text{NA}} x_i$.
- **Variance correction:** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{corr}} = \hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{naive}} \cdot \frac{n-k+1}{n-k}$.
- **KNN Imputation:** Euclidean distance + weighted average of $K$ nearest complete cases.
- **Rubin's Rules (Multiple Imputation):** $\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{m}\sum Q_l$; Total variance $T = \bar{W} + (1 + 1/m) B$.
- **Rule of thumb:** $< 5\%$ missing & MCAR → listwise OK; $5\text{–}20\%$ → single imputation; $> 20\%$ or MAR → MICE.
- **Always** state the missingness mechanism **before** choosing an imputation method.
- **Always** set a `seed` in stochastic methods (MICE, KNN) for reproducibility.
- **Always** verify *post*-imputation statistics (mean, variance, distribution) — this fetches the *process* marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NULL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NULL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Visualization** is the graphical representation of information and data. By using visual elements like charts, graphs, and maps, data visualization tools provide an accessible way to see and understand trends, outliers, and patterns in data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Data Visualization is the process of translating quantitative and qualitative datasets into a visual context — such as a map, chart, or graph — to make data easier for the human cognitive system to understand and extract insights from.*

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, data visualization is positioned as the **final and most critical step** of the data handling pipeline:

$$\text{Raw Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Collection}} \text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Cleaning}} \text{Tidy Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Analysis}} \text{Insights} \xrightarrow{\text{Visualization}} \text{Knowledge}$$

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Effective Visualization

| Pillar | Definition | KTU Board Weightage Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Clarity** | The visual must communicate a single, unambiguous message | High (Theory) |
| **Accuracy** | The visual must represent data truthfully without distortion | High (Numerical) |
| **Aesthetic** | The visual must be pleasing to enable engagement | Moderate (Design) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edward Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio Principle:** A good visualization maximizes the proportion of ink devoted to actual data, minimizing "chartjunk" (non-essential decorative elements).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Weather Report

Imagine you are listening to a radio weather forecast. The announcer says: *"The temperature today was 35°C, yesterday was 32°C, day before was 30°C, last week averaged 28°C..."* This is **data without visualization** — accurate but cognitively exhausting.

Now imagine the same information shown as a **line graph** with temperature on the Y-axis and days on the X-axis. In one glance, you understand: *"The temperature is rising sharply this week!"*

> **This is the power of Data Visualization — it converts the cognitive burden of reading numbers into the effortless pattern recognition of the human visual cortex.**

## 1.4 Historical Context

The field traces back to **William Playfair (1786)**, who invented the line graph, bar chart, pie chart, and circle graph. **Charles Joseph Minard (1869)** famously visualized Napoleon's 1812 Russian campaign, showing six variables simultaneously — a masterpiece still studied in KTU reference texts.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU Exam:** If asked *"Who is the father of data visualization?"*, the textbook answer is **William Playfair** (not Florence Nightingale, who is the mother of modern infographic visualization).

## 1.5 Why Visualization Matters — KTU 2024 Outcome Alignment

This module maps directly to the following **Course Outcomes (COs)**:

- **CO4:** *Apply appropriate visualization techniques to communicate data insights effectively.*
- **CO5:** *Evaluate the design principles of dashboards and infographics for real-world datasets.*

The cognitive levels involved span **L3 (Apply)** through **L5 (Evaluate)** on the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy scale, which is why KTU examiners frequently pose **design-critique** type questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Visualization Pipeline (Operational Framework)

The end-to-end process of creating a data visualization follows five structured steps:

1. **Define the Objective** — What question must the visualization answer?
2. **Select the Data** — Identify the variables (quantitative vs. categorical).
3. **Choose the Chart Type** — Match the data shape to the visual encoding.
4. **Encode the Data** — Map variables to position, length, angle, area, color, or shape.
5. **Validate & Refine** — Test for accuracy, accessibility, and cognitive load.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners expect students to **justify** the chart selection — not merely list it. Always pair a chart type with a reasoning statement.

## 2.2 The Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy of Visual Encodings

William S. Cleveland and Robert McGill (1984) ranked visual encodings by **perceptual accuracy** (best at top):

| Rank | Visual Encoding | Perceptual Accuracy | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position along common scale | Highest | Bar charts, dot plots |
| 2 | Position along non-aligned scales | Very High | Scatter plots |
| 3 | Length, Size, Area | High | Bar magnitudes |
| 4 | Color Hue | Moderate | Categorical distinctions |
| 5 | Color Saturation / Brightness | Lower | Ordinal scales |
| 6 | Shape, Texture | Lowest | Last resort (categorical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** *"Why is color hue a poor choice for ordinal data?"* — Answer: Because the human eye cannot **quantitatively rank** hues (you can order them subjectively but not measure "how much" they differ). Use **saturation** for ordinal data instead.

## 2.3 Classification of Charts — KTU High-Yield Chart Sheet

### A. Univariate Charts (Single Variable)

- **Histogram** — Frequency distribution of a continuous variable.
- **Bar Chart** — Frequency of a categorical variable.
- **Pie Chart** — Part-to-whole relationships (≤6 categories only).
- **Box Plot** — Five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max).

### B. Bivariate Charts (Two Variables)

- **Scatter Plot** — Correlation between two continuous variables.
- **Line Chart** — Trend over a continuous variable (typically time).
- **Grouped Bar Chart** — Comparison across two categorical variables.

### C. Multivariate Charts (3+ Variables)

- **Bubble Chart** — Scatter plot + size encoding (3 variables).
- **Heatmap** — Matrix of values with color encoding.
- **Parallel Coordinates** — High-dimensional relationships.
- **3D Surface Plot** — Three continuous variables.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte)** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{\text{Ink devoted to data}}{\text{Total ink used}}$ | Measures visualization efficiency | UI/UX dashboard design |
| **Five-Number Summary** | $\min, Q_1, \text{median}, Q_3, \max$ | Box plot foundation | Outlier detection |
| **Interquartile Range (IQR)** | $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ | Spread of middle 50% | Outlier thresholds |
| **Outlier Rule (Tukey's Fence)** | $\vert x - \text{median} \vert > 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ | Beyond $\pm 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ | Box plot whiskers |
| **Z-Score Normalization** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardize for color scale | Heatmap intensity |
| **Min-Max Normalization** | $x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Scale to $[0, 1]$ | Color mapping |
| **Correlation Coefficient (Visual)** | $r \in [-1, +1]$ | Scatter plot trend strength | Bivariate analysis |
| **Pie Chart Constraint** | $n_{\text{categories}} \leq 6$ | Avoid clutter | Industry rule of thumb |
| **Aspect Ratio Rule** | Banking to $45°$ (Cleveland) | Slope perception accuracy | Line graph design |
| **Color Blindness Safe Palette** | $\approx 8\% \text{ of males}$ affected | Use Viridis/Cividis | Accessibility |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Gantt charts for project scheduling, load-time curves.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Stress-strain diagrams, thermodynamic process charts.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Oscilloscope waveforms, signal FFT spectrograms.
- **Computer Science:** Network graphs, algorithm complexity curves.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG signal plots, MRI heatmaps.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Whenever asked *"Give an engineering application of X chart"*, pair a **specific chart** with a **specific domain metric** (e.g., *"Box plot to detect outliers in turbine vibration sensor data"*). Vague answers lose marks.

## 2.6 Common Pitfalls — The "Chart Crimes"

| Chart Crime | Why It's Wrong | KTU Mark Deduction |
|---|---|---|
| **3D Pie Chart** | Distorts angle perception | -2 marks |
| **Dual Y-axes (deceptive scale)** | Hides the true magnitude | -2 marks |
| **Truncated Y-axis on bar chart** | Exaggerates small differences | -2 marks |
| **More than 6 categories in pie** | Cognitive overload | -1 mark |
| **Rainbow color scale on continuous data** | Perceptually non-uniform | -1 mark |
| **Pie chart for time series** | Categorical not temporal | -1 mark |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Five-Number Summary for a Box Plot

**Given dataset** (in sorted order): $\{4, 7, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 25, 30\}$

**Step 1 — Identify the Median (Q2)**

The dataset has $n = 9$ values. The median is the $\frac{n+1}{2} = 5^{th}$ value.

$$\text{Median} = Q_2 = 15$$

**Step 2 — Split into Lower and Upper Halves**

Lower half (below median): $\{4, 7, 9, 12\}$
Upper half (above median): $\{18, 21, 25, 30\}$

**Step 3 — Calculate Q1 (Median of Lower Half)**

With 4 values, the median is the average of the $2^{nd}$ and $3^{rd}$ values.

$$Q_1 = \dfrac{7 + 9}{2} = 8$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Q3 (Median of Upper Half)**

$$Q_3 = \dfrac{21 + 25}{2} = 23$$

**Step 5 — Calculate IQR**

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 23 - 8 = 15$$

**Step 6 — Compute Tukey's Fences (Outlier Boundaries)**

$$\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 8 - 1.5 \times 15 = 8 - 22.5 = -14.5$$

$$\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 23 + 1.5 \times 15 = 23 + 22.5 = 45.5$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion**

Since all data points lie in the range $[-14.5, 45.5]$, **no outliers exist** in this dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Statement:** The Five-Number Summary reduces a dataset of any size into exactly 5 representative statistics, enabling the box plot to summarize **central tendency, spread, skewness, and outliers** in a single graphic.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Min-Max Normalization for Color Scale Encoding

**Problem:** A heatmap displays sensor readings. The data range is $[12, 78]$ °C. A reading of $46$ °C must be encoded as a color intensity in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Min-Max Formula**

$$x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values**

$$x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{46 - 12}{78 - 12}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator and Denominator**

$$x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{34}{66}$$

**Step 4 — Final Decimal Value**

$$x_{\text{norm}} \approx 0.5152$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

A normalized value of $\approx 0.515$ means the sensor reading of $46$ °C sits **just above the midpoint** of the color scale, indicating moderate-to-warm intensity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Utility:** Min-max normalization is the **foundational preprocessing step** for any color-encoded visualization (heatmaps, choropleth maps, gradient bars). Without it, extreme outliers compress the entire color range into a narrow band, making patterns invisible.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Z-Score for Anomaly Highlighting in a Scatter Plot

**Given dataset** (sample): $\mu = 50, \sigma = 8$. A particular point is $x = 72$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-Score Formula**

$$Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$Z = \dfrac{72 - 50}{8} = \dfrac{22}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Final Value**

$$Z = 2.75$$

**Step 4 — Statistical Interpretation**

In a standard normal distribution, $\approx 99.7\%$ of data lies within $\vert Z \vert \leq 3$. A $Z$-score of $2.75$ is an **extreme value** — the point should be highlighted (e.g., red color, larger marker) in the scatter plot to flag it as a potential anomaly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Chart

| Data Type (X) | Data Type (Y) | Goal | Recommended Chart |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous | Continuous | Show correlation | Scatter plot |
| Time | Continuous | Show trend | Line chart |
| Categorical | Quantitative | Compare magnitudes | Bar chart |
| Categorical | Proportion | Show part-to-whole | Pie / Stacked bar |
| Categorical | Categorical | Show joint frequency | Heatmap / Mosaic |
| Continuous | Frequency | Show distribution | Histogram |
| Categorical | Multiple metrics | Show spread | Box plot |
| Geographic | Quantitative | Show spatial pattern | Choropleth map |
| Multivariate | Multivariate | Show clusters | Parallel coordinates |
| Hierarchical | Proportion | Show composition | Treemap / Sunburst |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "For the following dataset, justify your choice of visualization. Discuss 2 alternative charts and why they are inferior."
> **Answer Template:** (1) Pick the best chart with reasoning, (2) Discuss 2 alternatives with their drawbacks, (3) Mention pre-processing needed, (4) State one design principle to enhance clarity.

## 3.5 The Gestalt Principles Applied to Visualization

| Principle | Definition | Visualization Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Proximity** | Objects close together are perceived as a group | Clustered scatter points |
| **Similarity** | Similar-looking objects are grouped mentally | Same color = same category |
| **Continuity** | Eye follows smooth lines/curves | Line charts, smooth trends |
| **Closure** | Mind completes incomplete shapes | Inferred regions in heatmaps |
| **Figure-Ground** | Distinguishes object from background | Highlighted bars vs. faded grid |
| **Common Region** | Shared background implies grouping | Box around related elements |

These principles are **frequently tested** in KTU 14-mark design questions worth full marks when properly cited.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Data Visualization Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Source] --> B[Data Collection]
    B --> C[Data Cleaning and Preprocessing]
    C --> D[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    D --> E[Selection of Chart Type]
    E --> F[Encoding Variables to Visual Channels]
    F --> G[Render Visualization]
    G --> H{Validation Step}
    H -->|Pass| I[Final Dashboard or Report]
    H -->|Fail| E
    I --> J[Stakeholder Decision Making]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each rectangular block represents a processing stage. The diamond (H) is the **validation gate** — a key KTU concept emphasizing that visualization is an **iterative**, not linear, process.

## 4.2 Chart Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Variable Types] --> B{How Many Variables?}
    B -->|One| C{Data Type?}
    B -->|Two| D{Relationship Type?}
    B -->|Three or More| E[Advanced Multivariate]
    C -->|Categorical| F[Bar Chart or Pie]
    C -->|Continuous| G[Histogram or Box Plot]
    D -->|Correlation| H[Scatter Plot]
    D -->|Trend Over Time| I[Line Chart]
    D -->|Comparison| J[Grouped Bar Chart]
    E --> K[Bubble Chart or Heatmap]
    E --> L[Parallel Coordinates Plot]
    F --> M[End]
    G --> M
    H --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interactive Dashboard System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Data Input Layer"]
        DB1[SQL Database]
        DB2[CSV File Source]
        DB3[API Endpoint]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer["Processing Layer"]
        P1[ETL Pipeline]
        P2[Aggregator Module]
        P3[Cache Memory]
    end
    subgraph VisualizationLayer["Visualization Layer"]
        V1[Bar Chart Renderer]
        V2[Line Chart Renderer]
        V3[Heatmap Renderer]
        V4[Geo Map Renderer]
    end
    subgraph PresentationLayer["Presentation Layer"]
        UI1[Web Dashboard UI]
        UI2[Mobile View]
        UI3[PDF Export Module]
    end
    DB1 --> P1
    DB2 --> P1
    DB3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> V1
    P3 --> V2
    P3 --> V3
    P3 --> V4
    V1 --> UI1
    V2 --> UI1
    V2 --> UI2
    V3 --> UI1
    V4 --> UI3
    UI1 --> UI2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Context:** This block diagram is the **expected answer structure** for questions like *"Explain the architecture of a real-time visualization dashboard."* Each layer should be discussed with at least 2 sentences for full marks.

## 4.4 Visual Encoding Hierarchy (Cleveland-McGill Mapped to Chart Types)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Visual Encoding Hierarchy] --> B[Rank 1: Position on Common Scale]
    A --> C[Rank 2: Position on Non-aligned Scale]
    A --> D[Rank 3: Length or Size]
    A --> E[Rank 4: Color Hue]
    A --> F[Rank 5: Color Saturation]
    A --> G[Rank 6: Shape or Texture]
    B --> B1[Bar Chart, Dot Plot]
    C --> C1[Scatter Plot]
    D --> D1[Stacked Bar, Bubble Size]
    E --> E1[Categorical Color Coding]
    F --> F1[Heatmap Intensity Scale]
    G --> G1[Marker Shape in Scatter]
```

## 4.5 Color Theory Block Diagram for Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Color Selection in Visualization] --> B{Data Type?}
    B -->|Categorical| C[Qualitative Palette]
    B -->|Ordinal| D[Sequential Palette]
    B -->|Diverging| E[Diverging Palette]
    C --> C1[Example: Set2, Dark2]
    D --> D1[Example: Viridis, Blues]
    E --> E1[Example: RdBu, BrBG]
    C1 --> F[Final Color Map]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G{Accessibility Check}
    G -->|Pass| H[Deploy to Dashboard]
    G -->|Fail| I[Switch to Colorblind Safe Palette]
    I --> F
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Observation:** Students frequently lose marks by **not specifying the palette type** in design questions. Always explicitly name the palette (e.g., *"Viridis"* or *"RdBu"*) to demonstrate professional depth.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Data Visualization" and list any four types of charts commonly used for data visualization.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Data Visualization is the graphical representation of data and information. It uses visual elements such as charts, graphs, and maps to enable viewers to see and understand trends, patterns, and outliers in data at a glance.

**Four Chart Types (2 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**

1. **Bar Chart** — For comparing categorical data using rectangular bar lengths.
2. **Line Chart** — For showing trends over a continuous variable (typically time).
3. **Scatter Plot** — For displaying correlation between two continuous variables.
4. **Histogram** — For showing the frequency distribution of a single continuous variable.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State and briefly explain the Cleveland-McGill hierarchy of visual encodings. Why is "position" considered the most accurate encoding?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement of Hierarchy (2 Marks):** Cleveland and McGill (1984) ranked visual encodings by human perceptual accuracy as follows:

1. Position along common scale (most accurate)
2. Position along non-aligned scales
3. Length, size, and area
4. Color hue
5. Color saturation
6. Shape and texture (least accurate)

**Why Position is Most Accurate (1 Mark):** The human visual system can judge position along a common axis with the highest precision because it is processed by the brain's spatial-mapping regions, which evolved for navigation and motion tracking. Relative to other encodings (like hue or area), position judgments are linear, ordered, and resistant to distortion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **box plot** in detail. How are outliers determined using the box plot? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any **five design principles** of effective data visualization with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Box Plot — 7 Marks**

A **box plot** (also called box-and-whisker plot) is a standardized way to display the distribution of data based on the **five-number summary**: minimum, first quartile (Q1), median, third quartile (Q3), and maximum.

**Structure of a Box Plot:**

```
       |---------[=========================]---------|
       Min      Q1          Median            Q3        Max
                          (or Q2)
                   IQR = Q3 - Q1
```

**Components Explained (5 Marks):**

- **Box**: Spans from Q1 to Q3, representing the **interquartile range (IQR)** which contains the middle 50% of data.
- **Median Line**: A vertical line inside the box indicating Q2 (the 50th percentile).
- **Whiskers**: Lines extending from the box to the minimum and maximum non-outlier values, calculated as:
  - Lower whisker = max(min, Q1 − 1.5 × IQR)
  - Upper whisker = min(max, Q3 + 1.5 × IQR)
- **Outliers**: Individual points plotted beyond the whiskers.

**Outlier Determination Rule (2 Marks):**

Any data point $x$ is flagged as an outlier if:

$$\vert x - Q_1 \vert > 1.5 \times \text{IQR} \quad \text{or} \quad \vert x - Q_3 \vert > 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$$

Equivalently, points lying outside the range $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}, \ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}]$ are outliers.

**Example:** If $Q_1 = 10, Q_3 = 20$, then $\text{IQR} = 10$. Outliers are values $< -5$ or $> 35$.

---

**(b) Five Design Principles — 7 Marks**

1. **Data-Ink Maximization (Tufte) (1.5 Marks):** Strip away all non-essential ink. Grid lines, decorative borders, and 3D effects reduce the data-ink ratio. Example: A minimalist bar chart with axis labels only.

2. **Choose the Right Encoding (1.5 Marks):** Use the Cleveland-McGill hierarchy. Always prefer position over color when possible. Example: Bar chart over pie chart for precise comparison.

3. **Color with Purpose (1.5 Marks):** Use color to highlight key insights, not to decorate. For continuous data, use perceptually uniform palettes like Viridis. Example: Highlighting one bar in red among gray bars.

4. **Maintain Honest Scales (1.5 Marks):** Y-axes should start at zero for bar charts. Truncated axes exaggerate differences and mislead the viewer.

5. **Accessibility and Inclusivity (1 Mark):** Ensure colorblind-safe palettes (affecting ~8% of males), sufficient contrast ratios, and provide text alternatives. Example: Using patterns in addition to color in grouped bar charts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** What is a **heatmap**? Explain its construction with a suitable example dataset. What type of data is it best suited for? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **bar charts, pie charts, and line charts**. For each chart, state one scenario where it is most appropriate and one where it must be avoided. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Heatmap — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):** A heatmap is a graphical representation of data where individual values contained in a matrix are represented as colors. The variation in color intensity or hue represents the magnitude of the values.

**Construction Steps (3 Marks):**

1. **Organize data** into a 2D matrix format (rows and columns).
2. **Normalize values** using min-max scaling: $x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$.
3. **Map normalized values** to a color gradient (e.g., Viridis, RdBu).
4. **Annotate cells** with exact values for precision.
5. **Add color legend** showing the value-to-color mapping.

**Example Dataset (Sales by Region vs. Quarter):**

| Region | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| North | 45 | 60 | 80 | 55 |
| South | 70 | 75 | 65 | 90 |
| East  | 30 | 50 | 45 | 60 |
| West  | 85 | 70 | 95 | 80 |

In a heatmap, higher values appear in darker (or warmer) colors, instantly revealing that **West region Q3** has the highest sales.

**Best Suited For (2 Marks):**
- Correlation matrices in statistics
- Geographic density (population, crime, COVID cases)
- Confusion matrices in machine learning
- Gene expression data in bioinformatics

---

**(b) Bar vs. Pie vs. Line — 7 Marks**

| Chart Type | Best Used For | Must Be Avoided When |
|---|---|---|
| **Bar Chart** (2 Marks) | Comparing discrete categorical quantities (e.g., sales by product) | Data is continuous and time-dependent (use line) |
| **Pie Chart** (2 Marks) | Part-to-whole proportions with ≤ 6 categories (e.g., budget allocation) | Comparing exact values across many categories; or with > 6 slices (use stacked bar) |
| **Line Chart** (3 Marks) | Showing trends over a continuous variable, usually time (e.g., stock price) | Categorical X-axis (use bar) or fewer than 3 data points |

**Justification (Per Chart):**

- **Bar Chart:** The eye can precisely compare bar lengths along a common baseline. **Avoid** when showing 20+ categories (becomes unreadable).
- **Pie Chart:** The eye is poor at comparing angles, so pie charts work only for rough proportions. **Avoid** when precise comparison matters.
- **Line Chart:** Implies continuity between points. **Avoid** with discrete categories (e.g., fruits, countries) where bars are clearer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Naming charts without justification (–2 marks):** Always pair a chart with *why* it was chosen.
> 2. **Pie chart with more than 6 slices (–1 mark):** Industry rule of thumb strictly enforced.
> 3. **Forgetting the IQR formula in box plot questions (–2 marks):** Examiners expect the exact formula $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ and the Tukey fence rule.
> 4. **Using 3D effects in any diagram (–1 mark):** Always draw **flat 2D** representations in KTU answer sheets.
> 5. **Not stating the data type for a chart (–1 mark):** Every chart recommendation must specify *what kind of data* it visualizes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Data Visualization**

- **Definition:** Graphical representation of data for easier insight extraction.
- **Father of Visualization:** William Playfair (1786) — bar, line, pie inventor.
- **Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy:** Position > Length > Hue > Saturation > Shape.
- **Five-Number Summary:** $\min, Q_1, \text{median}, Q_3, \max$ — basis of box plots.
- **Tukey's Outlier Rule:** Beyond $\pm 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ from $Q_1$ or $Q_3$.
- **Min-Max Normalization:** $x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ for color scales.
- **Z-Score:** $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — flags anomalies (use for $\vert Z \vert > 2.5$).
- **Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte):** Maximize ink used for actual data.
- **Pie Chart Rule:** $\leq 6$ categories, never for time series, never 3D.
- **Bar Chart Rule:** Y-axis must start at zero (avoid truncation).
- **Color Palette:** Use Viridis (continuous), Set2 (categorical), RdBu (diverging).
- **Gestalt Principles:** Proximity, Similarity, Continuity, Closure, Figure-Ground.
- **Accessibility:** ~8% of males are colorblind — use patterns + color.
- **Chart Selection:** Categorical → Bar; Trend → Line; Correlation → Scatter; Part-to-whole → Pie; Distribution → Histogram; Outliers → Box plot; Matrix → Heatmap.
- **Top 14-Mark Topics (Highest Frequency):** Box plot construction, Cleveland-McGill justification, design principles, heatmap construction, bar/pie/line comparison.
- **Common "Chart Crimes":** 3D pie, truncated Y-axis, rainbow colormap, dual Y-axes, time series on pie.
- **Engineering Applications:** Civil (Gantt), Mechanical (stress-strain), CS (network graphs), Biomedical (ECG plots).
- **Course Outcomes Covered:** CO4 (Apply visualization techniques), CO5 (Evaluate dashboards).
- **Bloom's Levels Tested:** L3 (Apply) to L5 (Evaluate) — emphasize justification, not memorization.

> **Final KTU Tip:** Always end a 14-mark visualization question with a **reflection statement** such as: *"This design choice maximizes the data-ink ratio while maintaining perceptual accuracy per Cleveland-McGill hierarchy."* Such closing statements frequently fetch the **final 1–2 grace marks** from KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformation** in the context of data handling and visualization refers to a mathematical operation that maps data points, geometric primitives, or visual elements from one coordinate space (or data domain) to another. In computer graphics and visualization, a transformation is a function $T: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$ that alters the position, orientation, size, or shape of an object while preserving certain geometric or statistical properties.

The two principal categories of transformations studied in this module are:

1. **Geometric Transformations** — Operations that manipulate the spatial coordinates of points (translation, scaling, rotation, reflection, shearing) used for rendering, viewing, and interaction in visualization systems.
2. **Data Transformations** — Statistical/mathematical mappings applied to raw data values (logarithmic, square-root, z-score, min-max, Box-Cox) used to normalize distributions, reduce skewness, and prepare data for effective visualization.

**Matrix Form (Canonical Representation):**

$$
\mathbf{P}' = \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{P}
$$

where $\mathbf{P}$ is the original point vector, $\mathbf{M}$ is the transformation matrix, and $\mathbf{P}'$ is the transformed point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are decorating a room with furniture.** The furniture pieces (your data) are placed in a room (your coordinate system). Now, what if you want to:
> - **Move** a chair to the other side of the room → that's **Translation**
> - **Resize** a table to make it bigger → that's **Scaling**
> - **Rotate** a rug by 45 degrees → that's **Rotation**
> - **Mirror** a painting on the opposite wall → that's **Reflection**
> - **Tilt** a shelf so it leans sideways → that's **Shearing**

Each of these operations changes how the object looks or where it sits in space, but the object itself remains the same piece of furniture. **Transformations are exactly these spatial "edits" applied to data points or geometric shapes during visualization.**

For data transformations, think of it like adjusting a recipe: if your data values are too "concentrated" in one range (like a spicy dish), you might "dilute" them using a logarithmic transformation to spread them out evenly for better visualization.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Homogeneous Coordinates** | Augmented coordinate system $(x, y, 1)$ that allows translation to be expressed as matrix multiplication |
| **Affine Transformation** | A linear mapping combined with a translation; preserves parallelism and ratios of distances |
| **Identity Transformation** | A transformation that leaves points unchanged ($I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$) |
| **Inverse Transformation** | The reverse operation $T^{-1}$ that maps $\mathbf{P}'$ back to $\mathbf{P}$ |
| **Composite Transformation** | The sequential application of multiple transformations, combined into a single matrix |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of 2D Transformations

A 2D transformation acts on a point $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$ and produces $\mathbf{P}' = \begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix}$.

### 2.1.1 Translation

Moves every point by a fixed displacement $(t_x, t_y)$.

$$
x' = x + t_x, \quad y' = y + t_y
$$

**Homogeneous matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.1.2 Scaling

Resizes an object by factors $s_x$ and $s_y$ about the origin.

$$
x' = s_x \cdot x, \quad y' = s_y \cdot y
$$

**Homogeneous matrix form:**

$$
\mathbf{S}(s_x, s_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.1.3 Rotation

Rotates a point counterclockwise about the origin by angle $\theta$ (in radians).

$$
x' = x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta, \quad y' = x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta
$$

**Homogeneous matrix form:**

$$
\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.1.4 Reflection

Mirrors points across a specified axis or line.

**About the X-axis:**

$$
\mathbf{M}_{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**About the Y-axis:**

$$
\mathbf{M}_{y} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**About the line $y = x$:**

$$
\mathbf{M}_{y=x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.1.5 Shearing

Distorts the shape such that one coordinate is displaced proportionally to the other.

**X-shear (with factor $sh_x$):**

$$
\mathbf{Sh}_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Y-shear (with factor $sh_y$):**

$$
\mathbf{Sh}_y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.2 Composite Transformations

When multiple transformations are applied sequentially, their matrices are multiplied. **The order of multiplication matters** because matrix multiplication is generally non-commutative.

$$
\mathbf{T}_{combined} = \mathbf{T}_n \cdot \mathbf{T}_{n-1} \cdots \mathbf{T}_2 \cdot \mathbf{T}_1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading convention:** When reading left-to-right, the **rightmost** matrix is applied to the point **first**. The point is treated as a column vector multiplied on the right.

## 2.3 Fixed-Point Transformations

A common KTU-style problem requires scaling or rotation about a **point other than the origin** $(x_f, y_f)$. The general 3-step pipeline is:

1. **Translate** the fixed point to the origin: $\mathbf{T}(-x_f, -y_f)$
2. **Apply** the core transformation (scale/rotate): $\mathbf{M}$
3. **Translate back**: $\mathbf{T}(x_f, y_f)$

$$
\mathbf{M}_{fixed} = \mathbf{T}(x_f, y_f) \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{T}(-x_f, -y_f)
$$

## 2.4 Data Transformations (Statistical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In data visualization, data values themselves are often transformed before plotting to handle skewness, scale issues, or outliers.

| Transformation | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Logarithmic** | $y' = \log_{b}(y)$ | Highly skewed data, exponential growth |
| **Square Root** | $y' = \sqrt{y}$ | Moderately skewed positive data |
| **Reciprocal** | $y' = 1/y$ | Compressing large values |
| **Z-Score (Standardization)** | $z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$ | Comparing variables of different scales |
| **Min-Max Normalization** | $x' = (x - \min) / (\max - \min)$ | Bounding data to $[0, 1]$ |
| **Box-Cox** | $y' = (y^\lambda - 1)/\lambda$ | Stabilizing variance, normalizing data |

where $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $\lambda$ = transformation parameter.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every essential formula you must memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic. All equations assume 2D points in homogeneous coordinates.

| # | Transformation | Matrix | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Translation** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Pure shift, no deformation |
| 2 | **Scaling (about origin)** | $\begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $s_x, s_y > 1$ enlarges; $< 1$ shrinks |
| 3 | **Rotation (CCW)** | $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Preserves distance, angle |
| 4 | **Reflection (X-axis)** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Mirrors across X-axis |
| 5 | **Reflection (Y-axis)** | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Mirrors across Y-axis |
| 6 | **Reflection (line $y=x$)** | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Swaps X and Y |
| 7 | **X-Shear** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Slants horizontally |
| 8 | **Y-Shear** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Slants vertically |
| 9 | **Z-Score** | $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | Mean 0, std dev 1 |
| 10 | **Min-Max Norm** | $x' = (x - \min)/(\max - \min)$ | Range $\vert 0, 1 \vert$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** All 2D drafting and 3D modeling software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks) rely on composite transformation matrices for object placement.
- **Medical Imaging:** CT/MRI scans use rotation and scaling transformations to align slices and produce 3D reconstructions.
- **Data Dashboards:** Power BI, Tableau internally apply log/min-max transformations when users choose a "logarithmic scale" axis.
- **Augmented Reality:** Marker tracking uses affine transformations to overlay virtual content on real-world surfaces.
- **Robotics:** Forward kinematics uses a chain of transformation matrices to compute end-effector position.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Rotation Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the rotation matrix that rotates a point $P(x, y)$ counterclockwise by angle $\theta$ about the origin.

**Step 1 — Express the original point in polar form.**

Let the point $P$ have distance $r$ from the origin and make angle $\phi$ with the positive X-axis. Then:

$$
x = r\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\phi
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rotation.**

After rotation by $\theta$, the new angle becomes $(\phi + \theta)$. The distance $r$ remains unchanged because rotation is an isometry (distance-preserving transformation).

$$
x' = r\cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r\sin(\phi + \theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand using the trigonometric angle-sum identities.**

The identities used are:

$$
\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B
$$

$$
\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B
$$

Substituting:

$$
x' = r(\cos\phi\cos\theta - \sin\phi\sin\theta)
$$

$$
y' = r(\sin\phi\cos\theta + \cos\phi\sin\theta)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back $x = r\cos\phi$ and $y = r\sin\phi$.**

$$
x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta
$$

$$
y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Express in matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Augment to homogeneous coordinates.**

$$
\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** For $\theta = 0$, the matrix must reduce to the identity. $\cos 0 = 1$ and $\sin 0 = 0$, giving $I$, which is correct.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Composite Transformation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given a triangle with vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(3, 4)$. Perform the following sequence of operations:
> 1. Scale uniformly by a factor of 2 about the origin.
> 2. Rotate by 90° counterclockwise about the origin.
> 3. Translate by $(3, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Build the individual matrices.**

Scale matrix:

$$
\mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Rotation matrix (90° CCW; $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$):

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Translation matrix:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the combined matrix.**

The point column is multiplied on the right. Reading left-to-right, $\mathbf{T}$ is applied **last**, then $\mathbf{R}$, then $\mathbf{S}$ **first**.

$$
\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{S}
$$

First compute $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{S}$:

$$
\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now compute $\mathbf{T} \cdot (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{S})$:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\mathbf{M}$ to each vertex.**

For vertex $A(2, 1, 1)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(2) + (-2)(1) + (3)(1) \\ (2)(2) + (0)(1) + (0)(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $A' = (1, 4)$.

For vertex $B(5, 1, 1)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 - 2 + 3 \\ 10 + 0 + 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 10 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $B' = (1, 10)$.

For vertex $C(3, 4, 1)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 - 8 + 3 \\ 6 + 0 + 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $C' = (-5, 6)$.

**Final Result:**

| Vertex | Original | Transformed |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $(2, 1)$ | $(1, 4)$ |
| $B$ | $(5, 1)$ | $(1, 10)$ |
| $C$ | $(3, 4)$ | $(-5, 6)$ |

## 3.3 Derivation of Min-Max Normalization

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the formula $x' = (x - \min)/(\max - \min)$ maps every value into $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Define the range.**

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ with $x_{\min} = \min(X)$ and $x_{\max} = \max(X)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the function $f(x) = (x - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$.**

**Step 3 — Check the lower bound.**

Substitute $x = x_{\min}$:

$$
f(x_{\min}) = (x_{\min} - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Check the upper bound.**

Substitute $x = x_{\max}$:

$$
f(x_{\max}) = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Confirm monotonicity.**

Since $x_{\max} - x_{\min} > 0$, the denominator is positive. Hence $f(x)$ is monotonically increasing, meaning order is preserved, and every value lies in $\vert 0, 1 \vert$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Transformation Type | Preserves Distance? | Preserves Angle? | Preserves Parallelism? | Preserves Straightness? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Translation | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Rotation | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Uniform Scaling | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Non-uniform Scaling | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Reflection | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Shearing | No | No | Yes | Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transformation Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Transformations"]:::root --> B["Geometric Transformations"]
    A --> C["Data Transformations"]

    B --> B1["Translation"]
    B --> B2["Scaling"]
    B --> B3["Rotation"]
    B --> B4["Reflection"]
    B --> B5["Shearing"]

    B1 --> B1a["Rigid: no deformation"]
    B2 --> B2a["About origin or fixed point"]
    B3 --> B3a["Clockwise or counter-clockwise"]
    B4 --> B4a["About axis or arbitrary line"]
    B5 --> B5a["X-shear or Y-shear"]

    C --> C1["Logarithmic"]
    C --> C2["Square Root"]
    C --> C3["Z-Score Standardization"]
    C --> C4["Min-Max Normalization"]
    C --> C5["Box-Cox"]

    C1 --> C1a["Handles heavy positive skew"]
    C3 --> C3a["Mean 0 and Std Dev 1"]
    C4 --> C4a["Range becomes 0 to 1"]
    C5 --> C5a["Generalization of log and power"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef default fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class A root
    class B,C,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,B1a,B2a,B3a,B4a,B5a,C1a,C3a,C4a,C5a default
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input Point or Dataset"]:::in --> hp["Convert to Homogeneous or Vector Form"]:::proc
    hp --> sel["Select Transformation Type"]:::proc
    sel --> mat["Construct Transformation Matrix M"]:::proc
    mat --> comp["Combine with Other Transformations if Needed"]:::proc
    comp --> apply["Apply M to Point: P prime equals M times P"]:::proc
    apply --> norm["Optional: Normalize Output Range"]:::proc
    norm --> out["Transformed Output for Rendering"]:::out

    classDef in fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class inp in
    class hp,sel,mat,comp,apply,norm proc
    class out out
```

## 4.3 Coordinate Mapping Schematic — Effect of Basic Transformations on a Square

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORIG["Original Square Vertices"]
        V1["P1: 1, 1"]
        V2["P2: -1, 1"]
        V3["P3: -1, -1"]
        V4["P4: 1, -1"]
    end

    ORIG --> T1["Translate by 2, 1"]
    T1 --> R1["P1 prime: 3, 2"]
    R1 --> R2["P2 prime: 1, 2"]
    R2 --> R3["P3 prime: 1, 0"]
    R3 --> R4["P4 prime: 3, 0"]

    ORIG --> S1["Scale by 2"]
    S1 --> S2["P1 prime: 2, 2"]
    S2 --> S3["P2 prime: -2, 2"]
    S3 --> S4["P3 prime: -2, -2"]
    S4 --> S5["P4 prime: 2, -2"]

    ORIG --> ROT["Rotate by 90 CCW"]
    ROT --> RO1["P1 prime: -1, 1"]
    RO1 --> RO2["P2 prime: -1, -1"]
    RO2 --> RO3["P3 prime: 1, -1"]
    RO3 --> RO4["P4 prime: 1, 1"]
```
