**Syntax:** `=COUNTIF(range, criteria)`
- `range` → The cells to evaluate.
- `criteria` → The condition (e.g., `">50"`, `"Pass"`, `"<>0"`).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

The compressive strength (in MPa) of 8 steel alloy specimens tested in a Materials Engineering lab is recorded below:

$$D = \{410,\; 425,\; 395,\; 430,\; 405,\; 420,\; 415,\; 400\}$$

#### **(a)** Compute the **AVERAGE**, **MEDIAN**, **MIN**, **MAX**, and **Range** of the dataset. State the Excel function syntax for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** Compute the **sample standard deviation (STDEV.S)** and **sample variance (VAR.S)**. Using the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**, comment on the quality consistency of the alloy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Central Tendency and Extremum Functions**

**Step 1: Compute AVERAGE**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{410 + 425 + 395 + 430 + 405 + 420 + 415 + 400}{8} = \frac{3300}{8} = 412.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Excel: `=AVERAGE(A1:A8)` → **412.5**

**Step 2: Compute MEDIAN**

Sorted: $\{395, 400, 405, 410, 415, 420, 425, 430\}$

For $n=8$, median = average of 4th and 5th values:

$$\text{Median} = \frac{410 + 415}{2} = 412.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Excel: `=MEDIAN(A1:A8)` → **412.5**

**Step 3: MIN and MAX**

$$\text{MIN} = 395 \text{ MPa}, \quad \text{MAX} = 430 \text{ MPa}$$

Excel: `=MIN(A1:A8)` → **395**, `=MAX(A1:A8)` → **430**

**Step 4: Range**

$$\text{Range} = 430 - 395 = 35 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**Part (b) — Dispersion and Quality Analysis**

**Step 1: Compute squared deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - 412.5$ | $(x_i - 412.5)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 410 | $-2.5$ | $6.25$ |
| 425 | $+12.5$ | $156.25$ |
| 395 | $-17.5$ | $306.25$ |
| 430 | $+17.5$ | $306.25$ |
| 405 | $-7.5$ | $56.25$ |
| 420 | $+7.5$ | $56.25$ |
| 415 | $+2.5$ | $6.25$ |
| 400 | $-12.5$ | $156.25$ |
| **Sum** | | **$\mathbf{1050.00}$** |

**Step 2: Apply sample standard deviation formula ($n-1 = 7$)**

$$s = \sqrt{\frac{1050}{7}} = \sqrt{150} \approx 12.247 \text{ MPa}$$

Excel: `=STDEV.S(A1:A8)` → **12.247**

**Step 3: Sample Variance**

$$s^2 = \frac{1050}{7} = 150 \text{ (MPa)}^2$$

Excel: `=VAR.S(A1:A8)` → **150**

**Step 4: Coefficient of Variation**

$$CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{12.247}{412.5} \times 100\% \approx 2.97\%$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation**

A CV of **2.97%** indicates **excellent quality consistency**. Per standard metallurgical acceptance (CV $\le$ 5% = "Excellent"), this alloy batch exhibits **highly uniform mechanical properties** and is suitable for **critical load-bearing applications** (e.g., aerospace structural members, bridge cables).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

A Power Engineering department records the daily power output (in MW) of a solar farm for 7 days:

$$D = \{45,\; 52,\; 48,\; 60,\; 55,\; 50,\; 58\}$$

#### **(a)** Compute the **SUM**, **AVERAGE**, and **STDEV.P** of the dataset. State the Excel syntax. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** Using **COUNTIF** and **AVERAGEIF** with the condition *"output greater than 50 MW"*, determine:
  (i) How many days exceeded 50 MW.
  (ii) The mean output on those high-output days.
  (iii) Comment on whether the AVERAGEIF result differs significantly from the overall AVERAGE. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Aggregation and Population Statistics**

**Step 1: SUM**

$$\text{SUM} = 45 + 52 + 48 + 60 + 55 + 50 + 58 = 368 \text{ MW}$$

Excel: `=SUM(A1:A7)` → **368**

**Step 2: AVERAGE**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{368}{7} \approx 52.571 \text{ MW}$$

Excel: `=AVERAGE(A1:A7)` → **52.571**

**Step 3: STDEV.P (population) — using $n$ in denominator**

Squared deviations:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - 52.571$ | $(x_i - 52.571)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 45 | $-7.571$ | $57.32$ |
| 52 | $-0.571$ | $0.326$ |
| 48 | $-4.571$ | $20.90$ |
| 60 | $+7.429$ | $55.19$ |
| 55 | $+2.429$ | $5.90$ |
| 50 | $-2.571$ | $6.61$ |
| 58 | $+5.429$ | $29.47$ |
| **Sum** | | **$\mathbf{175.71}$** |

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{175.71}{7}} = \sqrt{25.10} \approx 5.010 \text{ MW}$$

Excel: `=STDEV.P(A1:A7)` → **5.010**

---

**Part (b) — Conditional Aggregates**

**(i) COUNTIF for output > 50 MW**

Days exceeding 50: $\{52, 60, 55, 58\}$ → count = **4**

Excel: `=COUNTIF(A1:A7, ">50")` → **4**

**(ii) AVERAGEIF for output > 50 MW**

$$\text{AVERAGEIF} = \frac{52 + 60 + 55 + 58}{4} = \frac{225}{4} = 56.25 \text{ MW}$$

Excel: `=AVERAGEIF(A1:A7, ">50", A1:A7)` → **56.25**

**(iii) Comparative Analysis**

$$\text{Overall AVERAGE} = 52.571 \text{ MW}, \quad \text{Conditional AVERAGE} = 56.25 \text{ MW}$$

$$\Delta = 56.25 - 52.571 = 3.679 \text{ MW} \quad (\approx 7\% \text{ higher})$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The high-output days (top quartile) produce **~7% more** power than the average day, indicating **stable, above-mean performance on 4 out of 7 days**. This consistency is favorable for **grid-load forecasting** and **predictive maintenance scheduling**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Summarizing Function Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong denominator in standard deviation** — Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ (or vice versa). KTU board **strictly deducts 1–2 marks** if `STDEV.S` vs `STDEV.P` is misused.
>
> 2. **Median calculation error on even-sized datasets** — Students often pick the wrong "middle" index. The correct approach: for $n=10$, median = average of $x_{(5)}$ and $x_{(6)}$.
>
> 3. **COUNTIF criteria syntax** — Students frequently write `>50` without quotation marks: `=COUNTIF(A1:A10, >50)` is **WRONG**. Correct: `=COUNTIF(A1:A10, ">50")`. **Marks are forfeited for syntax errors.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU expects units (MPa, MW, °C) in numerical answers. A correct value without units can cost **0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Misinterpreting AVERAGEIF arguments** — The correct argument order is `AVERAGEIF(range, criteria, average_range)`. Students often swap `range` and `average_range`.
>
> 6. **Skipping intermediate steps in deviation calculation** — The board examiner wants to see the $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ table explicitly. Showing only the final $s$ value without the deviation table **loses 2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Summarizing Functions**

- ✅ **Summarizing Functions** reduce a dataset of size $n$ into a single representative scalar (or small vector).
- ✅ The **5 families** are: Central Tendency, Dispersion, Extremum, Counting, Conditional Aggregates.
- ✅ **AVERAGE** = $\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ — sensitive to outliers; best for symmetric data.
- ✅ **MEDIAN** = middle sorted value — robust to outliers; best for skewed data.
- ✅ **MODE** = most frequent value — only measure valid for categorical data.
- ✅ **STDEV.S** uses **$n-1$** (Bessel's correction) — for **samples**.
- ✅ **STDEV.P** uses **$n$** — for **entire populations**.
- ✅ **VAR.S / VAR.P** are the **squared** versions of the standard deviations.
- ✅ **MIN, MAX, LARGE, SMALL** are extremum functions; Range = MAX $-$ MIN.
- ✅ **COUNT** counts numbers; **COUNTA** counts non-empty; **COUNTBLANK** counts empty.
- ✅ **COUNTIF, SUMIF, AVERAGEIF, MAXIFS, MINIFS** apply a single condition; the plural versions (`*IFS`) support multiple conditions.
- ✅ **COUNTIF criteria must be in quotes**: `">50"`, `"Pass"`, `"<>0"`.
- ✅ **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** = $\frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ — used as a normalized measure of dispersion.
- ✅ The **5-Number Summary** = {Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max} — feeds directly into **boxplot visualization**.
- ✅ Summarizing functions are the **pre-requisite computational step** before any meaningful visualization (histogram, boxplot, dashboard).
- ✅ **Bessel's correction ($n-1$)** is used because sample variance is an **unbiased estimator** of population variance — the $n$ version systematically underestimates $\sigma^2$.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **always state units** (MPa, MW, °C) and **show intermediate working** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Cleaning** (also called *Data Cleansing* or *Data Scrubbing*) is the foundational process in the Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) pipeline that identifies, corrects, removes, or imputes inconsistencies, errors, missing values, and noisy entries from raw datasets to produce a **high-quality, reliable, and analysis-ready** data matrix.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme module outcomes, data cleaning is the act of transforming a "dirty" raw dataset (containing noise, missing values, outliers, duplicates, and type inconsistencies) into a "clean" dataset that satisfies the *fitness-for-use* criterion for downstream mining, statistical inference, and visualization tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Data Cleaning is the process of detecting and resolving errors, missing values, outliers, duplicates, and inconsistencies in data to improve its quality and ensure accurate analytics.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dirty Data** | Data that is incorrect, incomplete, duplicated, or contains noise |
| **Missing Data** | Absence of a value in a record where one is expected |
| **Noisy Data** | Data containing random error or variance (e.g., measurement noise) |
| **Outlier** | A data point that deviates significantly from the rest of the distribution |
| **Imputation** | Statistical method used to estimate and fill in missing values |
| **Tuple/Record** | A single row in a dataset (one observation) |
| **Attribute/Feature** | A single column in a dataset (one variable) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Messy Wardrobe"

Imagine you inherit a **huge, stuffed wardrobe** full of clothes (this is your *raw dataset*). Before you can actually wear or use anything:
- Some shirts have **stains** (noisy data — measurement errors).
- Some hangers are **empty** — you know the shirt *should* be there, but it isn't (missing data).
- A few shirts are **duplicated** (duplicate records).
- Some labels are **mislabeled** (e.g., "size 32" written on a "size 38" shirt → inconsistent data).
- Some shirts are **wildly different** from the rest (e.g., a winter coat sitting in a summer wardrobe → outliers).

You cannot analyze trends ("What do I wear most often?") until you **clean the wardrobe** — wash the stained ones, fill in the empty hangers with reasonable substitutes, remove duplicates, fix labels, and decide what to do with the coat.

**That act of "cleaning the wardrobe" = Data Cleaning.** Just as you cannot forecast your clothing needs from a messy wardrobe, no machine learning model or visualization can produce reliable insights from dirty data.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Garbage In – Garbage Out" (GIGO) Principle:**
> Industry studies show that data scientists spend **60–80% of their time** on data cleaning and preparation. A clean dataset is the single biggest determinant of a successful analytical model.

### 1.4 Sources of Dirty Data

Dirty data is generated from multiple real-world sources, including:
- **Human errors** during manual data entry (typos, transpositions).
- **System limitations** (sensors with finite precision, signal interference).
- **Integration issues** when merging datasets from heterogeneous sources (schema mismatch, unit mismatch).
- **Transmission errors** during data transfer or storage corruption.
- **Temporal inconsistencies** (timestamps from different time zones without normalization).
- **Business process errors** (defaulted values being misclassified as real values, e.g., `-1` for "unknown age").
- **Intentional concealment** or data masking in privacy-preserving systems.

### 1.5 Importance in the KDD Pipeline

Data cleaning sits immediately after **Data Integration** and before **Data Transformation / Mining** in the KDD pipeline. Its role is to:
1. Improve **data quality dimensions** (accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness).
2. Reduce **bias** introduced by missing or erroneous records.
3. Improve the **convergence** and **accuracy** of downstream machine learning algorithms.
4. Enable **trustworthy visualization** (a clean scatter plot tells a true story; a dirty one misleads).
5. Reduce **storage and compute cost** by removing redundant records.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Tasks in Data Cleaning

Data cleaning is a multi-stage procedure comprising six broad tasks. These are the **high-yield classification points** KTU examiners frequently test:

#### A. Handling Missing Values
Missing data is the most common defect. Three principled strategies exist:

**Strategy 1 — Ignore the Tuple**
- Drop the row entirely.
- **Best for**: Large datasets where the missing percentage per row is small (< 5%).
- **Risk**: Bias if data is *not missing at random* (NMAR).

**Strategy 2 — Manual Imputation**
- Domain expert fills in the value manually.
- **Best for**: Small, critical datasets (e.g., medical records).
- **Drawback**: Time-consuming, not scalable.

**Strategy 3 — Automatic Imputation (Statistical / ML-based)**
- **Mean Imputation**: Replace missing value with the attribute mean.
- **Median Imputation**: Better for skewed distributions (robust to outliers).
- **Mode Imputation**: For categorical attributes.
- **Regression Imputation**: Predict missing value from other correlated attributes.
- **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Imputation**: Use the mean of K most similar records.
- **Multiple Imputation (MI)**: Generate multiple plausible values, run analysis on each, pool results.

#### B. Handling Noisy Data
Noise = random variance in a measured variable. Four principal smoothing techniques exist:

1. **Binning** — Sort values, partition into equal-frequency or equal-width bins, smooth by bin mean, bin median, or bin boundaries.
2. **Regression** — Fit a regression line/curve and replace values with regression predictions.
3. **Clustering** — Detect outliers as points far from any cluster centroid; remove or correct.
4. **Combined Computer-Human Inspection** — Flag suspicious values for domain-expert review.

#### C. Handling Inconsistencies
- **Code inconsistency** (e.g., "Male", "M", "m", "male" all for the same gender) → Standardize via mapping table or replace function.
- **Unit inconsistency** (kg vs. lbs, INR vs. USD) → Unit conversion.
- **Cross-field inconsistency** (e.g., `age = 200`, `DOB = 2010`) → Use a domain-rule engine.
- **Date format inconsistency** (DD/MM/YYYY vs. MM/DD/YYYY) → Standardize using ISO 8601 (`YYYY-MM-DD`).

#### D. Handling Duplicates
- **Exact duplicates**: Identical across all attributes → Drop with `DISTINCT`.
- **Fuzzy duplicates** (near-duplicates): "Rahul Kumar" vs. "Rahul Kumer" → Use approximate string matching (Jaro-Winkler, Levenshtein distance).

#### E. Handling Outliers
- Detect via **Z-score**, **IQR (Interquartile Range)**, **DBSCAN clustering**, or **boxplot visualization**.
- Treat by:
  - **Removal** (if clearly erroneous).
  - **Capping/Winsorization** (replace with 5th/95th percentile).
  - **Transformation** (log, sqrt) to reduce skew.
  - **Separate analysis** (outliers may carry valuable information in fraud detection).

#### F. Data Type Validation & Schema Enforcement
- Enforce that `Age` is an integer in `[0, 120]`.
- Enforce that `Email` matches a regex.
- Enforce that `Date` parses to a valid calendar date.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a Board Exam favorite. Memorize it line-by-line.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Imputation** | $\hat{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Replace missing numeric value |
| 2 | **Median Imputation** | $\hat{x} = \begin{cases} x_{(\frac{n+1}{2})} & n \text{ odd} \\ \tfrac{1}{2}\left(x_{(\frac{n}{2})}+x_{(\frac{n}{2}+1)}\right) & n \text{ even} \end{cases}$ | Robust to outliers, skewed data |
| 3 | **Z-Score (Outlier Detection)** | $Z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ ⇒ outlier |
| 4 | **IQR Method (Outliers)** | $\text{Lower} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$, $\text{Upper} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ | Boxplot-based detection |
| 5 | **Min-Max Normalization** | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)}$ | Scale to $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Z-Score Normalization** | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}$ | Scale to zero mean, unit variance |
| 7 | **Decimal Scaling** | $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$, where $j = \lceil \log_{10}(\max \vert v \vert) \rceil$ | Compact scaling |
| 8 | **Binning — Bin Mean Smoothing** | $\hat{x}_i = \bar{x}_{\text{bin}}$ for all $x_i$ in the bin | Smoothing noisy data |
| 9 | **Binning — Bin Boundary Smoothing** | Replace with nearest boundary value | Preserve extreme values |
| 10 | **Jaccard Similarity (Duplicates)** | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Set-based duplicate detection |
| 11 | **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_k - y_k)^2}$ | KNN imputation, clustering |
| 12 | **Levenshtein Distance** | Min. number of single-character edits to convert string $s$ to $t$ | Fuzzy duplicate detection |

**Where:**
- $\mu$ = mean of attribute $A$
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of attribute $A$
- $Q_1$ = first quartile (25th percentile)
- $Q_3$ = third quartile (75th percentile)
- $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$

### 2.3 Binning in Detail (Most Tested Sub-Topic)

Binning is a *local smoothing* technique that discretizes numeric data. Three flavors:

**1. Equal-Width Binning**
Divide the range $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ into $k$ intervals of width:
$$w = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{k}$$

**2. Equal-Frequency Binning (Quantile Binning)**
Each bin contains approximately $n/k$ data points. Produces uniform-information bins, more useful for skewed data.

**3. Smoothing by Bin Means, Medians, or Boundaries**
- **Bin Mean**: Replace every value in the bin with the bin's mean.
- **Bin Median**: Replace with the bin's median (more robust).
- **Bin Boundaries**: Replace with the nearer of the two boundary values (preserves extreme behavior).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: Binning is foundational for **discretization** in machine learning. Decision tree algorithms (C4.5, CART) implicitly use binning. Image processing pipelines (histogram equalization) use equal-width binning. Financial risk models use equal-frequency binning to bucket credit scores.

### 2.4 Normalization vs. Standardization

Two confusable terms. KTU questions often ask for the difference:

| Aspect | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Standardization |
|---|---|---|
| Range | Scales to $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, 1]$ | No fixed range, mean = 0, std = 1 |
| Sensitivity to outliers | **High** (a single outlier compresses everything) | **Moderate** (less affected, but outliers still shift mean) |
| Best for | Bounded data, neural networks, image pixels | Algorithms assuming Gaussian (logistic regression, PCA, SVM) |
| Formula | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min}{\max - \min}$ | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ |

### 2.5 Data Quality Dimensions (ISO/IEC 25012 Standard)

A clean dataset must satisfy:
1. **Accuracy** — Values are close to true values.
2. **Completeness** — No missing mandatory fields.
3. **Consistency** — No contradictions across records/attributes.
4. **Timeliness** — Data is up-to-date.
5. **Validity** — Values conform to defined rules (range, type, format).
6. **Uniqueness** — No unintended duplicates.
7. **Precision** — Values are stored to a sufficient number of decimal places.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Min-Max Normalization with Full Derivation

**Problem:** Given a dataset attribute $A = \{12, 25, 7, 40, 18, 33\}$, normalize to range $[0, 1]$. Show all steps.

**Step 1 — Identify the min and max.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min(A) &= \min\{12, 25, 7, 40, 18, 33\} = 7 \\
\max(A) &= \max\{12, 25, 7, 40, 18, 33\} = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State the transformation formula.**

$$v' = \frac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply element-wise with explicit computation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{12}  &= \frac{12 - 7}{40 - 7} = \frac{5}{33} \approx 0.1515 \\
v'_{25}  &= \frac{25 - 7}{40 - 7} = \frac{18}{33} \approx 0.5455 \\
v'_{7}   &= \frac{7 - 7}{40 - 7}  = \frac{0}{33} = 0.0000 \\
v'_{40}  &= \frac{40 - 7}{40 - 7} = \frac{33}{33} = 1.0000 \\
v'_{18}  &= \frac{18 - 7}{40 - 7} = \frac{11}{33} \approx 0.3333 \\
v'_{33}  &= \frac{33 - 7}{40 - 7} = \frac{26}{33} \approx 0.7879
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary condition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min(v') &= 0 \;\; \text{(corresponds to original minimum 7)} \\
\max(v') &= 1 \;\; \text{(corresponds to original maximum 40)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalization to arbitrary range $[L, H]$.**

If the target range is $[L, H]$ (e.g., $[-1, 1]$ for symmetric neural activation):

$$v' = L + \frac{(v - \min(A))(H - L)}{\max(A) - \min(A)}$$

**Verification for $[-1, 1]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{12}  &= -1 + \frac{5 \times 2}{33} = -1 + 0.303 = -0.697 \\
v'_{40}  &= -1 + \frac{33 \times 2}{33} = -1 + 2 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Z-Score Outlier Detection

**Problem:** Test scores = $\{78, 82, 85, 80, 79, 81, 84, 76, 95, 80\}$. Find outliers using Z-score threshold $|Z| > 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{78 + 82 + 85 + 80 + 79 + 81 + 84 + 76 + 95 + 80}{10} \\
&= \frac{820}{10} = 82
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance and standard deviation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 \\
&= \frac{(78-82)^2 + (82-82)^2 + (85-82)^2 + (80-82)^2 + (79-82)^2}{10} \\
&\quad + \frac{(81-82)^2 + (84-82)^2 + (76-82)^2 + (95-82)^2 + (80-82)^2}{10} \\
&= \frac{16 + 0 + 9 + 4 + 9 + 1 + 4 + 36 + 169 + 4}{10} \\
&= \frac{252}{10} = 25.2 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{25.2} \approx 5.02
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z-score for the suspect value $x = 95$.**

$$
Z_{95} = \frac{95 - 82}{5.02} = \frac{13}{5.02} \approx 2.59
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since $|Z_{95}| = 2.59 > 2$, the score **95 is an outlier**. The student is either exceptionally gifted or the data is erroneous — flag for review.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Equal-Frequency Binning with Bin Mean Smoothing

**Problem:** Sort the data $\{8, 12, 15, 22, 25, 31, 38, 42, 45, 50\}$ into 3 equal-frequency bins, then smooth by bin mean.

**Step 1 — Determine bin size.**

$$
k = \left\lfloor \frac{n}{m} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{10}{3} \right\rfloor = 3 \;\; \text{(with remainder 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Form equal-frequency bins (≈ 3 or 4 elements each).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1} &= \{8, 12, 15, 22\} \;\; (n_1 = 4) \\
\text{Bin 2} &= \{25, 31, 38\} \;\; (n_2 = 3) \\
\text{Bin 3} &= \{42, 45, 50\} \;\; (n_3 = 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bin means.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{\text{Bin 1}} &= \frac{8 + 12 + 15 + 22}{4} = \frac{57}{4} = 14.25 \\
\bar{x}_{\text{Bin 2}} &= \frac{25 + 31 + 38}{3} = \frac{94}{3} \approx 31.33 \\
\bar{x}_{\text{Bin 3}} &= \frac{42 + 45 + 50}{3} = \frac{137}{3} \approx 45.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Replace each value in the bin with its bin mean (smoothed dataset).**

$$
A_{\text{smoothed}} = \{14.25, 14.25, 14.25, 14.25, 31.33, 31.33, 31.33, 45.67, 45.67, 45.67\}
$$

**Step 5 — Loss of variance check (sanity).**

Original variance is high; smoothed variance collapses to inter-bin variance. This is *intentional* — binning discards intra-bin noise. Validate downstream that this loss of granularity is acceptable for the use case.

### 3.4 Worked Example — IQR-Based Outlier Detection

**Problem:** The same dataset $\{8, 12, 15, 22, 25, 31, 38, 42, 45, 50\}$. Find outliers using the 1.5 × IQR rule.

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_1$ and $Q_3$.**

For $n = 10$ sorted values:
- Lower half: $\{8, 12, 15, 22, 25\}$ → Median = $15$ ⇒ $Q_1 = 15$
- Upper half: $\{31, 38, 42, 45, 50\}$ → Median = $42$ ⇒ $Q_3 = 42$

**Step 2 — Compute IQR.**

$$
IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 42 - 15 = 27
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fences.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Fence} &= Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR = 15 - 1.5 \times 27 = 15 - 40.5 = -25.5 \\
\text{Upper Fence} &= Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR = 42 + 1.5 \times 27 = 42 + 40.5 = 82.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since all values lie in $[-25.5, 82.5]$, **no outliers** are detected. (Z-score flagged 95 as an outlier in §3.2, illustrating that the two methods can disagree on the same data — IQR is more robust for small samples.)

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Choosing a Technique

| Problem Detected | Recommended Cleaning Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Single missing numeric value, MCAR, normal dist. | Mean Imputation | Unbiased, simple |
| Missing value, skewed distribution | Median Imputation | Robust to skew |
| Missing value, correlated with other features | Regression / KNN Imputation | Preserves inter-feature relationships |
| Categorical missing value | Mode Imputation | Most frequent category |
| Heavy noise in small dataset | Binning (equal-frequency, mean smoothing) | Local smoothing |
| Heavy noise with continuous trend | Regression smoothing | Captures the trend |
| Suspicious data clumps | Clustering (DBSCAN, K-Means) | Identifies clusters + outliers |
| High-stakes data (medical, financial) | Manual expert review | Domain knowledge |
| Bounded data, need $[0, 1]$ | Min-Max Normalization | Preserves distribution shape |
| Unbounded data, need zero mean, unit variance | Z-Score Standardization | Centered, scaled |
| Outliers in normally distributed data | Z-Score method | Theoretical under Gaussian |
| Outliers in skewed or non-Gaussian data | IQR / boxplot | Non-parametric, robust |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Cleaning Pipeline — Process Flow

This is the canonical KDD cleaning pipeline. Examiners frequently ask to draw or describe it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Ingest] --> B[Data Profiling and Audit]
    B --> C{Defect Detected?}
    C -- No --> Z[Clean Dataset]
    C -- Yes --> D[Missing Value Treatment]
    D --> E[Noise Smoothing]
    E --> F[Outlier Detection and Handling]
    F --> G[Duplicate Removal]
    G --> H[Inconsistency Resolution]
    H --> I[Type and Schema Validation]
    I --> J[Transformation: Normalize / Discretize]
    J --> K{Quality Check Passed?}
    K -- No --> B
    K -- Yes --> Z
```

**Node interpretations:**
- **Data Profiling and Audit**: Compute summary stats, missing %, unique counts, type checks.
- **Transformation**: Includes normalization, aggregation, concept hierarchy generation.
- **Quality Check**: Re-run audits to confirm defect rate has dropped below the threshold.

### 4.2 Defect Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Dirty Data Defects] --> R1[Missing Values]
    R --> R2[Noise and Outliers]
    R --> R3[Duplicates]
    R --> R4[Inconsistencies]
    R --> R5[Schema and Type Errors]
    R1 --> R1a[MCAR]
    R1 --> R1b[MAR]
    R1 --> R1c[NMAR]
    R2 --> R2a[Random Noise]
    R2 --> R2b[Global Outliers]
    R2 --> R2c[Local Outliers]
    R3 --> R3a[Exact Duplicates]
    R3 --> R3b[Fuzzy Duplicates]
    R4 --> R4a[Code Standardization]
    R4 --> R4b[Unit Conversion]
    R4 --> R4c[Cross-Field Rule]
    R5 --> R5a[Data Type Mismatch]
    R5 --> R5b[Range Constraint Violation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **MCAR** = Missing Completely At Random, **MAR** = Missing At Random, **NMAR** = Not Missing At Random. KTU examiners love to ask students to distinguish these and recommend an imputation strategy for each.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Outlier Detection Subsystem

When the topic requires a deep architectural view of one component, here is the outlier detection subsystem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src[Input Attribute Vector X] --> s1[Step 1: Compute Central Tendency]
    s1 --> s2[Step 2: Compute Dispersion]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Choose Detection Method]
    s3 --> m1[Z-Score: Threshold at 3]
    s3 --> m2[IQR: Fences at 1.5 x IQR]
    s3 --> m3[DBSCAN: Density Cluster]
    m1 --> f1[Flag Outliers]
    m2 --> f1
    m3 --> f1
    f1 --> dec{Action}
    dec --> d1[Remove if Erroneous]
    dec --> d2[Winsorize at 5th/95th Percentile]
    dec --> d3[Log Transform if Skewed]
    dec --> d4[Keep and Analyze Separately]
    d1 --> out[Cleaned Output Vector]
    d2 --> out
    d3 --> out
    d4 --> out
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Hierarchy: Data Cleaning in the KDD Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[KDD Pipeline] --> P1[1. Data Selection]
    P1 --> P2[2. Data Preprocessing]
    P2 --> P2a[2a. Data Cleaning]
    P2a --> P2a1[Missing Values]
    P2a --> P2a2[Noisy Data]
    P2a --> P2a3[Inconsistencies]
    P2 --> P2b[2b. Data Integration]
    P2b --> P2c[2c. Data Transformation]
    P2c --> P2c1[Normalization]
    P2c --> P2c2[Discretization]
    P2c --> P2c3[Concept Hierarchy Generation]
    P2c --> P3[3. Data Mining]
    P3 --> P4[4. Pattern Evaluation]
    P4 --> P5[5. Knowledge Presentation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define data cleaning. List any four major tasks involved in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

> **Data Cleaning** is the process of detecting, correcting, or removing corrupt, incomplete, duplicated, or inconsistent records from a dataset to improve its quality for analytical use.
>
> Four major tasks are:
> 1. **Handling missing values** (imputation or removal).
> 2. **Smoothing noisy data** (binning, regression, clustering).
> 3. **Resolving inconsistencies** (standardizing codes, units, formats).
> 4. **Detecting and removing duplicates** (exact and fuzzy).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tasks — write tasks as a list, not a paragraph.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Min-Max Normalization and Z-Score Normalization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Normalization |
|---|---|---|
| Range | Scales to $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, 1]$ | No fixed range |
| Mean & SD | Mean changes, SD changes | Mean = 0, SD = 1 |
| Formula | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min}{\max - \min}$ | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Outlier sensitivity | High (outliers compress the range) | Moderate |
| Best for | Neural networks, bounded data | PCA, SVM, linear regression |

*(2 marks for the table-style comparison, 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **different types of missing data** (MCAR, MAR, NMAR) with a suitable example for each. Which imputation technique is most appropriate for each? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain **binning techniques** (equal-width, equal-frequency) and the three smoothing methods (bin mean, bin median, bin boundary). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Three types of missing data mechanisms:**
>
> **1. MCAR (Missing Completely At Random)** [1 mark]
> The probability of a value being missing is independent of both observed and unobserved data.
> *Example*: A survey response sheet gets accidentally torn; the missing answers are unrelated to the respondent's age, gender, or income.
> *Imputation*: Mean, median, or listwise deletion are unbiased. **Mean imputation is acceptable** [1 mark].
>
> **2. MAR (Missing At Random)** [1 mark]
> The missingness can be explained by *observed* variables, not the missing one itself.
> *Example*: In a salary survey, men are less likely to disclose their salary than women. Since "gender" is observed, we can model the missingness.
> *Imputation*: **Regression imputation** or **multiple imputation** using observed variables (e.g., gender, education) as predictors [1 mark].
>
> **3. NMAR (Not Missing At Random)** [1 mark]
> The missingness depends on the *unobserved* value itself. This is the most challenging case.
> *Example*: High-income individuals systematically refuse to disclose their income.
> *Imputation*: Requires **domain modeling, sensitivity analysis, or pattern-mixture models** [1 mark]. Simple mean imputation would severely underestimate the average income.
>
> **Summary Table (for 1 mark presentation):**
> | Type | Missingness Depends On | Recommended Method |
> |---|---|---|
> | MCAR | Nothing | Mean/Median imputation, deletion |
> | MAR | Observed variables | Regression, multiple imputation |
> | NMAR | Unobserved value | Domain modeling, sensitivity analysis |

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Binning** is a local data smoothing technique that partitions a sorted numeric attribute into contiguous intervals (bins) and replaces each value with a representative value of its bin. [1 mark]
>
> **1. Equal-Width Binning** [1.5 marks]
> Divides the range $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ into $k$ equal-width intervals of width $w = (x_{\max} - x_{\min})/k$.
> *Example*: Data $\{4, 8, 15, 22, 28, 35\}$ with $k = 3$:
> $w = (35 - 4)/3 \approx 10.33$, giving bins $[4, 14.33), [14.33, 24.66), [24.66, 35.33)$.
>
> **2. Equal-Frequency Binning** [1.5 marks]
> Each bin contains approximately $n/k$ data points.
> *Example*: Same data with $k = 3$ ⇒ bins $\{4, 8, 15\}$, $\{22, 28\}$, $\{35\}$ (each has ≈ 2 elements).
>
> **3. Smoothing Methods** [3 marks total]
> After binning, each value is replaced by:
> - **Bin Mean** (1 mark): $\bar{x}_{\text{bin}} = \frac{1}{n_{\text{bin}}} \sum_{i \in \text{bin}} x_i$. Example: bin $\{4, 8, 15\}$ has mean $= 9$.
> - **Bin Median** (1 mark): Median of the bin. Example: bin $\{4, 8, 15\}$ has median $= 8$.
> - **Bin Boundary** (1 mark): Replace with the nearer of the two boundary values. Example: in bin $\{4, 8, 15\}$ (boundaries 4 and 15), 8 stays as 8 (nearer to 4, but distance to 15 is 7, to 4 is 4, so 8 → 4); 15 stays as 15.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a clear example, explain the **IQR method and Z-score method** for outlier detection. Compare their effectiveness. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A dataset contains the attribute $A = \{10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34, 100\}$.
- (i) Apply **Min-Max normalization** to bring the values to range $[0, 1]$.
- (ii) Identify any **outliers** using the IQR method.
- (iii) Discuss what would happen to the normalized values if the value 100 is treated as an outlier and removed before normalization. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Outliers** are data points whose values lie far outside the expected range of the rest of the data. [0.5 mark]
>
> **Z-Score Method** [2 marks]
> The Z-score of a data point $x_i$ measures how many standard deviations it lies from the mean:
> $$Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$
> Conventionally, $|Z_i| > 3$ is treated as an outlier. Z-score assumes the data is approximately normally distributed and is sensitive to the very outliers it tries to detect (an outlier inflates $\sigma$, reducing its own Z-score).
>
> **IQR Method** [2 marks]
> Based on the spread of the middle 50% of the data:
> $$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$$
> Outliers lie outside $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR, \; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$.
> The IQR method is non-parametric, makes no distribution assumption, and is robust to outliers themselves.
>
> **Comparison** [2.5 marks]
> | Aspect | Z-Score | IQR |
> |---|---|---|
> | Distribution assumption | Yes (Gaussian) | No |
> | Robustness to outliers | Low (mean & std affected) | High |
> | Sample size | Needs large $n$ for normality | Works for any $n$ |
> | Threshold | 2 or 3 (problem-dependent) | 1.5 × IQR (standard) |
> | Use case | Normally distributed data | Skewed, non-Gaussian data |
>
> **Practical recommendation:** IQR is the safer default in real-world dirty data; Z-score is preferred when normality can be verified.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Given:** $A = \{10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34, 100\}$, $n = 8$.
>
> **(i) Min-Max Normalization to $[0, 1]$** [2 marks]
> $\min(A) = 10$, $\max(A) = 100$.
> $$v' = \frac{v - 10}{100 - 10} = \frac{v - 10}{90}$$
> Computed values:
> - $v'_{10}  = 0.000$
> - $v'_{14}  = 4/90 \approx 0.044$
> - $v'_{18}  = 8/90 \approx 0.089$
> - $v'_{22}  = 12/90 \approx 0.133$
> - $v'_{26}  = 16/90 \approx 0.178$
> - $v'_{30}  = 20/90 \approx 0.222$
> - $v'_{34}  = 24/90 \approx 0.267$
> - $v'_{100} = 90/90 = 1.000$
>
> **(ii) IQR Outlier Detection** [2 marks]
> Sorted data is already in order. $n = 8$, so:
> - Lower half (4 values): $\{10, 14, 18, 22\}$ ⇒ $Q_1 = (14 + 18)/2 = 16$.
> - Upper half (4 values): $\{26, 30, 34, 100\}$ ⇒ $Q_3 = (30 + 34)/2 = 32$.
> - $IQR = 32 - 16 = 16$.
> - Lower Fence $= 16 - 1.5(16) = 16 - 24 = -8$.
> - Upper Fence $= 32 + 1.5(16) = 32 + 24 = 56$.
> - Value 100 lies above the upper fence of 56 ⇒ **100 is an outlier**.
>
> **(iii) Re-normalization after removing the outlier** [3 marks]
> New dataset: $A' = \{10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34\}$.
> $\min(A') = 10$, $\max(A') = 34$.
> $$v'' = \frac{v - 10}{34 - 10} = \frac{v - 10}{24}$$
> The normalized values now spread more evenly across $[0, 1]$:
> - $v''_{10} = 0.000$, $v''_{14} = 0.167$, $v''_{18} = 0.333$, $v''_{22} = 0.500$, $v''_{26} = 0.667$, $v''_{30} = 0.833$, $v''_{34} = 1.000$.
>
> **Discussion:** Without removing the outlier, the legitimate data $\{10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34\}$ all compress into the narrow range $[0, 0.267]$ — a 4× loss of resolution. The outlier dominates the range, destroying discriminative power. After removal, the data is uniformly spread across $[0, 1]$. **Conclusion:** Outlier removal (or capping/winsorization) should precede Min-Max normalization. This illustrates the well-known sensitivity of Min-Max scaling to extreme values.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MCAR with MAR** — Do not write "missing at random" to mean "missing completely at random". They are distinct. (–1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Writing the normalization formula without specifying the range** — If the question says "normalize to $[0, 1]$" and you write the general formula, you *must* also state that the target range is $[0, 1]$. (–1 mark.)
> 3. **Forgetting to verify boundary values** — After Min-Max, always check that the min maps to 0 and the max maps to 1. Omitting this verification is a common step-skipping penalty. (–0.5 to –1 mark.)
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of Z-score method** — Z-score assumes near-Gaussian distribution. Writing the formula without this caveat costs a mark.
> 5. **Mixing IQR's 1.5 multiplier with the 3.0 multiplier from Z-score** — These are different thresholds for different methods. Use 1.5 × IQR, not 3 × IQR.
> 6. **Omitting numerical verification in the final step** — When asked to normalize, the examiner expects all eight transformed values written out explicitly. Showing only the formula = ~30% marks only.
> 7. **Treating "Cleaning" as the same as "Preprocessing"** — Cleaning is a subset of preprocessing. Transformation (normalization, aggregation) is *preprocessing*, not *cleaning* per se. (–1 mark on definitional questions.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Data Cleaning (KTU 2024 Module 4):**
>
> - [ ] **Definition**: Data cleaning = detecting + correcting/removing/imputing errors, missing values, noise, duplicates, and inconsistencies in a dataset.
> - [ ] **Six major tasks**: Missing values, noise, outliers, duplicates, inconsistencies, schema/type validation.
> - [ ] **Missing-data mechanisms**: MCAR, MAR, NMAR — each requires a different imputation strategy.
> - [ ] **Imputation methods** (in increasing complexity): Ignore, mean, median, mode, regression, KNN, multiple imputation, expert-driven.
> - [ ] **Noisy data** smoothing: Binning (mean, median, boundary), regression, clustering, combined inspection.
> - [ ] **Binning types**: Equal-width (uniform interval size), equal-frequency (uniform count per bin).
> - [ ] **Outlier detection**: Z-score (parametric, Gaussian), IQR (non-parametric, robust), clustering-based (DBSCAN).
> - [ ] **Normalizations**: Min-Max (to $[0,1]$ or $[-1,1]$), Z-score (zero mean, unit variance), decimal scaling.
> - [ ] **Duplicates**: Exact (use `DISTINCT`) vs. fuzzy (Levenshtein, Jaro-Winkler distances).
> - [ ] **Inconsistency sources**: Code (gender labels), unit (kg/lb), date format, cross-field rules.
> - [ ] **Data quality dimensions** (ISO 25012): Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness, precision.
> - [ ] **Pipeline position**: Cleaning sits *after* data integration and *before* transformation/mining in KDD.
> - [ ] **Key insight**: Min-Max is sensitive to outliers; Z-score is moderately sensitive; IQR is robust. Choose accordingly.
> - [ ] **Practical rule**: "Garbage In, Garbage Out" — 60–80% of a data scientist's time is spent on cleaning. The pipeline iterates (clean → audit → re-clean) until quality thresholds are met.
> - [ ] **Examiner hot-button**: The board frequently tests the *difference between cleaning, preprocessing, and integration* — keep them distinct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finding and Removing Duplicates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finding and Removing Duplicates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Data Handling and Visualization**, a **duplicate record** is defined as an observation (row) or attribute (column value) that appears more than once in a dataset, where "appearing more than once" implies complete or partial identity with another record based on a chosen subset of identifying keys. The process of **finding duplicates** (also termed **duplicate detection**, **record linkage**, or **entity resolution**) involves identifying these redundant entries, while **removing duplicates** (also called **deduplication** or **deduping**) refers to the systematic elimination of redundant records to enforce **data integrity**, ensure **uniqueness constraints**, and produce a clean, reliable dataset ready for downstream **exploratory data analysis (EDA)** and machine learning pipelines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Library Card Catalog**
> Imagine you walk into a library and find **three identical copies** of the same book, *Data Science from Scratch* by Joel Grus, all catalogued under the same accession number. The librarian has two options:
> 1. **Find duplicates:** Scan the entire catalog and flag every entry where *Title + Author + ISBN* matches another entry.
> 2. **Remove duplicates:** Physically strike out two of the three records so only **one canonical (master) record** survives.
>
> The "canonical record" rule is crucial — we never destroy *all* copies; we keep one authoritative version and delete the rest.

This library metaphor maps directly to the data world: every **row** is a book record, and the **ISBN/Title+Author** is the **key** we use to detect redundancy.

### 1.3 Categories of Duplicates (Taxonomy)

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Exact Duplicates** | All column values match exactly across rows | Row 5 and Row 12 are character-by-character identical |
| **Partial Duplicates** | Subset of columns match; other columns differ | Same `CustomerID` but different `Address` (data entry variation) |
| **Fuzzy / Near Duplicates** | Values are similar but not identical (typos, formatting) | "Kerala" vs "Kerela" vs "kerala" |
| **Logical Duplicates** | Different identifiers but represent the same real-world entity | `EMP001` and `EMP-001` (same employee) |

### 1.4 Why Duplicates Are a Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight — The 4 Pillars of Damage Caused by Duplicates**
> 1. **Statistical Bias** — Inflated counts and means (e.g., a customer's purchase recorded twice skews average revenue).
> 2. **Machine Learning Poisoning** — Models overfit to repeated patterns, reducing generalization.
> 3. **Storage Waste** — Bloated databases increase query latency and I/O cost.
> 4. **Reporting Errors** — Dashboards show incorrect KPIs, leading to bad business decisions.

### 1.5 Uniqueness and Primary Key Foundation

A **Primary Key (PK)** is a column (or combination of columns) whose values **uniquely** identify each row in a relational table. Duplicate detection is fundamentally the act of verifying that the chosen key column(s) satisfy this **uniqueness constraint**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Composite Key:** A primary key composed of **two or more columns** is called a *composite key*. For example, in a `Student_Course` enrollment table, `(StudentID, CourseID)` together uniquely identify each enrollment row, though neither alone is unique.

---

### Standard Metric Used Throughout This Module

- **Jaccard Similarity Coefficient:** $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$, range **[0, 1]**, where **1 = identical** and **0 = completely disjoint**.
- **Threshold (τ):** A user-defined cutoff (commonly **0.85** or **0.90**) above which two records are declared duplicates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Duplicate Detection Pipeline — Five Logical Stages

A robust duplicate detection workflow follows a **structured, sequential pipeline**. Each stage builds upon the previous one:

1. **Data Profiling Stage** — Compute descriptive statistics, value counts, and cardinality to understand the dataset's shape.
2. **Key Selection Stage** — Identify which column(s) act as the *de facto* identifier (e.g., `ID`, `Email`, composite `(FirstName, LastName, DOB)`).
3. **Detection Stage** — Apply an algorithm to flag duplicates (exact match, hash-based, or fuzzy matching).
4. **Review & Canonicalization Stage** — Human-in-the-loop verification of flagged pairs; select the *master record*.
5. **Removal / Merge Stage** — Execute deduplication (`keep='first'`, `keep='last'`, `keep=False`) or perform **record merging** (merge fields from duplicates into a single golden record).

### 2.2 Algorithmic Approaches — Theory Behind Each Method

#### (a) Set-Based Exact Detection
**Why it works:** A mathematical **set** contains no duplicate elements. By converting a column to a set, duplicates collapse automatically.

**How it works:**
- Convert the key column into a Python `set()`.
- Compare the set length to the column length.
- If `len(set) < len(column)`, duplicates exist.

**Complexity:** $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ space.

#### (b) Sort-Based Detection
**Why it works:** After sorting, identical keys become **adjacent** in memory.

**How it works:**
- Sort the dataframe by the key column.
- Scan linearly; whenever `row[i] == row[i-1]`, flag it.

**Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ time (due to sort), $O(1)$ extra space.

#### (c) Hash-Based Detection (HashMap / Dictionary)
**Why it works:** Hash tables offer **O(1) average-case lookup**. Inserting into a dictionary and detecting repeated keys is blazingly fast.

**How it works:**
- Iterate through rows.
- Maintain a `hash_map: key → list_of_row_indices`.
- If a key already exists in the map, those rows are duplicates.

**Complexity:** $O(n)$ average, $O(n)$ worst-case (collisions), $O(n)$ space.

#### (d) Fuzzy Matching (Similarity-Based)
**Why it works:** Real-world data has typos. Exact matching fails on "Jon" vs "John". Fuzzy matching computes a **similarity score** between two strings.

**How it works:**
- For every pair $(r_i, r_j)$, compute $sim(r_i, r_j)$.
- If $sim > \tau$, declare duplicate.

**Naïve complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — impractical for large data. Optimized via **blocking** (group by a coarse key first).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Canonicalization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before deduplication, raw data must be **canonicalized** — normalized to a standard form. This includes:
> - **Case normalization:** "KERALA" → "kerala"
> - **Whitespace trimming:** "  John " → "John"
> - **Punctuation stripping:** "St." → "Street"
> - **Date format unification:** "01/02/2024" → "2024-02-01"
>
> Without canonicalization, "Kerala" and "kerala" appear as different values and escape exact-match deduplication.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Method | Purpose | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exact Duplicate Count | $D = n - \vert U \vert$ where $n$ = total rows, $\vert U \vert$ = unique values | Quantify redundancy | $O(n)$ |
| 2 | Duplicate Percentage | $P_D = \dfrac{D}{n} \times 100\%$ | Quality KPI | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | Jaccard Similarity | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Fuzzy match (sets) | $O(\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ |
| 4 | Levenshtein Distance | $\text{lev}(a,b) = $ min edits (insert/delete/substitute) | Fuzzy match (strings) | $O(\vert a \vert \cdot \vert b \vert)$ |
| 5 | Normalized Levenshtein | $N_{lev} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{lev}(a,b)}{\max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert)}$ | Bounded similarity [0,1] | same as above |
| 6 | Cosine Similarity (TF-IDF) | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{B} \Vert}$ | Text fuzzy match | $O(V)$ where $V$ = vocab |
| 7 | Hash-Based Detection | `dict[key].append(index)` | Fast exact dedup | $O(n)$ avg |
| 8 | Blocking Key Size | $B = n / k$ (k blocks) | Reduces pairwise cost | $O(n^2/k)$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & FinTech:** Detecting duplicate KYC records to prevent fraudulent account creation.
- **E-Commerce:** Merging product listings from multiple sellers (Amazon's product catalog dedup).
- **Healthcare:** Ensuring one patient has one master Electronic Health Record (EHR) — critical for safety.
- **CRM Systems:** Consolidating customer records from web, mobile, and in-store touchpoints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Example Dataset Construction

Let us define a representative dataset of **6 rows × 4 columns** representing customer records:

| Row | CustomerID | Name | Email | City |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | C001 | Anil Kumar | anil@gmail.com | Kochi |
| 1 | C002 | Anil kumar | anil@gmail.com | kochi |
| 2 | C003 | Priya Menon | priya@yahoo.com | Trivandrum |
| 3 | C001 | Anil Kumar | anil@gmail.com | Kochi |
| 4 | C004 | Rahul Raj | rahul@outlook.com | Calicut |
| 5 | C005 | Priya M. | priya@yahoo.com | Trivandrum |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step — Finding Duplicates Using pandas

**Step 1: Import libraries and load data**

```python
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'CustomerID': ['C001', 'C002', 'C003', 'C001', 'C004', 'C005'],
    'Name':       ['Anil Kumar', 'Anil kumar', 'Priya Menon',
                   'Anil Kumar', 'Rahul Raj', 'Priya M.'],
    'Email':      ['anil@gmail.com', 'anil@gmail.com', 'priya@yahoo.com',
                   'anil@gmail.com', 'rahul@outlook.com', 'priya@yahoo.com'],
    'City':       ['Kochi', 'kochi', 'Trivandrum',
                   'Kochi', 'Calicut', 'Trivandrum']
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)
```

**Output:**

```
  CustomerID         Name             Email        City
0       C001   Anil Kumar   anil@gmail.com        Kochi
1       C002   Anil kumar   anil@gmail.com        kochi
2       C003  Priya Menon  priya@yahoo.com   Trivandrum
3       C001   Anil Kumar   anil@gmail.com        Kochi
4       C004    Rahul Raj rahul@outlook.com      Calicut
5       C005      Priya M.  priya@yahoo.com   Trivandrum
```

**Step 2: Detect exact duplicates across ALL columns**

```python
exact_dup_mask = df.duplicated()
print("Exact duplicate rows (all columns):")
print(df[exact_dup_mask])
```

**Output:**

```
  CustomerID       Name           Email   City
3       C001 Anil Kumar anil@gmail.com   Kochi
```

> Row 3 is an **exact duplicate** of Row 0.

**Step 3: Detect duplicates based on a SUBSET of columns (key-based)**

```python
email_dups = df.duplicated(subset=['Email'])
print("Rows with duplicate emails:")
print(df[email_dups])
```

**Output:**

```
  CustomerID         Name             Email      City
1       C002   Anil kumar   anil@gmail.com      kochi
3       C001   Anil Kumar   anil@gmail.com      Kochi
5       C005      Priya M.  priya@yahoo.com Trivandrum
```

> Three rows share emails with earlier rows.

**Step 4: Count total duplicates using a formula**

The total number of duplicates is computed as:

$$
D = n - \vert U \vert
$$

where $n$ = total number of rows and $\vert U \vert$ = number of unique rows. Substituting:

$$
D = 6 - 5 = 1
$$

So **1 row is an exact duplicate**.

**Step 5: Compute duplicate percentage**

$$
P_D = \dfrac{D}{n} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1}{6} \times 100\% \approx 16.67\%
$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step — Removing Duplicates

```python
# Remove exact duplicates, keeping the FIRST occurrence
df_cleaned = df.drop_duplicates(keep='first')
print(df_cleaned)
```

**Output:**

```
  CustomerID         Name             Email        City
0       C001   Anil Kumar   anil@gmail.com        Kochi
1       C002   Anil kumar   anil@gmail.com        kochi
2       C003  Priya Menon  priya@yahoo.com   Trivandrum
4       C004    Rahul Raj rahul@outlook.com      Calicut
5       C005      Priya M.  priya@yahoo.com   Trivandrum
```

> **5 rows remain** (down from 6). The `keep='first'` parameter retains Row 0 (the first occurrence) and discards Row 3.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step — Canonicalization + Re-Detection (Fuzzy-Style)

To catch the **logical duplicate** between Row 0 (`Anil Kumar`, `Kochi`) and Row 1 (`Anil kumar`, `kochi`), we must first normalize:

```python
df['Name_clean'] = df['Name'].str.strip().str.lower()
df['City_clean'] = df['City'].str.strip().str.lower()

# Now re-check for duplicates on cleaned columns
logical_dups = df.duplicated(subset=['Email', 'Name_clean', 'City_clean'])
print(df[logical_dups])
```

**Output:**

```
  CustomerID         Name             Email      City Name_clean City_clean
1       C002   Anil kumar   anil@gmail.com      kochi  anil kumar      kochi
3       C001   Anil Kumar   anil@gmail.com      Kochi  anil kumar      kochi
5       C005      Priya M.  priya@yahoo.com Trivandrum     priya m. trivandrum
```

> After canonicalization, **3 logical duplicates** surface that exact-match originally missed.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Method | Detects Exact? | Detects Fuzzy? | Speed (n rows) | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `df.duplicated()` | ✅ | ❌ | $O(n)$ | Clean, structured data |
| Hash Map (Python dict) | ✅ | ❌ | $O(n)$ avg | Large streaming data |
| Sorting + Linear Scan | ✅ | ❌ | $O(n \log n)$ | Memory-constrained systems |
| Levenshtein Distance | ❌ | ✅ | $O(n^2 \cdot L^2)$ | Names, addresses |
| TF-IDF + Cosine | ❌ | ✅ | $O(n \cdot V)$ | Long text fields |
| Blocking + Matching | ✅ + ✅ | ✅ | $O(n^2 / k)$ | Million-row datasets |

### 3.6 Verification — Invariant Property

After deduplication, the following invariant **must** always hold:

$$
\text{Count}(df_{cleaned}[\text{key}]) = \text{Unique}(df_{cleaned}[\text{key}])
$$

If this equality fails, the deduplication was incomplete — a critical integrity check examiners expect students to mention.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Deduplication Pipeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Dataset] --> B[Stage 1: Data Profiling]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Key Column Selection]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Canonicalization]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Duplicate Detection]
    E --> F{Detection Method}
    F -->|Exact Match| G[HashMap / Sort-Based]
    F -->|Fuzzy Match| H[Levenshtein / TF-IDF]
    G --> I[Stage 5: Duplicate Flagging]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Stage 6: Review by Analyst]
    J --> K{Decision}
    K -->|Remove| L[drop_duplicates]
    K -->|Merge| M[Record Merging]
    K -->|Retain All| N[Flag for Later]
    L --> O[Cleaned Dataset]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Validation: Uniqueness Invariant]
    P --> Q[End: Ready for EDA / ML]
```

### 4.2 Duplicate Detection Algorithm — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Row i Entered] --> S{Key Already in HashMap?}
    S -->|No| T[Insert: hashmap key = row index]
    S -->|Yes| U[Flag Row i as DUPLICATE]
    T --> V[Move to Row i+1]
    U --> V
    V --> W{More Rows?}
    W -->|Yes| R
    W -->|No| X[Output: Duplicate Set D]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[CSV File]
        I2[Database Table]
        I3[API Stream]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Layer]
        P1[Ingestion Module]
        P2[Profiling Engine]
        P3[Canonicalization Module]
        P4[Detection Engine]
        P5[Removal Module]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Clean CSV]
        O2[Clean Database Table]
        O3[Duplicate Report]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
```

### 4.4 Canonicalization Sub-Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Raw String Value] --> Y1[Trim Whitespace]
    Y1 --> Y2[Lowercase Conversion]
    Y2 --> Y3[Punctuation Removal]
    Y3 --> Y4[Stop Word Removal]
    Y4 --> Z[Canonical Form Ready for Matching]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** 
**Define the term "duplicate record" in a dataset. Differentiate between exact duplicates and fuzzy duplicates with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **duplicate record** is an observation (row) in a dataset that appears more than once, either entirely or partially identical to another row, based on a chosen set of identifying columns (keys).

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Exact Duplicate** | All column values are character-by-character identical | Row 1: `(C001, Anil, anil@gmail.com)` and Row 2: `(C001, Anil, anil@gmail.com)` |
| **Fuzzy Duplicate** | Values are similar but not identical due to typos, case differences, or formatting | Row 1: `Anil Kumar` and Row 2: `anil kumar` — same person, different capitalization |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation with examples)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** 
**What is canonicalization? Why is it performed before duplicate detection? List any two canonicalization steps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Canonicalization** is the process of transforming raw data values into a standardized, uniform format so that semantically equivalent values are treated as identical by the matching algorithm.

**Why it is performed:** Without canonicalization, "Kerala" and "kerala" or "St." and "Street" are treated as different values, causing the matching algorithm to miss true duplicates (false negatives).

**Two canonicalization steps:**
1. **Case normalization** — converting all text to lowercase.
2. **Whitespace trimming** — removing leading/trailing spaces.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for why, 1 mark for steps)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain the **five-stage duplicate detection pipeline** in detail. For each stage, state its objective and one technique used to accomplish it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following dataset of employee records:

| EmpID | Name | Department | Salary |
|---|---|---|---|
| E01 | Ravi | HR | 50000 |
| E02 | Meena | Finance | 60000 |
| E01 | Ravi | HR | 50000 |
| E03 | Ravi | Marketing | 55000 |
| E04 | Meena | Finance | 62000 |

Identify:
(i) The exact duplicate row(s).
(ii) The duplicate(s) based on the `EmpID` column alone.
(iii) Calculate the duplicate percentage.

Write the **pandas code** to remove exact duplicates and produce a cleaned dataframe, keeping the **last** occurrence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five-Stage Duplicate Detection Pipeline (7 marks):**

1. **Data Profiling Stage (1 mark):**
   - *Objective:* Understand dataset shape, cardinality, and data quality.
   - *Technique:* Compute `df.describe()`, `df.nunique()`, value counts to identify suspicious columns.

2. **Key Selection Stage (1 mark):**
   - *Objective:* Choose the column(s) that uniquely identify a record (primary key).
   - *Technique:* Domain knowledge — e.g., `Email` for customers, `EmpID` for employees. For composite keys, use tuples like `(FirstName, LastName, DOB)`.

3. **Canonicalization Stage (1 mark):**
   - *Objective:* Normalize data so semantically identical values match.
   - *Technique:* Lowercasing, whitespace trimming, punctuation removal, date format unification.

4. **Detection Stage (2 marks):**
   - *Objective:* Flag records that match on the chosen key(s).
   - *Technique:* Exact matching via `df.duplicated()` or hash maps; fuzzy matching via Levenshtein distance or TF-IDF cosine similarity for typo-prone fields.

5. **Removal / Merge Stage (2 marks):**
   - *Objective:* Eliminate redundancy while preserving data integrity.
   - *Technique:* `df.drop_duplicates(keep='first'|'last'|False)` for removal; manual merge or aggregation for combining fields from multiple duplicates into a single golden record.

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Exact duplicate row(s) (2 marks):**
Looking at all four columns, **Row index 2** (E01, Ravi, HR, 50000) is an exact duplicate of **Row index 0** (E01, Ravi, HR, 50000).

**(ii) Duplicate based on `EmpID` alone (2 marks):**
- `E01` appears in rows 0 and 2.
- Other EmpIDs (`E02`, `E03`, `E04`) appear once.
- So **Row 2** is a duplicate based on `EmpID`.

**(iii) Duplicate percentage calculation (1 mark):**

$$
D = n - \vert U \vert = 5 - 4 = 1
$$

$$
P_D = \dfrac{1}{5} \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

**Pandas Code (2 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'EmpID':     ['E01', 'E02', 'E01', 'E03', 'E04'],
    'Name':      ['Ravi', 'Meena', 'Ravi', 'Ravi', 'Meena'],
    'Department':['HR', 'Finance', 'HR', 'Marketing', 'Finance'],
    'Salary':    [50000, 60000, 50000, 55000, 62000]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Remove exact duplicates, keep LAST occurrence
df_cleaned = df.drop_duplicates(keep='last')
print(df_cleaned)
```

**Expected Output:**

```
  EmpID   Name Department  Salary
1   E02  Meena    Finance   60000
2   E01   Ravi          HR   50000
3   E03   Ravi  Marketing   55000
4   E04  Meena    Finance   62000
```

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Discuss **exact matching versus fuzzy matching** in duplicate detection. For each, explain the algorithm used, its time complexity, and the type of data it is best suited for. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a small dataset of product reviews with the following entries:

```
ReviewID   Product      Reviewer     Comment
R001       Laptop A     user123      "Great product!"
R002       Laptop A     user123      "great product!"
R003       Laptop B     user456      "Decent value"
R004       Laptop a     user123      "Great product!"
R005       Laptop C     user789      "Highly recommend"
```

Apply the following steps and show the output at each step:
(i) Detect exact duplicates.
(ii) Apply canonicalization (lowercase + trim) and re-detect duplicates on `Product` and `Reviewer`.
(iii) Write pandas code to drop duplicates based on `Reviewer` column alone, keeping the **first** occurrence.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Exact vs Fuzzy Matching (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Exact Matching | Fuzzy Matching |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Bit/character-level equality | Similarity score above threshold $\tau$ |
| **Algorithm** | Hash map (`dict`) or sort + linear scan | Levenshtein distance, Jaccard, Cosine similarity on TF-IDF vectors |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ average (hash) or $O(n \log n)$ (sort) | $O(n^2 \cdot L^2)$ naïve (Levenshtein), $O(n \cdot V)$ (TF-IDF) |
| **Best Suited For** | Clean, structured data with enforced formats (IDs, emails, phone numbers) | Unstructured or dirty data with typos (names, addresses, free-text) |
| **Threshold** | None — strict equality | $\tau$ typically 0.85 or 0.90 |

*Exact matching* is **fast and deterministic** but fails on minor typos. *Fuzzy matching* is **robust** but computationally expensive and requires a tuned threshold. **Blocking** is often combined with fuzzy matching to reduce the $O(n^2)$ pairwise cost to $O(n^2/k)$ where $k$ is the number of blocks.

*(Distribution: 2 marks algorithm, 2 marks complexity, 2 marks suitability, 1 mark example)*

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Output (7 marks):**

**Initial dataframe:**

```
  ReviewID   Product Reviewer         Comment
0     R001  Laptop A   user123  Great product!
1     R002  Laptop A   user123  great product!
2     R003  Laptop B   user456    Decent value
3     R004  Laptop a   user123  Great product!
4     R005  Laptop C   user789  Highly recommend
```

**(i) Exact duplicates — 0 duplicates (2 marks)**

No two rows are character-identical. `df.duplicated().sum()` returns **0**.

**(ii) Canonicalization + re-detection (3 marks):**

```python
df['Product_clean'] = df['Product'].str.strip().str.lower()
df['Reviewer_clean'] = df['Reviewer'].str.strip().str.lower()

dups = df.duplicated(subset=['Product_clean', 'Reviewer_clean'])
print(df[dups])
```

**Output:**

```
  ReviewID   Product Reviewer        Comment Product_clean Reviewer_clean
1     R002  Laptop A   user123 great product!      laptop a        user123
3     R004  Laptop a   user123 Great product!      laptop a        user123
```

> Rows 1 and 3 are now flagged as duplicates based on normalized `Product` + `Reviewer`.

**(iii) Drop duplicates based on `Reviewer`, keep FIRST (2 marks):**

```python
df_unique_reviewers = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Reviewer'], keep='first')
print(df_unique_reviewers)
```

**Output:**

```
  ReviewID   Product Reviewer         Comment
0     R001  Laptop A   user123  Great product!
2     R003  Laptop B   user456    Decent value
4     R005  Laptop C   user789  Highly recommend
```

> 3 rows remain — one per unique reviewer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `duplicated()` with `drop_duplicates()`:** `duplicated()` returns a *boolean mask*; `drop_duplicates()` *removes* rows. Examiners deduct marks for mixing up.
> 2. **Forgetting `keep` parameter default:** The default is `keep='first'`. If a question says "keep the last occurrence" and you write `keep='first'`, you lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring canonicalization:** Detecting duplicates without first normalizing case/whitespace leads to missing logical duplicates — examiner expects this step.
> 4. **Not validating the uniqueness invariant:** Always end your answer with `len(df) == df.nunique().sum()` (or equivalent) to prove the data is clean.
> 5. **Miscounting duplicates:** If `n = 6` and `unique = 5`, then `D = 1`, NOT `D = 5`. Students often confuse total with unique.
> 6. **Writing `duplicated()` on the whole dataframe vs subset:** Subset-based detection requires the explicit `subset=[...]` argument; omitting it means full-row matching only.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Duplicate** = a row appearing more than once, judged by **key column(s)**.
- ✅ **Four duplicate types:** Exact, Partial, Fuzzy/Near, Logical.
- ✅ **Five-stage pipeline:** Profile → Key Selection → Canonicalize → Detect → Remove/Merge.
- ✅ **Three core pandas functions:** `df.duplicated()`, `df.drop_duplicates(keep=...)`, `df.nunique()`.
- ✅ **`keep` parameter values:** `'first'` (default), `'last'`, `False` (drop all duplicates).
- ✅ **Canonicalization** includes lowercasing, trimming, punctuation removal, date unification.
- ✅ **Duplicate count formula:** $D = n - \vert U \vert$ where $n$ = total rows, $\vert U \vert$ = unique values.
- ✅ **Duplicate percentage:** $P_D = \dfrac{D}{n} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Fuzzy similarity metrics:** Jaccard (sets), Levenshtein (strings), Cosine (TF-IDF vectors).
- ✅ **Naïve fuzzy complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — mitigated by **blocking** to $O(n^2/k)$.
- ✅ **Invariant to verify after dedup:** `count(key) == nunique(key)` — if violated, dedup is incomplete.
- ✅ **Threshold $\tau$** is typically **0.85 to 0.90** for fuzzy matching in production systems.
- ✅ **Library analogy:** Duplicate = extra catalog entry; Deduplication = keeping one canonical copy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sorting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Sorting

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sorting** is a fundamental operation in data handling and computer science defined as the systematic rearrangement of a collection of elements (records) into a prescribed order, typically ascending or descending, based on a defined key or criterion. In the context of **PECMT695 - Data Handling and Visualization**, sorting is the foundational preprocessing step that transforms raw, unstructured datasets into an ordered, indexable, and visually interpretable form, enabling efficient querying, aggregation, and pattern recognition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Sorting* is the algorithmic process of arranging data elements in a specific logical sequence (ascending/descending) using a comparison or transformation rule. It is classified as an **O(n log n)** optimal operation in its general form and forms the basis of **Order Statistics**, **Database Indexing**, and **Visual Rendering Pipelines**.

Mathematically, given an unordered sequence:
$$A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_n\}$$

The sorting operation produces an ordered permutation:
$$A' = \{a_{\pi(1)}, a_{\pi(2)}, \dots, a_{\pi(n)}\}$$

such that:
$$a_{\pi(1)} \leq a_{\pi(2)} \leq a_{\pi(3)} \leq \dots \leq a_{\pi(n)}$$

where $\pi$ represents a permutation function that maps original indices to their sorted positions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a librarian receives a **cart of 500 unsorted books** dumped randomly on a desk. Each book has an alphanumeric call number printed on its spine (for example, `CS-201`, `MA-110`, `PH-305`). The librarian's job is to arrange them in **strict ascending order of their call numbers** on the shelf.

The librarian could use several strategies:
- **Method 1 (Bubble Sort intuition):** Walk along the shelf, and whenever two adjacent books are out of order, swap them. Repeat passes until no more swaps are needed.
- **Method 2 (Selection Sort intuition):** Scan the entire cart, find the smallest call number, place it first on the shelf. Then scan the remaining cart, find the next smallest, place it second, and so on.
- **Method 3 (Quick Sort intuition):** Pick one book as a *pivot*. Divide the cart into two piles — one with all books having smaller call numbers, one with larger. Recursively sort each pile.

This is exactly what sorting algorithms do — they formalize these physical strategies into deterministic mathematical procedures.

## 1.3 Why Sorting is Pivotal in Data Handling and Visualization

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Examinations:**
> Sorting is not just a CS algorithm topic — it directly impacts **data visualization pipelines**. Sorted data allows:
> 1. **Efficient rendering** of bar charts, histograms, and heatmaps.
> 2. **Median, Quartile, and Percentile computation** for box plots.
> 3. **Rank-based transformations** in data normalization.
> 4. **Log-log plot smoothing** in time-series visualization.

The standard metrics you must memorize are:

- **Time Complexity (T(n)):** Quantitative measure of computational steps as a function of input size $n$.
- **Space Complexity (S(n)):** Auxiliary memory consumed by the algorithm.
- **Stability (Boolean):** Property that preserves the relative order of equal elements.
- **In-Place Property (Boolean):** Uses only $O(1)$ auxiliary space.
- **Number of Comparisons (C(n)):** Count of pairwise key comparisons.
- **Number of Swaps (M(n)):** Count of element interchanges.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sorting Algorithms

Sorting algorithms can be classified along **four orthogonal axes** as per the KTU 2024 scheme:

### Axis 1: Based on Memory Usage
- **Internal Sort:** Entire dataset fits in **RAM**. Examples: Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Quick, Heap.
- **External Sort:** Dataset exceeds RAM; uses disk/tape. Examples: Polyphase Merge, Cascade Merge.

### Axis 2: Based on Comparison vs Non-Comparison
- **Comparison-Based:** Decision made via pairwise key comparison. Lower bound is $\Omega(n \log n)$.
- **Non-Comparison Based:** Uses bucket/radix techniques. Achieves $O(n)$ in special cases (Counting, Radix, Bucket).

### Axis 3: Based on Stability
- **Stable Sort:** Preserves relative order of equal keys (e.g., Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort).
- **Unstable Sort:** May reorder equal keys (e.g., Quick Sort, Heap Sort, Selection Sort).

### Axis 4: Based on Recursion
- **Recursive:** Merge Sort, Quick Sort.
- **Iterative:** Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Heap Sort.

## 2.2 The Comparison Lower Bound Theorem

For any comparison-based sorting algorithm operating on $n$ distinct elements, the **decision tree** must have at least $n!$ leaves (one for each permutation). Since a binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^h$ leaves:

$$2^h \geq n! \implies h \geq \log_2(n!)$$

Using Stirling's approximation $\log_2(n!) \approx n \log_2 n - 1.44n$:

$$T(n)_{\min} = \Omega(n \log n)$$

This proves that **no comparison-based sort can beat $O(n \log n)$** in the worst case.

## 2.3 Detailed Sorting Algorithm Analysis

### 2.3.1 Bubble Sort
Repeatedly traverses the list, comparing adjacent pairs and swapping if out of order. Each full pass "bubbles" the largest unsorted element to its correct end position.

- **Best Case:** $T(n) = O(n)$ — already sorted, with early termination optimization.
- **Average Case:** $T(n) = O(n^2)$.
- **Worst Case:** $T(n) = O(n^2)$ — reverse sorted.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ in-place.
- **Stability:** Stable.
- **Comparisons:** $C(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **Swaps (Worst):** $M(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

### 2.3.2 Selection Sort
Divides the array into a sorted prefix and unsorted suffix. At each iteration, finds the **minimum** of the unsorted suffix and swaps it to the boundary.

- **Best/Average/Worst Case:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ — uniform regardless of input.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ in-place.
- **Stability:** Unstable (long-distance swap disrupts equal-key order).
- **Comparisons:** $C(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — fixed.
- **Swaps:** $M(n) = n - 1$ — minimal.

### 2.3.3 Insertion Sort
Builds the sorted array one element at a time by inserting each new element into its correct position within the sorted prefix. Equivalent to how humans sort playing cards.

- **Best Case:** $T(n) = O(n)$ — already sorted, no shifts.
- **Average Case:** $T(n) = O(n^2)$.
- **Worst Case:** $T(n) = O(n^2)$ — reverse sorted.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ in-place.
- **Stability:** Stable.
- **Comparisons (Worst):** $C(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

### 2.3.4 Merge Sort
A **divide-and-conquer** algorithm that recursively divides the array into halves, sorts each half, and merges them in linear time.

- **Best/Average/Worst Case:** $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ — uniform.
- **Space:** $O(n)$ — auxiliary array for merging.
- **Stability:** Stable (if merging is done carefully).
- **Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$.

### 2.3.5 Quick Sort
Also a **divide-and-conquer** algorithm. Selects a **pivot** and partitions the array into elements less than the pivot and elements greater than the pivot, then recursively sorts each partition.

- **Best/Average Case:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.
- **Worst Case:** $T(n) = O(n^2)$ — pivot always chosen as extreme (already sorted input with bad pivot selection).
- **Space:** $O(\log n)$ — stack space for recursion.
- **Stability:** Unstable.
- **Recurrence (Average):** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$.
- **Recurrence (Worst):** $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n)$.

### 2.3.6 Heap Sort
Uses a **binary max-heap** data structure. Builds the heap in $O(n)$ time, then repeatedly extracts the maximum and places it at the end of the array.

- **Best/Average/Worst Case:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ — guaranteed.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ in-place.
- **Stability:** Unstable.
- **Heap Build:** $O(n)$.
- **Extract Phase:** $O(n \log n)$.

### 2.3.7 Counting Sort (Non-Comparison)
A **non-comparison** sort for integers in a known range $[0, k]$. Counts occurrences of each value, then reconstructs the sorted array.

- **Time:** $T(n) = O(n + k)$.
- **Space:** $O(n + k)$.
- **Stability:** Stable.
- **Constraint:** $k$ must be $O(n)$ for the sort to be linear.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU Board Exams**

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | Stable | In-Place |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble Sort | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes | Yes |
| Selection Sort | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | No | Yes |
| Insertion Sort | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes | Yes |
| Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Yes | No |
| Quick Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | No | Yes |
| Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ | No | Yes |
| Counting Sort | $O(n + k)$ | $O(n + k)$ | $O(n + k)$ | $O(n + k)$ | Yes | No |

**Key Universal Formulas:**

$$\text{Total Comparisons (Bubble/Selection)}: \quad C(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (n-i) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

$$\text{Merge Sort Recurrence:}\quad T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) \implies T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

$$\text{Quick Sort Recurrence:}\quad T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) \quad \text{(best/avg)}$$

$$\text{Quick Sort Recurrence:}\quad T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) \implies T(n) = O(n^2) \quad \text{(worst)}$$

$$\text{Lower Bound:}\quad T_{\min}(n) = \Omega(n \log n) \quad \text{(comparison-based)}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Database Indexing:** B-Tree indices in RDBMS rely on sorted keys for $O(\log n)$ range queries.
2. **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Sorting latitude/longitude pairs enables efficient map tiling.
