<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Power of a Statistical Test". Why is a power of 0.80 considered a conventional minimum in research design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The power of a statistical test is the probability of **correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis** $H_0$ when the alternative hypothesis $H_1$ is actually true. Mathematically:

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ is true})$$

A power of **0.80 (80%)** is the conventional minimum recommended by statisticians (notably Cohen, 1988) because it balances two competing demands: (1) it is **high enough** to reliably detect real effects (only 20% chance of missing them), and (2) it keeps the required sample size **practically feasible** for most research budgets. Lowering the threshold (e.g., to 0.50) would lead to many missed effects; raising it (e.g., to 0.99) would require prohibitively large samples.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Type I and Type II errors. How are they related to the significance level $\alpha$ and the power of the test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Type I Error ($\alpha$) | Type II Error ($\beta$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true | Not rejecting $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true |
| Also called | False Positive | False Negative |
| Controlled by | Pre-set significance level | Effect size, $n$, and $\alpha$ |
| Relationship to Power | Setting $\alpha$ sets the upper bound on $\beta$ | Power = $1 - \beta$ |

For a fixed sample size $n$, decreasing $\alpha$ (e.g., from 0.05 to 0.01) **increases $\beta$**, which **decreases power**. The only way to reduce both errors simultaneously is to **increase the sample size**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). Both alternatives are provided below with complete model solutions.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**A researcher wants to test whether a new teaching method improves student scores. The historical mean score is $\mu_0 = 70$ with $\sigma = 12$. The researcher believes the new method raises the true mean to $\mu_1 = 75$.**

**(a)** [7 marks] If the researcher plans to use a sample of $n = 36$ students at $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), calculate the power of the test.

**(b)** [7 marks] What sample size $n$ is required to achieve a power of 0.90 at the same significance level?

---

#### Model Solution A(a) — Power Calculation

**Given:** $\mu_0 = 70$, $\mu_1 = 75$, $\sigma = 12$, $n = 36$, $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 1: Compute the effect size (true difference).**
$$\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0 = 75 - 70 = 5$$

**Step 2: Compute the standard error of the mean.**
$$\text{SE} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2$$

**Step 3: Compute the standardized difference (non-centrality).**
$$\lambda = \frac{\delta}{\text{SE}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

**Step 4: Apply the one-tailed power formula.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} &= \Phi\!\left(\lambda - z_{\alpha}\right) \\
&= \Phi(2.5 - 1.645) \\
&= \Phi(0.855) \\
&\approx 0.6038 \;\;(\text{or } 60.38\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The test has only a **60.38% chance** of detecting the 5-point improvement, which is **below** the conventional 80% threshold. The researcher needs a larger sample.

---

#### Model Solution A(b) — Required Sample Size for Power = 0.90

**Step 1: Identify the $z$-values.**
- $z_{\alpha} = 1.645$ (one-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$)
- $z_{\beta} = z_{0.10} = 1.28$ (since $\beta = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$)

**Step 2: Apply the sample-size formula.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \left(\frac{(z_{\alpha} + z_{\beta})\,\sigma}{\delta}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{(1.645 + 1.28) \times 12}{5}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{2.925 \times 12}{5}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{35.1}{5}\right)^{2} \\
&= (7.02)^{2} \\
&= 49.28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Round up to the next whole number.**
$$\boxed{\,n = 50 \text{ students}\,}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** To achieve 90% power, the researcher must enroll at least **50 students** — an increase of 14 over the original plan.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**A quality-control engineer suspects that a machine is producing a defective rate higher than the industry standard of $p_0 = 0.05$. She suspects the true rate is $p_1 = 0.08$.**

**(a)** [7 marks] If she inspects $n = 200$ items at $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), what is the power of her test? Use the normal approximation to the binomial.

**(b)** [7 marks] How many items must she inspect to achieve a power of 0.85? (Use the pooled standard error approximation.)

---

#### Model Solution B(a) — Power for a Proportion Test

**Given:** $p_0 = 0.05$, $p_1 = 0.08$, $n = 200$, one-tailed $\alpha = 0.05$, $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 1: Compute the pooled standard error under $H_0$.**
$$
\text{SE}_0 = \sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 0.95}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0475}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0002375} \approx 0.01541
$$

**Step 2: Compute the difference in proportions.**
$$p_1 - p_0 = 0.08 - 0.05 = 0.03$$

**Step 3: Compute the standardized effect under $H_1$ (using $p_1$ for SE under $H_1$).**
$$
\text{SE}_1 = \sqrt{\frac{p_1(1-p_1)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.08 \times 0.92}{200}} = \sqrt{0.000368} \approx 0.01918
$$

**Step 4: Compute the $Z$-score representing the distance between $p_1$ and the critical value.**

The critical proportion (boundary) is:
$$
p_c = p_0 + z_{\alpha}\,\text{SE}_0 = 0.05 + 1.645 \times 0.01541 \approx 0.05 + 0.02535 = 0.07535
$$

The standardized distance from $p_1$ to this boundary (using $\text{SE}_1$):
$$
Z = \frac{p_1 - p_c}{\text{SE}_1} = \frac{0.08 - 0.07535}{0.01918} = \frac{0.00465}{0.01918} \approx 0.2424
$$

**Step 5: Power is the probability that $Z$ exceeds zero under $H_1$.**

$$
\text{Power} = \Phi(0.2424) \approx 0.5957 \;\;(\text{or } 59.57\%)
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** With only 200 items, the engineer has a **59.6%** chance of detecting the 3% increase in defect rate — clearly underpowered.

---

#### Model Solution B(b) — Required Sample Size for Power = 0.85

**Step 1: Identify $z$-values.**
- $z_{\alpha} = 1.645$ (one-tailed)
- $z_{\beta} = z_{0.15} = 1.036$ (since $\beta = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$)

**Step 2: Use the pooled-proportion sample-size formula.**
$$
n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1) + p_0(1-p_0)}}{\vert p_1 - p_0 \vert}\right)^{2}
$$

where $\bar{p} = (p_0 + p_1)/2 = (0.05 + 0.08)/2 = 0.065$.

**Step 3: Compute the two square-root terms.**
$$
\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.065 \times 0.935} = \sqrt{0.12155} \approx 0.3486
$$
$$
\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1) + p_0(1-p_0)} = \sqrt{0.0736 + 0.0475} = \sqrt{0.1211} \approx 0.3480
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the formula.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \left(\frac{1.645 \times 0.3486 + 1.036 \times 0.3480}{0.03}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{0.5734 + 0.3605}{0.03}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{0.9339}{0.03}\right)^{2} \\
&= (31.13)^{2} \\
&\approx 969.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Round up.**
$$\boxed{\,n = 970 \text{ items}\,}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The engineer must inspect **970 items** — nearly 5× the original sample — to achieve 85% power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** the sample size. KTU board examiners deduct **0.5 mark** if $n$ is left as a non-integer (e.g., 49.28). Always write $n = \lceil \text{value} \rceil$.
> 2. **One-tailed vs. two-tailed confusion.** When the question states "greater than" or "less than", use $z_{\alpha}$ (one-tailed). For "different from" or "not equal to", use $z_{\alpha/2}$ (two-tailed). Using the wrong $z$ value can change the answer by 5–10%.
> 3. **In Power formula:** never confuse $\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$ with "$\alpha + \beta = 1$". They are **not** complementary sums; $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are independent probabilities constrained by the chosen $n$.
> 4. **In Sample Size formula:** write the full three-line derivation, not just the final value. Examiners award **2 of the 7 marks** for showing the algebraic rearrangement.
> 5. **In Proportion Tests:** always specify which variance estimate is being used ($p_0$ under $H_0$ vs. $p_1$ under $H_1$). Mixing them is the single most common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):**

- **Definition:** Power $= 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})$ — memorize this cold.
- **The Four Power Levers:** $\alpha$ (up = power up), $n$ (up = power up), $\delta$ (up = power up), $\sigma$ (up = power **down**).
- **Conventional Thresholds:** $\alpha = 0.05$ and Power $= 0.80$ are the **default** values for any KTU problem unless stated otherwise.
- **Master the Two Formulas:**
  1. **Power** $= \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\delta}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} - z_{\alpha/2}\right)$ (two-tailed version; drop $/2$ for one-tailed).
  2. **Sample Size** $n = \left(\dfrac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})\,\sigma}{\delta}\right)^{2}$.
- **Effect Size Benchmarks (Cohen's $d$):** Small $= 0.2$, Medium $= 0.5$, Large $= 0.8$. Memorize these — KTU board loves them.
- **$z$-value Table Memorization for $\alpha = 0.05$:**
  - One-tailed: $z_{0.05} = 1.645$
  - Two-tailed: $z_{0.025} = 1.96$
  - For $\beta = 0.20$ (Power $= 0.80$): $z_{0.20} = 0.842$
  - For $\beta = 0.10$ (Power $= 0.90$): $z_{0.10} = 1.282$
  - For $\beta = 0.05$ (Power $= 0.95$): $z_{0.05} = 1.645$
- **Trade-off Rule:** Reducing $\alpha$ while keeping $n$ fixed ⇒ Power **drops**. The cure is more data.
- **Proportion Test Pooled SE:** $\text{SE}_0 = \sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}$ under $H_0$; switch to $p_1$ when computing power.
- **Always round $n$ UP** to the next whole number.
- **"Underpowered study"** = Power $< 0.80$ = wasted data collection. This phrase is a **guaranteed 2-mark** short-answer hit in viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Distributions - Type Arguments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Common Distributions - Type Arguments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Data Handling and Visualization**, a **Common Distribution** refers to a *standardized probability distribution* that is repeatedly observed when modelling real-world data — namely the **Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Uniform, Exponential, Bernoulli, and Log-Normal** families. The phrase **"Type Arguments"** denotes the *parameter set* (shape, location, scale, intensity, etc.) that mathematically characterises a particular member of a distribution family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A *type argument* is the formal input parameter (e.g., `loc`, `scale`, `n`, `p`, `λ`) that **uniquely identifies a single member** of a distribution family. Without these arguments, the distribution is *unspecified*; with them, the distribution becomes a *concrete, computable function*.

In programming libraries such as Python's `scipy.stats`, the **type arguments** are passed as keyword arguments to a *frozen distribution object*, allowing the user to compute **PMF, PDF, CDF, percent-point function (PPF), moments, and samples** consistently.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Distribution Cookie Factory"

Imagine a **biscuit factory**:

- The **Type** of distribution is the *mould* (Star, Circle, Heart). The mould alone is abstract — it describes the *shape* of the family.
- The **Type Arguments** are the *settings on the machine* — width, depth, centre-point, and intensity. They convert the abstract mould into a *specific, edible biscuit*.

| Factory Analogy | Statistical Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Biscuit Mould | Distribution Family (e.g., Normal) |
| Machine Settings | Type Arguments (e.g., `loc=5, scale=2`) |
| Finished Biscuit | A Frozen Distribution Object |
| Sampling a biscuit | Drawing a random variate `rvs()` |
| Weighing the batch | Computing mean / variance |

So whenever a question says *"use a Normal distribution with mean 70 and standard deviation 10"*, the **Type** is `norm` and the **Type Arguments** are `loc=70, scale=10`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** "Type" answers *"WHICH family?"* and "Arguments" answer *"WHICH member of that family?"* This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU 2024 ESE.

## 1.3 Why These Distributions Are "Common"

A handful of distributions reappear in nearly every dataset because of two deep mathematical reasons:

1. **The Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** The sum/average of many independent random variables — regardless of their original distribution — converges to a **Normal** distribution.
2. **The Poisson Limit Theorem:** Rare events occurring independently in a fixed interval tend toward a **Poisson** distribution.

Hence, real-world phenomena (heights, measurement errors, server hits per minute, radioactive decay, manufacturing defects) almost always map to one of these canonical families.

> [!TIP]
> The constant that connects Poisson and Exponential distributions is the rate parameter **λ** — it is the most important "type argument" in the entire module. Memorise its role.

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants Used

| Symbol | Value / Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $e$ (Euler's number) | $\approx 2.71828$ |
| $\pi$ (Pi) | $\approx 3.14159$ |
| $\Gamma(\cdot)$ | Gamma function (generalises factorial) |
| $\Phi(\cdot)$ | Standard Normal CDF |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of "Type Argument" Concept

Every statistical distribution $f(x \mid \theta)$ is a function of:
- The **random variable** $x$ (input data point), and
- The **parameter vector** $\theta$ (the type arguments).

The Three-Tier Logic:

1. **Tier 1 — Family Selection:** Choose the type (e.g., `binom`).
2. **Tier 2 — Argument Assignment:** Pass specific values (e.g., `n=10, p=0.3`).
3. **Tier 3 — Computation:** Evaluate PMF/PDF/CDF, generate samples, compute moments.

## 2.2 The Seven Common Distributions — Master Formula Sheet

The following table is **high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE**. Memorise the **PMF/PDF column** and the **Type Arguments column**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Discrete distributions use **PMF** ($P(X=k)$); continuous distributions use **PDF** ($f(x)$). The sum of PMF over support $= 1$; the integral of PDF over support $= 1$.

| # | Distribution | Type | Support | PMF / PDF | Type Arguments | Mean $\mu$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Real-World Use |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Bernoulli** | Discrete | $x \in \{0,1\}$ | $P(X=k) = p^{k}(1-p)^{1-k}$ | `p` (success prob.) | $p$ | $p(1-p)$ | Coin toss, pass/fail |
| 2 | **Binomial** | Discrete | $x \in \{0,1,\dots,n\}$ | $P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k}p^{k}(1-p)^{n-k}$ | `n` (trials), `p` (success prob.) | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | # defects in batch, # heads in $n$ flips |
| 3 | **Poisson** | Discrete | $x \in \{0,1,2,\dots\}$ | $P(X=k) = \dfrac{\lambda^{k}e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | `λ` (rate / intensity) | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | Calls/hr, accidents/day |
| 4 | **Uniform (Discrete)** | Discrete | $x \in \{a, a+1, \dots, b\}$ | $P(X=k) = \dfrac{1}{b-a+1}$ | `a, b` (bounds) | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a+1)^2-1}{12}$ | Dice roll, lottery |
| 5 | **Uniform (Continuous)** | Continuous | $x \in [a, b]$ | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | `a` (loc), `b-a` (scale) | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | Random waiting within a window |
| 6 | **Exponential** | Continuous | $x \in [0, \infty)$ | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | `λ` (rate) or `1/λ` (scale) | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Time-between-events, reliability |
| 7 | **Normal (Gaussian)** | Continuous | $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$ | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | `μ` (loc / mean), `σ` (scale / std) | $\mu$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | Heights, errors, IQ |
| 8 | **Log-Normal** | Continuous | $x \in (0, \infty)$ | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | `μ` (mean of log), `σ` (std of log) | $e^{\mu+\sigma^{2}/2}$ | $(e^{\sigma^{2}}-1)e^{2\mu+\sigma^{2}}$ | Income, stock prices |

## 2.3 Argument Naming Convention in `scipy.stats`

KTU 2024 often references the **Python `scipy.stats` library** for data handling. The keyword arguments are *standardised*:

| Family | Scipy Class | Positional Argument | Keyword Argument |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Bernoulli | `bernoulli` | `p` | — |
| Binomial | `binom` | `n, p` | — |
| Poisson | `poisson` | `μ` (mu) or `λ` | — |
| Uniform | `uniform` | — | `loc=a, scale=b-a` |
| Exponential | `expon` | — | `loc=0, scale=1/λ` |
| Normal | `norm` | — | `loc=μ, scale=σ` |
| Log-Normal | `lognorm` | — | `s=σ, scale=e^μ` |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite:** A 3-mark question often asks: *"Identify the type arguments of a `scipy.stats.norm` object used to model a population with mean 100 and variance 25."* The correct answer: **`loc=100, scale=5`** (note: $\sigma=\sqrt{25}=5$, NOT $25$).

## 2.4 The Two Universal Arguments: `loc` and `scale`

Many continuous distributions share a canonical parameterisation:

$$X = \text{loc} + \text{scale} \cdot Z$$

where $Z$ is the *standard* (zero-mean, unit-variance) member. Hence:

- `loc` shifts the distribution **horizontally**.
- `scale` stretches the distribution **horizontally** (and scales variance by `scale²`).

This is the foundation of the **Location-Scale Family**, which includes Normal, Uniform, Exponential, Logistic, Cauchy, Laplace, and more.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Distribution | Real Engineering Scenario |
|:---|:---|
| Normal | Tolerance analysis in mechanical design; measurement uncertainty in instrumentation |
| Binomial | Quality control — # defective items in a batch of $n$ |
| Poisson | Network packet arrivals; queueing theory; failure-rate modelling |
| Exponential | Component lifetime; reliability engineering; radio-active decay |
| Log-Normal | Fatigue life of metals; rainfall data; income distribution |
| Uniform | Cryptographic random number generation; Monte Carlo sampling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **full mathematical derivations** of the most-asked properties of common distributions. Every algebraic step is shown.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean and Variance of the Binomial Distribution

**Given:** $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$ with PMF $P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k}p^{k}(1-p)^{n-k}$.

### Step 1 — Express Binomial as Sum of Bernoullis

Each of the $n$ independent Bernoulli trials is $X_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $P(X_i=1)=p$.

$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

### Step 2 — Compute $E[X_i]$ for a Single Bernoulli

For $X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$:

$$E[X_i] = \sum_{k \in \{0,1\}} k \cdot p^{k}(1-p)^{1-k} = 0 \cdot (1-p) + 1 \cdot p = p$$

### Step 3 — Apply Linearity of Expectation

$$E[X] = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = np$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\text{Var}(X_i)$

First, $E[X_i^{2}] = 0^{2}(1-p) + 1^{2}p = p$.

$$\text{Var}(X_i) = E[X_i^{2}] - (E[X_i])^{2} = p - p^{2} = p(1-p)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Independence

Since the $X_i$ are independent, variances add:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(1-p) = np(1-p)$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\mu = np \quad\text{and}\quad \sigma^{2} = np(1-p)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean of the Poisson Distribution

**Given:** $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$ with PMF $P(X=k) = \dfrac{\lambda^{k}e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

### Step 1 — Definition of Expectation

$$E[X] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{\lambda^{k}e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$$

### Step 2 — Pull Out the $k=0$ Term

The $k=0$ term is zero, so we can start the sum at $k=1$:

$$E[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{\lambda^{k}e^{-\lambda}}{k!} = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}e^{-\lambda}}{(k-1)!}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $j = k-1$

When $k=1, j=0$; as $k \to \infty, j \to \infty$:

$$E[X] = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j+1}e^{-\lambda}}{j!} = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!}$$

### Step 4 — Recognise the Taylor Series of $e^{\lambda}$

We know that $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!} = e^{\lambda}$.

$$E[X] = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\mu = \lambda}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean of the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** $X \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda)$ with PDF $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \ge 0$.

### Step 1 — Definition of Expectation

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx$$

### Step 2 — Integration by Parts

Let $u = x \Rightarrow du = dx$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx \Rightarrow v = -e^{-\lambda x}$.

$$E[X] = \left[-x e^{-\lambda x}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\,dx$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Boundary Term

As $x \to \infty$, $x e^{-\lambda x} \to 0$ (exponential dominates). At $x=0$, the term is $0$. So the boundary term is $0$.

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Remaining Integral

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\,dx = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda x}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A similar integration by parts yields $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ (excluded here for brevity, but it is a **high-yield KTU derivation**).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Why the Normal PDF Integrates to 1

**Given:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$.

### Step 1 — Substitution $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$, so $dz = dx/\sigma$

$$I = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^{2}}{2}\right)\sigma\,dz = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^{2}/2}\,dz$$

### Step 2 — Square the Integral

$$I^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-(x^{2}+y^{2})/2}\,dx\,dy$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Polar Coordinates

Let $r^{2} = x^{2}+y^{2}$ and $dA = r\,dr\,d\theta$:

$$I^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-r^{2}/2}\, r\,dr\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \left[-e^{-r^{2}/2}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Conclude

$$I = 1 \quad \text{(since } I > 0\text{)}$$

This confirms the Normal PDF is **properly normalised**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A call centre receives an average of $\lambda = 4$ calls per minute. Find the probability of receiving *exactly 6* calls in a minute. Also find the mean and standard deviation.

### Given
- Distribution: **Poisson**
- Type Argument: $\lambda = 4$
- Required: $P(X=6)$

### Step 1 — Apply the PMF

$$P(X=6) = \frac{\lambda^{6} e^{-\lambda}}{6!} = \frac{4^{6} e^{-4}}{720}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Numerically

$$4^{6} = 4096, \quad e^{-4} \approx 0.018316$$

$$P(X=6) = \frac{4096 \times 0.018316}{720} = \frac{75.020}{720} \approx 0.1042$$

### Step 3 — Mean and Variance

By the formula sheet: $\mu = \lambda = 4$, $\sigma^{2} = \lambda = 4$, so $\sigma = 2$.

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{P(X=6) \approx 0.1042 \quad ; \quad \mu = 4 \quad ; \quad \sigma = 2}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Aid

Use this **decision matrix** to identify the correct distribution for a problem:

| Scenario Clue | Correct Distribution | Type Argument(s) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| "# successes in $n$ trials" | Binomial | `n, p` |
| "Average rate $\lambda$" (discrete count) | Poisson | `λ` |
| "Time until next event" | Exponential | `λ` (rate) |
| "Probability density is bell-shaped" | Normal | `μ, σ` |
| "Each trial has 2 outcomes" | Bernoulli | `p` |
| "Equal probability over an interval" | Uniform | `a, b` |
| "Variable is product of many small %s" | Log-Normal | `μ, σ` of $\ln x$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> Differentiate between the **"type"** and **"type arguments"** of a probability distribution. Illustrate with the Normal distribution.

**Model Answer:**

The **type** of a probability distribution identifies the *family* of the function, while **type arguments** are the *parameter values* that uniquely specify a member of that family.

For the Normal distribution:

- **Type:** `norm` (Gaussian family)
- **Type arguments:** `loc = μ` (mean) and `scale = σ` (standard deviation, must be $> 0$).

A Normal distribution is *not* fully specified by saying "Normal"; one must additionally supply $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to obtain a concrete PDF:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

For example, $\text{Normal}(\mu=0, \sigma=1)$ is the *standard* normal, while $\text{Normal}(\mu=100, \sigma=15)$ is a different member used to model IQ scores.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> A Poisson distribution has $\lambda = 5$. State the **type**, the **type argument**, the **mean**, and the **variance**.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Value |
|:---|:---|
| Type | `poisson` (discrete) |
| Type Argument | $\lambda = 5$ |
| Mean $\mu$ | $\lambda = 5$ |
| Variance $\sigma^{2}$ | $\lambda = 5$ |

A useful property to remember: for a Poisson distribution, the **mean equals the variance**, both equal to $\lambda$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **mean** and **variance** of the Binomial distribution $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$ from first principles using the fact that $X$ is the sum of $n$ i.i.d. Bernoulli($p$) random variables.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing process produces items with a probability of defect $p = 0.02$. In a batch of $n = 200$ items, find:
> 1. The probability that **exactly 3** items are defective.
> 2. The probability that **at most 2** items are defective.
> 3. The expected number of defective items.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Setup:** Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli($p$) random variables where $X_i = 1$ if trial $i$ is a "success". Then:

$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$$

**Step 1 — Mean of a Bernoulli:**

$$E[X_i] = 0 \cdot (1-p) + 1 \cdot p = p$$

$$E[X_i^{2}] = 0^{2}(1-p) + 1^{2}p = p$$

**Step 2 — Mean of Binomial (Linearity):**

$$E[X] = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = np$$

**Step 3 — Variance of a Bernoulli:**

$$\text{Var}(X_i) = E[X_i^{2}] - (E[X_i])^{2} = p - p^{2} = p(1-p)$$

**Step 4 — Variance of Binomial (Independence):**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = n \cdot p(1-p)$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\mu = np \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma^{2} = np(1-p)}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $n = 200$, $p = 0.02$.

**Step 1 — Compute parameters:**

$$\mu = np = 200 \times 0.02 = 4$$
$$\sigma^{2} = np(1-p) = 200 \times 0.02 \times 0.98 = 3.92$$
$$\sigma \approx 1.98$$

**Step 2 — Probability of exactly 3 defectives:**

$$P(X=3) = \binom{200}{3}(0.02)^{3}(0.98)^{197}$$

$$\binom{200}{3} = \frac{200 \times 199 \times 198}{6} = 1{,}313{,}400$$

$$(0.02)^{3} = 8 \times 10^{-6}, \quad (0.98)^{197} \approx 0.01810$$

$$P(X=3) \approx 1{,}313{,}400 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01810 \approx 0.1902$$

**Step 3 — Probability of at most 2 defectives:**

$$P(X \le 2) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) + P(X=2)$$

$$P(X=0) = (0.98)^{200} \approx 0.0176$$

$$P(X=1) = 200 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{199} \approx 0.0718$$

$$P(X=2) = \binom{200}{2}(0.02)^{2}(0.98)^{198} \approx 0.1459$$

$$P(X \le 2) \approx 0.0176 + 0.0718 + 0.1459 \approx 0.2353$$

**Step 4 — Expected number of defectives:**

$$E[X] = np = 4$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{P(X=3) \approx 0.1902, \quad P(X \le 2) \approx 0.2353, \quad E[X] = 4}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Exponential distribution** as the continuous analogue of the Poisson distribution. Derive its **PDF, mean, and variance** from the relationship with the Poisson rate $\lambda$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The lifetime of a certain electronic component (in hours) follows an Exponential distribution with mean lifetime $1/\lambda = 1000$ hours. Compute:
> 1. The probability that the component **survives beyond 1500 hours**.
> 2. The probability that it **fails within the first 500 hours**.
> 3. The standard deviation of the lifetime.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Connection to Poisson:** If events occur according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ events per unit time, then the *waiting time* until the first event follows an Exponential distribution with the same rate $\lambda$.

**Derivation of PDF:** Let $T$ be the waiting time. The event "no event occurs in time $t$" for a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ is:

$$P(T > t) = P(\text{0 events in } t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^{0} e^{-\lambda t}}{0!} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The CDF is therefore:

$$F(t) = P(T \le t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

Differentiating gives the PDF:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

**Mean (from Section 3.3):**

$$E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Variance (similar derivation):**

$$E[T^{2}] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2} \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}}$$

$$\text{Var}(T) = E[T^{2}] - (E[T])^{2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \mu = \frac{1}{\lambda}, \quad \sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** Mean $= 1/\lambda = 1000 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0.001$ per hour.

**Step 1 — Probability of surviving beyond 1500 hours:**

The survival function is $P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$P(T > 1500) = e^{-0.001 \times 1500} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

**Step 2 — Probability of failing within 500 hours:**

$$P(T \le 500) = 1 - e^{-0.001 \times 500} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \approx 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

**Step 3 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

(Note: For an Exponential distribution, the **mean equals the standard deviation**.)

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{P(T > 1500) \approx 0.2231, \quad P(T \le 500) \approx 0.3935, \quad \sigma = 1000 \text{ hours}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `scale` with `variance`:** In `scipy.stats`, `scale = σ` (standard deviation), NOT $\sigma^{2}$. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint $\sigma > 0$:** The scale argument of a continuous distribution must be strictly positive. Examiners will deduct marks if you write `scale = 0`.
> 3. **Mixing up `loc`:** Some distributions (like `expon`) have `loc` defaulting to $0$. If a question says "shift by 5", you must explicitly set `loc = 5` — do not just add 5 to the sample.
> 4. **Poisson vs Exponential confusion:** Poisson is *discrete* (counts events); Exponential is *continuous* (measures time-between-events). They share the same $\lambda$, but represent different things.
> 5. **For $\text{Binomial}(n, p)$, the variance is $np(1-p)$, NOT $np$:** This is a classic 1-mark deduction mistake.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Type = Family; Arguments = Specific member.** Always state both when answering "identify the distribution" questions.
- **The two universal arguments** for continuous distributions are **`loc`** (horizontal shift) and **`scale`** (horizontal stretch).
- **Poisson & Exponential are linked by the same $\lambda$:** Poisson counts *events*; Exponential measures *time-between-events*.
- **Normal distribution:** $\text{Type} = \text{norm}$, $\text{Arguments} = (\mu, \sigma)$ → `loc=μ, scale=σ`.
- **Binomial:** $\text{Type} = \text{binom}$, $\text{Arguments} = (n, p)$. Mean $= np$, Variance $= np(1-p)$.
- **Poisson:** $\text{Type} = \text{poisson}$, $\text{Argument} = \lambda$. Mean $=$ Variance $= \lambda$.
- **Exponential:** $\text{Type} = \text{expon}$, $\text{Argument} = \lambda$ (rate) or `scale = 1/λ`. Mean $=$ SD $= 1/\lambda$.
- **Uniform (Continuous):** $\text{Type} = \text{uniform}$, $\text{Arguments} = (a, b)$ → `loc=a, scale=b-a`. Mean $= (a+b)/2$.
- **Bernoulli is Binomial with $n=1$:** A 1-trial binomial.
- **CLT ⇒ Normal is "universal"** for sums of i.i.d. variables.
- **Always check support:** Poisson/Exponential on $[0, \infty)$; Normal on $(-\infty, \infty)$; Uniform on $[a,b]$.
- **Don't confuse $\sigma$ with $\sigma^{2}$** in `scipy.stats.norm(scale=σ)`.
- **Use the decision matrix (Section 3.6)** to instantly identify which distribution a problem demands.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked *"What are the type arguments?"*, write the answer in the form **`<distribution_name>(<arg1> = ..., <arg2> = ...)`** — this is the precise format examiners expect and prevents partial-mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Probability Distribution?

A **Probability Distribution** is a mathematical function (or table) that describes all the possible values and likelihoods that a discrete or continuous random variable can take within a given sample space. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Data Handling and Visualization*, probability distributions form the statistical backbone for predictive modeling, hypothesis testing, and data-driven decision making.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** A probability distribution is a mapping from every possible outcome of a random experiment to its corresponding probability of occurrence, such that all probabilities are non-negative and sum (or integrate) to **1**.

## 1.2 Two Fundamental Families

| Family | Variable Type | Examples in KTU Syllabus |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete Probability Distributions** | Countable outcomes | Binomial, Poisson, Bernoulli |
| **Continuous Probability Distributions** | Uncountable (interval) outcomes | Normal, Exponential, Uniform |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tossing **3 fair coins** in your classroom. You cannot predict *exactly* which sequence (HHH, HHT, ...) will appear, but you *can* say with certainty that the chance of getting exactly 2 Heads is higher than the chance of getting 3 Heads. A probability distribution is essentially the **"official scorecard"** that ranks every possible outcome by its likelihood of showing up. Think of it as a weather forecast model — it does not tell you the exact temperature tomorrow, but it tells you the *probability* of rain, sunshine, or snow based on historical patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU Examinations:** Questions on Probability Distributions are high-weightage (14-mark Part B questions frequently test Binomial and Normal). You must be fluent with formulas, mean–variance relationships, and approximations (e.g., Poisson as a limiting case of Binomial).

## 1.4 The Random Variable — Foundation of Every Distribution

A **Random Variable (RV)** is a numerical quantity whose value depends on the outcome of a random experiment.

$$
X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}
$$

- **Discrete RV:** Takes countable values (e.g., number of defective items in a batch).
- **Continuous RV:** Takes uncountably infinite values over an interval (e.g., waiting time, height).

The *distribution* specifies the probability structure of this random variable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bernoulli Distribution (Building Block)

The simplest discrete distribution models a single trial with two outcomes: **Success (1)** with probability $p$, and **Failure (0)** with probability $q = 1 - p$.

$$
P(X = x) = p^x (1-p)^{1-x}, \quad x \in \{0, 1\}
$$

**Mean:** $\mu = p$  
**Variance:** $\sigma^2 = pq$

> [!NOTE]
> The Bernoulli distribution is the *atomic unit* out of which the Binomial distribution is constructed. KTU examiners love testing this conceptual link.

## 2.2 The Binomial Distribution

A **Binomial Distribution** models the number of successes $X$ in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials, each with the same success probability $p$.

**Assumptions (Mandatory for full marks in KTU):**
1. Fixed number of trials: $n$ is finite and pre-determined.
2. Each trial has only two outcomes: Success / Failure.
3. Trials are **independent**.
4. Probability of success $p$ is **constant** across all trials.

$$
P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

where $\binom{n}{x} = \dfrac{n!}{x! \, (n-x)!}$ and $q = 1 - p$.

**Mean:** $\mu = np$  
**Variance:** $\sigma^2 = npq$  
**Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Discrete Distributions

| Distribution | PMF / PDF | Mean $\mu$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ | KTU Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli ($n=1$) | $p^x q^{1-x}$ | $p$ | $pq$ | $0 \le p \le 1$ |
| Binomial ($n, p$) | $\binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$ | $np$ | $npq$ | $x = 0, 1, \ldots, n$ |
| Poisson ($\lambda$) | $\dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | $x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ |
| Uniform $(a, b)$ | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | $a \le x \le b$ |
| Exponential ($\lambda$) | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | $x \ge 0$ |
| Normal ($\mu, \sigma^2$) | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $-\infty < x < \infty$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL RULE — Markdown Table Safety:** Whenever a vertical bar occurs inside a math expression (such as $\vert x - \mu \vert$), I have used the LaTeX command `\vert` or wrapped the expression in display math `$$` outside the table cell to prevent the markdown parser from breaking the table structure.

## 2.3 The Poisson Distribution

The **Poisson Distribution** models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space, when events occur independently and at a constant average rate $\lambda$.

$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

**Mean = Variance = $\lambda$** (a unique and highly testable property).

> [!NOTE]
> **Poisson as a Limiting Case of Binomial:** When $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, but $np = \lambda$ remains constant, the Binomial distribution converges to the Poisson distribution. This is a frequent 7-mark KTU sub-question.

## 2.4 The Normal (Gaussian) Distribution

The **Normal Distribution** is the most important continuous distribution in statistics, characterized by its symmetric bell-shaped curve.

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad -\infty < x < \infty
$$

### The Empirical 68-95-99.7 Rule (Highly Testable!)

| Range | Probability of Lying Within |
|---|---|
| $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ | **68.27 %** |
| $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ | **95.45 %** |
| $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | **99.73 %** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control:** Binomial models proportion of defectives in manufacturing batches.
- **Telecommunications:** Poisson models call arrivals per minute at a switchboard.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Exponential models component lifetimes and time-to-failure.
- **Measurement Errors:** Normal distribution models sensor noise and tolerances.
- **Simulation & Data Visualization:** Distributions underpin Monte Carlo methods, used heavily in predictive dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Binomial Distribution

We treat $X$ as the sum of $n$ independent Bernoulli trials:
$$
X = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n, \quad \text{where each } X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)
$$

### Step 1 — Compute $E(X)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= E(X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n) \\
&= E(X_1) + E(X_2) + \cdots + E(X_n) \\
&= p + p + \cdots + p \quad (n \text{ times}) \\
&= np
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $E(X^2)$ Using Variance Identity

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= E\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right)^2\right] \\
&= E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i^2 + 2\sum_{i < j} X_i X_j\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $X_i$ is Bernoulli, $X_i^2 = X_i$, and because of independence $E(X_i X_j) = E(X_i) E(X_j) = p^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= np + 2 \binom{n}{2} p^2 = np + n(n-1)p^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Variance Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \\
&= np + n(n-1)p^2 - (np)^2 \\
&= np + n^2 p^2 - np^2 - n^2 p^2 \\
&= np - np^2 \\
&= np(1-p) \\
&= npq
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The identity $E(X^2) = np + n(n-1)p^2$ is derived by expanding the sum, separating the diagonal terms ($X_i^2 = X_i$) from the off-diagonal cross-terms, and applying independence. This step is what examiners look for in 14-mark derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Poisson Distribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!} \\
&= e^{-\lambda} \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^x}{(x-1)!} \\
&= e^{-\lambda} \lambda \sum_{y=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^y}{y!} \\
&= e^{-\lambda} \lambda \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, $E(X^2) = \lambda^2 + \lambda$, hence:
$$
\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = (\lambda^2 + \lambda) - \lambda^2 = \lambda
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Binomial (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** In a batch of 10 components, the probability that any component is defective is **0.1**. Find the probability that exactly 2 components are defective. Also compute the mean and variance.

**Given:** $n = 10$, $p = 0.1$, $q = 0.9$, $x = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 2) &= \binom{10}{2} (0.1)^2 (0.9)^{8} \\
&= 45 \times 0.01 \times 0.43046721 \\
&= 0.1937
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu = np = 10 \times 0.1 = 1.0
$$
$$
\sigma^2 = npq = 10 \times 0.1 \times 0.9 = 0.9
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Poisson Approximation

**Problem:** On average, **3 customers** arrive per minute at a billing counter. Find the probability that exactly 5 customers arrive in a given minute.

**Given:** $\lambda = 3$, $x = 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 5) &= \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^5}{5!} \\
&= \frac{0.049787 \times 243}{120} \\
&= \frac{12.098}{120} \approx 0.1008
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Normal Distribution Standardization

**Problem:** The marks in a KTU exam follow $N(\mu = 60, \sigma^2 = 100)$. Find the probability that a randomly selected student scored above **75**.

**Step 1 — Standardize to Z:**

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{75 - 60}{10} = 1.5
$$

**Step 2 — Read from Standard Normal Table:**

$$
P(Z \le 1.5) = 0.9332
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Required Probability:**

$$
P(X > 75) = 1 - P(Z \le 1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Binomial distribution. State its mean and variance.**

**Model Answer:**
A Binomial distribution gives the probability of obtaining exactly $x$ successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials, each with constant success probability $p$.

$$
P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, \ldots, n
$$

**Mean:** $\mu = np$  
**Variance:** $\sigma^2 = np(1-p)$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State any two properties of the Normal distribution.**

**Model Answer:**
1. The Normal curve is **symmetric** and **bell-shaped** about the mean $\mu$.
2. Mean, median, and mode are all equal and coincide at $\mu$.
3. The total area under the curve equals **1**.
4. It is fully characterized by two parameters: $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
5. It obeys the empirical rule: **68-95-99.7 %** within 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of the Binomial distribution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a factory, the probability that a manufactured item is defective is **0.05**. If a sample of **20 items** is selected, find:
1. The probability that exactly 2 items are defective.
2. The probability that at most 1 item is defective.
3. Mean and standard deviation of the distribution. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

Let $X$ be the number of successes in $n$ trials. Define $X = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n$, where each $X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$.

**Mean Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= E\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = np
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) \quad (\text{independence}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(1-p) = np(1-p)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Detailed expansion using $E(X^2) = np + n(n-1)p^2$ was shown in Section 3.1.)

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Given:** $n = 20$, $p = 0.05$, $q = 0.95$.

**(i) Probability of exactly 2 defectives:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 2) &= \binom{20}{2} (0.05)^2 (0.95)^{18} \\
&= 190 \times 0.0025 \times 0.3972 \\
&\approx 0.1887
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Probability of at most 1 defective:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \le 1) &= P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) \\
&= (0.95)^{20} + 20 \cdot (0.05)(0.95)^{19} \\
&= 0.3585 + 0.3774 \\
&\approx 0.7359
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mean and Standard Deviation:**

$$
\mu = np = 20 \times 0.05 = 1.0
$$
$$
\sigma = \sqrt{npq} = \sqrt{20 \times 0.05 \times 0.95} = \sqrt{0.95} \approx 0.9747
$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Define the Poisson distribution. Prove that its mean equals its variance $\lambda$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The number of calls received at a switchboard follows a Poisson distribution with mean **4 per minute**.
1. Find the probability of receiving exactly 6 calls in a minute.
2. Find the probability of receiving at most 2 calls in a minute.
3. Using the Poisson approximation to the Binomial, verify for a case where $n = 100$, $p = 0.04$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Definition:** The Poisson distribution models the number of events $X$ occurring in a fixed interval, when events happen independently at a constant average rate $\lambda$.

$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

**Proof that Mean = Variance = $\lambda$:**

Mean:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!} \\
&= e^{-\lambda} \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^x}{(x-1)!} \\
&= \lambda e^{-\lambda} \sum_{y=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^y}{y!} = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Variance: First compute $E(X^2)$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X(X-1)) &= \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x(x-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!} = \lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $E(X^2) = E(X(X-1)) + E(X) = \lambda^2 + \lambda$, giving $\text{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 + \lambda - \lambda^2 = \lambda$.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:** $\lambda = 4$.

**(i) $P(X = 6)$:**

$$
P(X = 6) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^6}{6!} = \frac{0.01832 \times 4096}{720} \approx 0.1042
$$

**(ii) $P(X \le 2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \le 2) &= e^{-4}\left[1 + 4 + \frac{4^2}{2!}\right] \\
&= 0.01832 \times (1 + 4 + 8) \\
&= 0.01832 \times 13 \approx 0.2381
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Poisson Approximation Check:** With $n = 100$, $p = 0.04$, we have $\lambda = np = 4$.

For $x = 3$:
$$
\text{Binomial: } \binom{100}{3}(0.04)^3(0.96)^{97} \approx 0.1991
$$
$$
\text{Poisson: } \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^3}{3!} = \frac{0.01832 \times 64}{6} \approx 0.1954
$$

The values are nearly identical, confirming Poisson as a valid Binomial approximation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Probability Distribution Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant $p$:** Many students write $P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$ but omit $q = 1 - p$ explicitly — losing **1 mark** immediately.
> 2. **Standard Normal Confusion:** In Normal distribution problems, students compute $P(X > a)$ as $P(Z > a)$ without first converting to $Z$. Always show the standardization step $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$.
> 3. **Poisson Range Error:** Poisson is defined for $x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ to infinity. Some students truncate the range, losing conceptual marks.
> 4. **Variance of Sum vs Sum of Variances:** For independent RVs only is $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$. Examiners check this assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Mean of Binomial:** Stating "mean = $p$" instead of "$np$" is one of the most frequent errors — costs a full **mark** in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **probability distribution** maps every outcome of a random experiment to its probability; total probability always equals **1**.
- **Binomial** requires four conditions: fixed $n$, two outcomes, independence, constant $p$. PMF: $\binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$. Mean $= np$, Variance $= npq$.
- **Poisson** is a limiting case of Binomial when $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, and $np = \lambda$ remains finite. Mean = Variance = $\lambda$.
- **Normal distribution** is symmetric, bell-shaped, fully defined by $\mu$ and $\sigma$. Use standardization $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ before consulting tables.
- **Empirical Rule:** 68-95-99.7 % of observations lie within 1, 2, 3 standard deviations of the mean.
- **Exponential distribution** has the **memoryless property**: $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$. Mean $= 1/\lambda$, Variance $= 1/\lambda^2$.
- **Uniform distribution** has constant density $1/(b-a)$ over $[a, b]$. Mean $= (a+b)/2$, Variance $= (b-a)^2/12$.
- Always **state the assumptions** of any distribution before applying it — this is what differentiates a full-mark answer from an average one in KTU evaluation.
- For Poisson approximation to Binomial, ensure $n$ is large ($> 50$) and $p$ is small ($< 0.1$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT695 - DATA HANDLING AND VISUALIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Normal Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Distribution (Gaussian Distribution):** A continuous probability distribution for a random variable $X$ whose probability density function (PDF) is symmetric and bell-shaped, characterized entirely by two parameters — the **mean** $\mu$ and the **variance** $\sigma^2$. It is denoted as:
> $$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of a normal random variable is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

where:
- $\mu$ = population **mean** (location parameter, determines center)
- $\sigma^2$ = population **variance** (scale parameter, determines spread)
- $\sigma$ = population **standard deviation**
- The constants $1$ and $2\pi$ in the denominator are normalization factors ensuring $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bell Curve" Intuition:** Imagine a classroom of 1,000 students taking an exam. A few students score very low, a few score very high, but the **majority cluster around the average score**. Plot their scores and you get a smooth, symmetric bell shape — the Normal Distribution.

**Everyday Examples of Normal Distributions:**
- **Heights** of adult males in a large population
- **Measurement errors** in scientific experiments
- **IQ scores** standardized around a mean of 100
- **Blood pressure** readings in a healthy population
- **Manufacturing tolerances** of mass-produced components (e.g., bolt diameters)

### 1.3 Why It Is Called the "Gaussian" Distribution

The distribution is named after the German mathematician **Carl Friedrich Gauss (1777–1855)**, who used it to model errors in astronomical observations. Its prominence in probability theory stems from the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**: the sum (or average) of a large number of independent, identically distributed random variables tends toward a normal distribution, regardless of the original distribution's shape.

### 1.4 Standard Normal Distribution (Special Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma^2 = 1$, the distribution is called the **Standard Normal Distribution**, denoted $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Its PDF simplifies to:
> $$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right)$$
> Every normal random variable can be converted to $Z$ via the **standardization transformation**:
> $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the PDF

The PDF formula $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ has four logical components, each with a specific role:

| Component | Mathematical Form | Functional Role |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Normalization constant** | $\frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}}$ | Scales the curve so the total area under it equals **1** (axiom of probability) |
| **Exponential decay** | $e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Ensures values far from $\mu$ have rapidly diminishing probability density |
| **Quadratic numerator** | $(x - \mu)^2$ | Creates symmetry about $x = \mu$ (squared distance from mean) |
| **Variance scaling** | $2\sigma^2$ in denominator | Larger $\sigma$ flattens and widens the curve; smaller $\sigma$ sharpens and narrows it |

### 2.2 Key Properties of the Normal Distribution

1. **Symmetry:** The PDF is perfectly symmetric about $x = \mu$ (i.e., the line $x = \mu$ is the axis of symmetry).
2. **Unimodality:** It has exactly **one peak (mode)**, located at $x = \mu$.
3. **Central Tendency Equality:** For a normal distribution, **mean = median = mode = $\mu$**.
4. **Asymptotic Tails:** The curve approaches the horizontal axis as $x \to \pm\infty$ but never touches it.
5. **Total Probability:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ (the entire area under the curve equals 1).
6. **Inflection Points:** The curve changes concavity at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$ (i.e., exactly one standard deviation from the mean).
7. **Linear Transformation:** If $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then for any constants $a, b$: $aX + b \sim \mathcal{N}(a\mu + b,\, a^2 \sigma^2)$.
8. **Reproducibility under Summation:** If $X_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $X_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ are independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1 + \mu_2,\, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$.

### 2.3 The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any normal distribution, approximately:
> - **68.27%** of data lies within $\mu \pm \sigma$
> - **95.45%** of data lies within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$
> - **99.73%** of data lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$

This empirical regularity is the cornerstone of statistical quality control and Six Sigma methodology in engineering.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | General PDF | $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Density of $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ |
| 2 | Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$ | Density of $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ |
| 3 | Standardization | $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converts any $X$ to standard $Z$ |
| 4 | Mean of $Z$ | $E[Z] = 0$ | Centered at origin |
| 5 | Variance of $Z$ | $\text{Var}(Z) = 1$ | Unit variance |
| 6 | CDF Notation | $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$ | Cumulative probability up to $x$ |
| 7 | Probability interval | $P(a < X < b) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Use standard normal table $\Phi(\cdot)$ |
| 8 | Symmetry identity | $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ | Standard normal table lookup |
| 9 | Empirical 1-$\sigma$ | $P(\mu-\sigma < X < \mu+\sigma) \approx 0.6826$ | $\approx 68.27\%$ |
| 10 | Empirical 2-$\sigma$ | $P(\mu-2\sigma < X < \mu+2\sigma) \approx 0.9544$ | $\approx 95.45\%$ |
| 11 | Empirical 3-$\sigma$ | $P(\mu-3\sigma < X < \mu+3\sigma) \approx 0.9974$ | $\approx 99.73\%$ |
| 12 | Linear transform | $Y = aX + b \Rightarrow Y \sim \mathcal{N}(a\mu+b, a^2\sigma^2)$ | Preserves normality |
| 13 | Sum of independents | $X_1 + X_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1+\mu_2, \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$ | Variances add |
| 14 | Difference of independents | $X_1 - X_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1-\mu_2, \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$ | Variances still add |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The normal distribution is foundational in **process control (SPC charts)**, **reliability engineering**, **signal processing noise modeling**, **Six Sigma quality programs**, and **hypothesis testing** in virtually every engineering discipline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Standardization Produces $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$

**Given:** $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and the transformation $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$.

**Goal:** Prove that $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

#### Step 1: Compute the Mean of $Z$

$$E[Z] = E\!\left[\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right]$$

$$E[Z] = \frac{1}{\sigma} E[X - \mu]$$

$$E[Z] = \frac{1}{\sigma}\big(E[X] - \mu\big)$$

$$E[Z] = \frac{1}{\sigma}(\mu - \mu) = 0$$

*(The constant $\mu$ and the linear scaling factor $\frac{1}{\sigma}$ are pulled out using linearity of expectation.)*

#### Step 2: Compute the Variance of $Z$

$$\text{Var}(Z) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

$$\text{Var}(Z) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\text{Var}(X - \mu)$$

*(Variance of a constant is zero, and $\text{Var}(cX) = c^2 \text{Var}(X)$ for constant $c = \tfrac{1}{\sigma}$.)*

$$\text{Var}(Z) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \cdot \sigma^2 = 1$$

#### Step 3: Confirm Normality Is Preserved

A **linear transformation** of a normal random variable yields another normal random variable. Formally, if $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and $Y = aX + b$, then $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$.

Here, $Z = \frac{1}{\sigma}X + \left(-\frac{\mu}{\sigma}\right)$, i.e., $a = \frac{1}{\sigma}$ and $b = -\frac{\mu}{\sigma}$.

#### Step 4: Combine the Results

$$E[Z] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(Z) = 1, \quad \text{and } Z \text{ is normal}$$

Therefore, by definition:

$$Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Engineering Quality Control Problem

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]:**  
> A machine fills bottles with a nominal volume of **500 mL**. The actual volume follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 500$ mL and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$ mL.  
> **(a)** Find the probability that a randomly selected bottle contains **less than 485 mL**.  
> **(b)** Find the probability that a randomly selected bottle contains **between 490 mL and 515 mL**.  
> **(c)** Find the value $L$ such that $P(X < L) = 0.95$.

---

#### Part (a) Solution: $P(X < 485)$

**Step 1:** Standardize the boundary value.

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{485 - 500}{10} = \frac{-15}{10} = -1.5$$

**Step 2:** Express the probability in terms of $Z$.

$$P(X < 485) = P\!\left(Z < -1.5\right)$$

**Step 3:** Use the standard normal table. From tables, $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$.

**Step 4:** Apply the symmetry identity $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.

$$P(Z < -1.5) = 1 - \Phi(1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668$$

**Result:** $P(X < 485) \approx \mathbf{0.0668}$ or **6.68%**.

---

#### Part (b) Solution: $P(490 < X < 515)$

**Step 1:** Standardize both boundaries.

$$Z_1 = \frac{490 - 500}{10} = \frac{-10}{10} = -1.0$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{515 - 500}{10} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.5$$

**Step 2:** Express probability in terms of $Z$.

$$P(490 < X < 515) = P(-1.0 < Z < 1.5)$$

**Step 3:** Use the difference of CDFs.

$$P(-1.0 < Z < 1.5) = \Phi(1.5) - \Phi(-1.0)$$

**Step 4:** Look up tables: $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$, $\Phi(-1.0) = 1 - \Phi(1.0) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$.

$$P(-1.0 < Z < 1.5) = 0.9332 - 0.1587 = 0.7745$$

**Result:** $P(490 < X < 515) \approx \mathbf{0.7745}$ or **77.45%**.

---

#### Part (c) Solution: Find $L$ such that $P(X < L) = 0.95$

**Step 1:** Translate to standard normal. If $P(X < L) = 0.95$, then standardizing:

$$P\!\left(Z < \frac{L - 500}{10}\right) = 0.95$$

**Step 2:** Identify the required $z$-value. From standard normal tables, $\Phi(1.645) = 0.9500$. So:

$$\frac{L - 500}{10} = 1.645$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $L$.

$$L - 500 = 1.645 \times 10 = 16.45$$

$$L = 500 + 16.45 = 516.45 \text{ mL}$$

**Result:** $L \approx \mathbf{516.45}$ **mL** (the 95th percentile of the bottle volume distribution).

---

### 3.3 Verification via the Empirical Rule

For this problem, $\mu = 500$ and $\sigma = 10$:

| Interval | Range (mL) | Empirical Probability |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| $\mu \pm \sigma$ | 490 to 510 | $\approx 68.27\%$ |
| $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ | 480 to 520 | $\approx 95.45\%$ |
| $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | 470 to 530 | $\approx 99.73\%$ |

Our Part (b) answer of 77.45% lies **between** the 1-$\sigma$ and 2-$\sigma$ empirical values, which is consistent and a useful sanity check. ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Normal Distribution Analysis Workflow

The following block diagram maps the complete functional flow from raw problem data to a final probability answer, illustrating how each component of the normal distribution methodology interacts with the others.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Step 1 Identify Problem Data] --> nodeB[Step 2 Extract Mean Mu and SD Sigma]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3 Define Random Variable X follows N of Mu Sigma Squared]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Step 4 Identify Probability Type}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Case A P of X less than a]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Case B P of X greater than a]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Case C P of a less than X less than b]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Case D Find X for given P]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Step 5 Apply Standardization Z equals X minus Mu over Sigma]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 6 Reference Standard Normal Table for Phi of z]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 7 Apply Symmetry Identity Phi of minus z equals one minus Phi of z]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Step 8 Compute Final Probability or Solve for Unknown]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Step 9 Verify via Empirical Rule and Sanity Check]
    nodeM --> endN[Final Answer P equals value or X equals value]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Key Properties and Transformations of Normal Distribution

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1[Input X follows N of Mu Sigma Squared] --> p2[Property 1 Symmetric about Mu]
    p1 --> p3[Property 2 Mean equals Median equals Mode equals Mu]
    p1 --> p4[Property 3 Inflection at Mu plus or minus Sigma]
    p1 --> p5[Property 4 Total Area equals One]
    p1 --> p6[Transformation Apply Z equals X minus Mu over Sigma]
    p6 --> p7[Output Z follows N of Zero One]
    p7 --> p8[Reference Standard Normal Phi Table]
    p8 --> p9[Compute Probabilities for any Interval]
    p2 --> p9
    p3 --> p9
    p4 --> p9
    p5 --> p9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The top diagram depicts the sequential workflow for solving any normal distribution problem (a **process flow**). The bottom diagram depicts the structural relationships between the **properties** and the **transformation mechanism** (a **concept map**). Together, they form a complete mental model of how raw data enters the system and emerges as a probabilistic interpretation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define the Normal Distribution. Write its probability density function and state any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** A continuous probability distribution is called a *Normal (or Gaussian) Distribution* if its probability density function is bell-shaped and symmetric about its mean. A random variable $X$ is said to follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ if its PDF is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

It is denoted as $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Four Properties (any 4):**
1. The curve is **symmetric** about the mean $x = \mu$.
2. The **mean, median, and mode** are all equal to $\mu$.
3. The total area under the curve is **1** (i.e., $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$).
4. The curve has **inflection points** at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$.
5. The **tails** approach but never touch the horizontal axis.

*(Full marks: 1 for definition + PDF, 2 marks distributed across the four properties.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**State the Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 rule) for a normal distribution. Illustrate with a sketch of the bell curve showing the three intervals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For any normal distribution $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the Empirical Rule states:

1. Approximately **68.27%** of the observations lie within one standard deviation of the mean, i.e., in the interval $(\mu - \sigma,\, \mu + \sigma)$.
2. Approximately **95.45%** of the observations lie within two standard deviations of the mean, i.e., in the interval $(\mu - 2\sigma,\, \mu + 2\sigma)$.
3. Approximately **99.73%** of the observations lie within three standard deviations of the mean, i.e., in the interval $(\mu - 3\sigma,\, \mu + 3\sigma)$.

**Sketch (describe):**
- A symmetric bell curve centered at $\mu$.
- Three nested horizontal bands at heights corresponding to $\mu \pm \sigma$ (innermost), $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ (middle), and $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (outermost), labelled with their respective percentages: 68.27%, 95.45%, 99.73%.

*(Full marks: 1 mark each for the three statements, plus partial credit for the sketch description.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** (theoretical/derivation) and **Part (b) — 7 marks** (applied numerical problem). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
Derive the standardization formula $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ and prove that if $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of the transformation:** Define the standard normal variable as

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

This is a linear transformation of $X$ of the form $Z = aX + b$ with $a = \frac{1}{\sigma}$ and $b = -\frac{\mu}{\sigma}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean of $Z$:** Using linearity of expectation,

$$E[Z] = E\!\left[\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma}\big(E[X] - \mu\big) = \frac{1}{\sigma}(\mu - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance of $Z$:** Using the property $\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$,

$$\text{Var}(Z) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\text{Var}(X - \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\,\text{Var}(X) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Normality preservation:** Any linear transformation of a normal random variable is also normally distributed. Hence $Z$ is normal.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Combining the mean ($0$), variance ($1$), and normality,

$$Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \qquad \blacksquare$$

*(Mark distribution: 1 for defining the transformation, 2 for mean, 2 for variance, 1 for normality argument, 1 for final conclusion.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
The lifetime (in hours) of a brand of electric bulbs follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 1200$ hours and standard deviation $\sigma = 100$ hours. Find:  
**(i)** The probability that a randomly selected bulb lasts **more than 1350 hours**.  
**(ii)** The probability that a randomly selected bulb lasts **between 1100 and 1300 hours**.  
**(iii)** The lifetime $L$ such that **90% of bulbs last at most $L$ hours**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 1200$ hours, $\sigma = 100$ hours, $X \sim \mathcal{N}(1200, 100^2)$.

#### (i) $P(X > 1350)$

Standardize: $Z = \dfrac{1350 - 1200}{100} = \dfrac{150}{100} = 1.5$

$$P(X > 1350) = P(Z > 1.5) = 1 - \Phi(1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668$$

**Answer:** $P(X > 1350) = \mathbf{0.0668}$ (about 6.68% of bulbs).

#### (ii) $P(1100 < X < 1300)$

Standardize both boundaries:

$$Z_1 = \frac{1100 - 1200}{100} = -1.0, \qquad Z_2 = \frac{1300 - 1200}{100} = 1.0$$

$$P(1100 < X < 1300) = P(-1.0 < Z < 1.0) = \Phi(1.0) - \Phi(-1.0)$$

Using $\Phi(-1.0) = 1 - \Phi(1.0)$:

$$= \Phi(1.0) - (1 - \Phi(1.0)) = 2\Phi(1.0) - 1 = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826$$

**Answer:** $P(1100 < X < 1300) = \mathbf{0.6826}$ (about 68.26% — consistent with the empirical 1-$\sigma$ rule ✓).

#### (iii) Find $L$ such that $P(X < L) = 0.90$

Standardize:

$$P\!\left(Z < \frac{L - 1200}{100}\right) = 0.90$$

From standard normal tables, $\Phi(1.28) = 0.8997 \approx 0.90$, so the required $z$-value is $z = 1.28$ (more precisely $z = 1.282$).

$$\frac{L - 1200}{100} = 1.28$$

$$L = 1200 + 1.28 \times 100 = 1200 + 128 = 1328 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** $L \approx \mathbf{1328}$ **hours** (the 90th percentile of bulb lifetime).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
State and explain any **six important properties** of the normal distribution. Why is it the most widely used distribution in statistical inference?

**Model Solution:**

**Six Key Properties:**

1. **Bell-shaped and symmetric:** The curve is symmetric about the vertical line $x = \mu$, meaning the left and right halves are mirror images.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode:** All three measures of central tendency coincide at $\mu$, a unique feature of this distribution.
3. **Unimodal:** It has exactly one peak, located at $x = \mu$.
4. **Asymptotic tails:** As $x \to \pm\infty$, $f(x) \to 0$, but the curve never actually touches the horizontal axis.
5. **Total area equals 1:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$, satisfying the probability axiom.
6. **Inflection points at $\mu \pm \sigma$:** The curvature changes sign exactly one standard deviation away from the mean.
7. *(Bonus)* **Family of distributions:** Different $(\mu, \sigma)$ pairs produce infinitely many normal curves, all of the same essential shape.

**Why It Is Most Widely Used:**

- **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** The sum or average of a large number of independent random variables — regardless of their individual distributions — tends to follow a normal distribution. This makes the normal distribution a universal model for aggregate phenomena.
- **Mathematical tractability:** Its PDF is differentiable, integrable in closed form, and has elegant transformation properties.
- **Empirical prevalence:** Many natural and engineering phenomena (measurement errors, biological metrics, manufacturing variations) empirically follow a normal pattern.
- **Foundation of inference:** Procedures such as $Z$-tests, $t$-tests (asymptotically), confidence intervals, and regression are all built upon normality assumptions.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark each for the six properties, 1 mark for the "why" justification.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
In a manufacturing process, the diameter of shafts produced follows $X \sim \mathcal{N}(25\text{ mm},\, 0.09\text{ mm}^2)$. Specifications require the diameter to be between **24.7 mm and 25.3 mm**.  
**(i)** What proportion of shafts meet the specification?  
**(ii)** If the process produces 5,000 shafts per day, how many defective shafts are produced daily?  
**(iii)** To reduce defects, the variance is halved (i.e., new $\sigma^2 = 0.045$). Recalculate the proportion meeting specification.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 25$ mm, $\sigma = \sqrt{0.09} = 0.3$ mm.

#### (i) $P(24.7 < X < 25.3)$

Standardize both boundaries:

$$Z_1 = \frac{24.7 - 25}{0.3} = \frac{-0.3}{0.3} = -1.0$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{25.3 - 25}{0.3} = \frac{0.3}{0.3} = 1.0$$

$$P(24.7 < X < 25.3) = P(-1.0 < Z < 1.0) = 2\Phi(1.0) - 1 = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826$$

**Answer:** Proportion meeting specification $= \mathbf{0.6826}$ (about 68.26%).

#### (ii) Defective shafts per day

Proportion defective $= 1 - 0.6826 = 0.3174$.

Daily defective count $= 0.3174 \times 5000 = \mathbf{1587}$ **shafts per day**.

#### (iii) New $\sigma$ after variance halving

New variance: $\sigma^2 = 0.045$, so new $\sigma = \sqrt{0.045} \approx 0.2121$ mm.

Re-standardize (note that $\mu$ is unchanged):

$$Z_1 = \frac{24.7 - 25}{0.2121} = \frac{-0.3}{0.2121} \approx -1.414$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{25.3 - 25}{0.2121} = \frac{0.3}{0.2121} \approx 1.414$$

$$P(-1.414 < Z < 1.414) = 2\Phi(1.414) - 1$$

From tables, $\Phi(1.41) \approx 0.9207$ and $\Phi(1.42) \approx 0.9222$. Interpolating at $z = 1.414$:

$$\Phi(1.414) \approx 0.9213$$

$$P(-1.414 < Z < 1.414) = 2(0.9213) - 1 = 0.8426$$

**Answer:** New proportion meeting specification $\approx \mathbf{0.8426}$ (84.26%, an increase of about 16 percentage points — a direct illustration of process-improvement value).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Normal Distribution Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize:** Many students directly substitute values into the original PDF $f(x)$ and try to "integrate" — this is a guaranteed zero. **Always convert to $Z$ first** using $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$.
> 2. **Sign errors in $Z$ calculation:** The formula is $(X - \mu)/\sigma$, not $(\mu - X)/\sigma$. Double-check the sign.
> 3. **Misuse of the symmetry identity:** Remember $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$, **not** $\Phi(-z) = \Phi(z)$.
> 4. **Forgetting the square root for $\sigma$:** When the problem gives variance $\sigma^2$, you must take $\sqrt{\sigma^2}$ to get $\sigma$ before standardizing. E.g., $\sigma^2 = 0.09 \Rightarrow \sigma = 0.3$.
> 5. **Incorrect z-value lookup:** Always use the row-column intersection correctly in the standard normal table. For $z = 1.50$, the row is "1.5" and the column is "0.00".
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Engineering exam answers should always carry units (e.g., "mL", "hours", "mm").
> 7. **No verification step:** Examiners reward a quick sanity check using the empirical rule (68-95-99.7) — even one sentence earns partial credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Normal Distributions**

- **Definition:** $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is a continuous, bell-shaped, symmetric distribution fully described by its mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.
- **PDF formula:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ — must commit to memory.
- **Standardization is the key operation:** $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ converts any normal to $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$.
- **Standard normal table values to memorize (commonly tested):** $\Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$, $\Phi(1.50) = 0.9332$, $\Phi(1.96) = 0.9750$, $\Phi(2.00) = 0.9772$, $\Phi(2.58) = 0.9950$.
- **Empirical Rule (high-yield for 3-mark questions):** 68% within $\mu \pm \sigma$, 95% within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$, 99.7% within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.
- **Symmetry identity (must know):** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.
- **Properties to enumerate:** symmetry, mean = median = mode, unimodality, asymptotic tails, total area = 1, inflection at $\mu \pm \sigma$, normality preserved under linear transformation.
- **Probability interval formula:** $P(a < X < b) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$.
- **Linear combination rule:** If $X_1$ and $X_2$ are independent normals, then $X_1 \pm X_2$ is also normal with means adding/subtracting and **variances adding**.
- **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** The normal distribution emerges naturally as the limit of sums/averages of many independent random variables — this is the reason for its universal prevalence.
- **Always verify your answer** using the empirical rule as a sanity check.
- **Always include units** in your final answer.
- **Inverse problems** ("find $L$ such that $P(X < L) = p$") require reading the table **backward** to find the $z$-value corresponding to cumulative probability $p$.
